sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text:
Anesthesiology is a discipline within The Ferguson Clinic’s practice of medicine specializing in:
The preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative evaluation and treatment of patients who are rendered unconscious and/or insensible to pain and emotional stress during surgical procedure.
Protects life functions and vital organs (e.g., brain, heart, lungs, kidneys, liver, endocrine, skin integrity, nerve (sensory and muscular) under the stress of anesthetic, surgical and other medical procedures.

sections.heading: Anesthesia Provider responsibilities include
sections.text:
Assessment of, consultation for and preparation of patients for anesthesia.
Medical management of patients and the anesthetic for the planned procedure.
Post-anesthetic evaluation and treatment
On-site medical direction of any non-physician who participates in the delivery of anesthesia care to the patient.
Perioperative pain management.
Responsibilities of Supervising Physician of Non-Physician Anesthetists:
The supervising physician is responsible for all aspects of the continuum of care: pre-, intra-, and post-procedure. While a patient is sedated, the responsible physician must be physically present and immediately available in the procedure suite.
Although the supervising physician is primarily responsible for pre-procedure patient evaluation, sedation practitioners must be trained adequately in pre-procedure patient evaluation to recognize when risk may be increased, and related policies and procedures must allow sedation practitioners to refuse to participate in specific cases if they perceive a threat to quality of care or patient safety.
The supervising physician is responsible for leading any acute resuscitation needs, including emergency airway management. Therefore, ACLS (PALS where appropriate) certification must be a standard requirement for sedation practitioners and for credentialing and privileging the non-anesthesiologist physicians who supervise them. However, because non-anesthesia professionals seldom perform controlled mask ventilation or tracheal intubation often enough to remain proficient, their training should emphasize avoidance of excessive sedation over rescue techniques.

sections.heading: GUIDELINES
sections.text:
Pre-anesthetic evaluation and preparation means that an anesthesia provider:
Review the chart.
Interviews the patient, parents, or guardians of a minor, next of kin if patient is unable to communicate, or reviews the available medical information if no information can be supplied by any of the above to.
Discuss the medical history, including anesthetic experiences and drug therapy.
Perform any examinations that would provide information that might assist in the decisions regarding anesthetic risk and management.
Orders tests and medications necessary to the conduct of anesthesia.
Obtains consultations, as necessary.
Records assessment and an anesthetic plan in the patient’s medical record.

sections.heading: Peri-anesthetic care means
sections.text:
Re-evaluation of the patient immediately prior to induction.
Preparation and check equipment, drugs, fluids, and gas supplies.
Appropriate monitoring of the patient.
Selection and administration of anesthetic agents to render the patient insensible to pain, while providing a level of comfort and relaxation commensurate with the invasiveness and physiologic stress of the planned procedure.
Support of life functions under the stress of anesthetic and surgical manipulations.
Recording the pertinent events of the procedure.

sections.heading: Post-anesthetic care means
sections.text:
Availability of nursing personnel and equipment as required for safe post-anesthetic care.
The anesthesia care provider gives the healthcare receiver transfer of care information pertinent to the patient’s specific needs and ensure a safe transition.
The anesthesia care team remains with the patient if medically necessary and until the receiving healthcare provider has all the information needed to assume care.
Assurance that the patient is discharged from the post-anesthesia care in accordance with policies established by The Ferguson Clinic’s Medical Director.
The duration of surveillance in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) is determined by the status of the patient and the judgment of the anesthesia providers.
The anesthesia provider shall participate in the Quality Improvement Committee (QIC) for evaluation of quality and appropriateness of the anesthetic care of patients and should participate in resolving identified problems.
The anesthesia provider will be familiar with and adhere to the Guidelines for the Ethical Practice of Anesthesiology (Approved by the ASA House of Delegates on October 15, 2003 and last amended on October 17, 2018).

sections.heading: Anesthesia Provider Primary Function
sections.text:
Perform accepted procedures commonly used to render patient insensible to pain during the performance of surgical procedures.
Support life functions during the period in which anesthesia is administered.

sections.heading: Anesthesia Provider Specific Duties
sections.text:
Preoperative evaluation and reasonable explanation to the patient of the proposed anesthetic procedure
Pre-medication of the patient if needed.
Administration of anesthesia to the patient based on the ASA standards and guidelines.
Performance of a documented post-operative visit or visits as indicated.

sections.heading: Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetists Criteria
sections.text:
Individual’s education, training, and demonstrated skills shall be approved by the Medical Director.
Such criteria should include competence to follow the anesthesia plan prescribed by the anesthesia providers and the technical ability to:
Induce anesthesia under the direction of an anesthesia providers.
Maintain anesthesia at prescribed levels.
Monitor and support life functions during the peri-operative period.
Recognize and report to the anesthesia provider any abnormal patient responses during anesthesia
Adherence to the ASA Guidelines

sections.heading: Other personnel involved in anesthetic care
sections.text:
Post-anesthesia Nurse – a registered nurse who cares for the patient recovering from anesthesia
Peri-operative Nurse – a registered nurse who cares for the patient in the procedure room

sections.heading: ASA Physical Status Definition
sections.text:
P-1:	A normal healthy patient
P-2:	A patient with a mild systemic disease
P-3:	A patient with severe systemic disease
P-4:	A patient with severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life
P5 and P6 do not apply.

sections.heading: Equipment and Facility
sections.text:
Anesthetic Apparatus
Must be inspected and tested before each use. If a leak or other defect is observed, the equipment must not be used until the fault is repaired.
Only Non-Flammable Agents
Shall be used for the pre-operative preparation of the surgical field, when electrical equipment employing an open spark is to be used during a procedure. For example, cautery or coagulation equipment.
Non-Flammable Anesthetic Agents
Will be used in all anesthetizing locations.
Condition of the Procedure Room
Electrical equipment shall be inspected at prescribed intervals and a written report of the results and any required corrective action shall be maintained.
Annual Conductivity Testing
The results of any required conductivity testing shall be made known to the Medical Director and Office Manager.
Anesthesia Personnel
Shall familiarize themselves with the rate, volume, and mechanism of air exchange within the procedure room, as well as with humidity control.
All Waste Gas
From the anesthesia machine is disposed by connection to the vacuum system in each procedure room.
Laser Surgery
All personnel must wear protective goggles.
All patients will wear protective eye coverings.
Laser-safe endotracheal tubes will be used when performing head and neck laser procedures for patients receiving general anesthetic.
Emergency Equipment
There shall be readily available to each anesthetizing location, an emergency cart with a defibrillator, emergency drugs and other resuscitation equipment equivalent to that used in an operating room.

sections.heading: INTRAOPERATIVE AWARENESS
sections.text:
Pre-operative Evaluation
Review patient medical records for potential risk factors
Substance use or abuse
Previous episode of intraoperative awareness
History of difficult intubation or anticipated difficult intubation
Chronic pain patients using high doses of opioids
ASA physical status IV or V
Limited hemodynamic reserve
Interview Patient
Assess level of anxiety
Obtain information regarding previous experiences with anesthesia
Determine other potential risk factors
Reduced anesthetic doses in the presence of paralysis
Planned use of muscle relaxants during the maintenance phase of general anesthesia
Planned use of nitrous oxide-opioid anesthesia

sections.heading: PRE-INDUCTION PHASE OF ANESTHESIA
sections.text:
Adhere to a checklist protocol for anesthesia machines and equipment to assure that the desired anesthetic drugs and doses will be delivered.
Verify the proper functioning of intravenous access, infusion pumps, and their connections, including the presence of appropriate backflow check valves
The decision to administer a benzodiazepine prophylactically should be made on a case-by-case basis for selected patients (e.g., patients requiring smaller dosages of anesthetics)

sections.heading: INTRA-OPERATIVE MONITORING
sections.text:
Use multiple modalities to monitor depth of anesthesia – Clinical techniques (i.e., checking for purposeful or reflex movement)
Neuromuscular blocking drugs may mask purposeful or reflex movement
Conventional monitoring systems (e.g., electrocardiogram, blood pressure, heart rate, end-tidal anesthetic analyzer, capnography)
Brain function monitoring
Not routinely indicated for general anesthesia patients
The decision to use brain function monitor should be made on a case-by-case basis by the individual practitioner for selected patients (e.g., light sedation)

sections.heading: INTRA-OPERATIVE AND POST-OPERATIVE MANAGEMENT
sections.text:
The decision to administer pre-medication intraoperatively after a patient unexpectedly becomes conscious should be made on a case-by-case basis
Speak with patients who report recall of intraoperative events to obtain details of the event and to discuss possible reasons for its occurrence
A questionnaire or structured interview may be used to obtain a detailed account of the patient’s experience
Once an episode of intraoperative awareness has been reported, an incident report regarding the event should be completed for quality management
Offer counseling or psychological support to those patients who report an episode of intraoperative awareness

sections.heading: Informed Consent
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that the patient must be given the opportunity to give an “informed consent” prior to the administration of anesthesia by any anesthesia providers and prior to the performance of operative and/or invasive procedures. The signed informed consent will be placed in the patient’s medical record.
Do-Not-Resuscitate Orders – Advanced Directives
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to suspend Advanced Directives while patients are being cared for at The Ferguson Clinic.  It will be the physician’s responsibility to inform the patient that in the case of an adverse event the patient will be stabilized and transported to Queen’s Medical Center.

sections.heading: STANDARDS FOR PATIENT CARE
sections.text: These standards apply to all patients who receive anesthesia or monitored anesthesia care. Under unusual circumstances these standards may be modified. When this is the care, the circumstances shall be documented in the patient’s medical record.

sections.heading: Pre-Operative Tests
sections.text:
Pre-anesthestic laboratory and diagnostic testing is often essential; however, no routine laboratory or diagnostic screening test is necessary for the pre-anesthetic evaluation of patients. Appropriate indications for ordering tests include identification of specific clinical indicators or risk factors (e.g., age, pre-existing disease, and magnitude of the surgical procedure). The Anesthesia provider will order test(s) when, in their judgment, the results may influence decisions regarding risks and management of the anesthesia and surgery. Relevant abnormalities will be noted, and action taken, if appropriate.
Basic Standards for Pre-Anesthesia Care
An Anesthesia Provider will be responsible for determining the medical status of the patient, developing a plan of anesthesia care, and acquainting the patient or the responsible adult with the proposed plan. The development of an appropriate plan of anesthesia care is based upon:
Reviewing the medical record
Interviewing and examining the patient to:
Discuss the medical history, previous anesthetic experience, and drug therapy.
Assess those aspects of the physical condition and concurrent health problems that might affect decisions regarding peri-operative risk and management.
Obtaining and/or reviewing tests and consultations necessary to the conduct of anesthesia.
Determining the appropriate prescription of pre-operative medications as necessary to the conduct of anesthesia.
The responsible Anesthesia Provider shall verify that the above has been properly performed and documented in the patient’s medical record.

sections.heading: STANDARD OF BASIC ANESTHETIC MONITORING
sections.text:
These standards apply to all anesthesia care. These standards may be exceeded at any time based on the judgment of the responsible anesthesia provider. They are intended to encourage quality patient care but observing them cannot guarantee any specific patient outcome. They are subject to revision from time to time, as warranted by the evolution of technology and practice. They apply to all general anesthetics, regional anesthetics and monitored anesthesia care. This set of standards addresses only the issue of basic anesthesia monitoring, which is one component of anesthesia care. In certain rare or unusual circumstances, 1) some of these methods of monitoring may be clinically impractical, and 2) appropriate use of the described monitoring methods may fail to detect untoward clinical developments, Brief interruptions of continual monitoring may be unavoidable. Under extenuating circumstances, the responsible anesthesia provider may waive the requirements marked with an asterisk (*); it is recommended that when this is done, it should be so stated (including reasons) in a note in the patient’s medical record.
Note that “continual” is defined as “repeated regularly and frequently in steady rapid succession whereas “continuous” means “prolonged without any interruption at any time.”

sections.heading: Standard I
sections.text: Qualified anesthesia personnel shall be present in the room throughout the conduct of all general anesthetics, regional anesthetics, and monitored anesthesia care.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Because of the rapid changes in patient status during anesthesia, qualified anesthesia personnel shall be continuously present to monitor the patient and provide anesthesia care. If an emergency requires the temporary absence of the person primarily responsible for the anesthetic, the best judgment of the anesthesia provider will be exercised in comparing the emergency with the anesthetized patient’s condition and in the selection of the person responsible for the anesthetic during the temporary absence.

sections.heading: Standard II
sections.text: During all anesthetics, the patient’s oxygenation, ventilation, circulation, and temperature shall be continually evaluated.

sections.heading: Oxygenation Objective
sections.text:
To ensure adequate oxygen concentration in the inspired gas and blood during anesthetics.
Inspired Gas: During every administration of general anesthesia using an anesthesia machine, the concentration of oxygen in the patient’s breathing system shall be measured by an oxygen analyzer with a low oxygen concentration limit alarm in use.
Blood Oxygenation: During all anesthetic, a quantitative method of assessing oxygenation such as pulse oximetry shall be employed. When the pulse oximeter is utilized, the variable pitch pulse tone and low threshold alarm shall be audible to the anesthesia providers or the anesthesia care team. * Adequate illumination and exposure of the patient is necessary to assess color.

sections.heading: Ventilation Objective
sections.text:
To ensure adequate ventilation of the patient during all anesthetics.
Every patient receiving general anesthesia shall have the adequacy of ventilation continually evaluated. While qualitative clinical signs such as chest excursion, observation of the reservoir breathing bad and auscultation of breath sounds may be useful, quantitative monitoring of the carbon dioxide content and/or volume of expired gas is mandatory.
When an endotracheal tube or laryngeal mask is inserted, its correct positioning must be verified by clinical assessment and by identification of carbon dioxide in the expired gas.
Continual end-tidal carbon dioxide analysis, in use from the time of endotracheal tube/laryngeal mask placement, until extubation/removal or initiating transfer to a post-operative care location, shall be performed using a quantitative method such as capnography, capnometry or mass spectrography.
When ventilation is controlled by a mechanical ventilator, there shall be a continuous use of a device that is capable of detecting disconnection or components of the breathing system. The device must give an audible signal when its alarm threshold is exceeded.
During regional anesthesia and monitored anesthesia care, the adequacy of ventilation shall be evaluated, at least, by continual observation of qualitative clinical signs and/or monitoring for the presence of exhaled carbon dioxide.

sections.heading: Circulation Objective
sections.text:
To ensure the adequacy of the patient’s circulatory function during all anesthetics.
Every patient receiving anesthesia shall have the electrocardiogram continuously displayed from the beginning of anesthesia until preparing to leave the anesthetizing location.
Every patient receiving anesthesia shall have arterial blood pressure and heart rate determined and evaluated at least every five minutes.
Every patient receiving general anesthesia shall have, in addition to the above, circulatory function continually evaluated by at least one of the following: palpation of the pulse, auscultation of heart sounds, monitoring of tracing of intra-arterial pressure, ultrasound peripheral pulse monitoring, or pulse or oximetry.

sections.heading: Body Temperature Objective
sections.text:
To aid in the maintenance of appropriate body temperature during all anesthetics.
There shall be readily available a means to continuously measure the patient’s temperature. When changes in body temperature are intended, anticipated, or suspected, the temperature shall be measured.

sections.heading: STANDARDS FOR POST-ANESTHESIA CARE
sections.text: These standards apply to post-anesthesia care.

sections.heading: Standard I
sections.text:
All patients who received general anesthesia, regional anesthesia, or monitored anesthesia care shall receive appropriate post-anesthesia management.
A Post-Anesthesia Care Unit (PACU) or an area, which provides equivalent post-anesthesia care, shall be available to receive patients after surgery and anesthesia. All patients who receive anesthesia shall be admitted to the PACU except by specific order of the anesthesia provider responsible for the patient’s care.
The medical aspects of care in the PACU shall be governed by policies and procedures, which have been reviewed and approved by the Medical Director and Anesthesia provider.
The design, equipment and staffing of the PACU shall meet requirements of the facility’s accrediting and licensing bodies.

sections.heading: Standard II
sections.text: A patient transported to the PACU shall be accompanied by a member of the Anesthesia Care Team who is knowledgeable about the patient’s condition. The patient shall be continually evaluated and treated during the transport with monitoring and support appropriate to the patient’s condition.

sections.heading: Standard III
sections.text:
Upon arrival to the PACU, the patient shall be re-evaluated, and a verbal report provided to the responsible PACU nurse by the member of the anesthesia care team who accompanied the patient.
The patient’s status on arrival in the PACU will be documented by the PACU nurse.
Information concerning the pre-operative condition and the surgical/anesthetic course shall be communicated to the PACU nurse. The handoff communication will be documented in the PACU nursing flow sheet.
A member of the anesthesia care team shall remain in the PACU until the PACU nurse accepts responsibility for the nursing care of the patient.

sections.heading: Standard IV
sections.text:
The patient’s condition shall be evaluated continually in the PACU
The patient shall be observed and monitored by methods appropriate to the patient’s medical condition. Attention will be given to monitoring oxygenation, ventilation, circulation, and temperature. During recovery from all anesthetics, a quantitative method of assessing oxygenation such as pulse oximetry shall be employed in the initial phase of recovery.
An accurate written report of the PACU period shall be maintained. Use of the Modified Aldrete Scoring System shall be used for each patient on admission, at appropriate intervals prior to discharge, and at the time of discharge.
General medical supervision and coordination of patient care in the PACU shall be the responsibility of an anesthesia provider.
There shall be a policy to assure the availability in the facility of a physician capable of managing complications and providing cardiopulmonary resuscitation for patient in the PACU.

sections.heading: Standard V
sections.text:
A physician is responsible for the discharge of the patient from the PACU
Discharge criteria shall be used to assess readiness for discharge as approved by the Medical Director and Anesthesia provider.
All patients shall be signed out, from the PACU, by a physician in cooperation with the anesthesia provider.

sections.heading: DOCUMENTATION OF ANESTHESIA CARE
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to set legibility standards for the medical record documentation and medical error reduction activities. Documentation is a factor in the provision of quality care and is the responsibility of an anesthesia provider. While anesthesia care is a continuum, it is usually viewed as consisting of pre-anesthesia, peri-anesthesia, and post-anesthesia components. Anesthesia care shall be documented to reflect these components and to facilitate review. The records should include documentation of the following:
Pre-Anesthesia Evaluation

sections.heading: Patient interview to review
sections.text:
Medical history
Anesthesia history
Medication history
Appropriate physical examination
Review of objective diagnostic data (e.g., laboratory, ECG, x-ray)
Assignment of ASA physical status
Formulation and discussion of an anesthesia plan with the patient or the patient’s legal representative
Informed Consents(s)
Intra-Operative/Procedural Record (Time based record of events)

sections.heading: Immediate review prior to initiation of anesthetic procedures
sections.text:
Patient re-evaluation
Check equipment, drugs, gas supply
Monitoring of the patient (e.g., recording vital signs)
Amounts of all drugs and agents used, and times of administration
Type and amounts of all intravenous fluids use, and times given.
Anesthetic technique(s) used
Unusual events during the administration of anesthesia
Status of the patient at the conclusion of anesthesia
Should anesthesia providers change for any reason during the case, documentation of an appropriate handoff communication shall occur between the anesthesia providers
Post-Anesthesia
Documentation of an appropriate handoff communication shall occur between the anesthesia provider and the PACU RN.
Patient evaluation on admission and discharge from the post-anesthesia care team
Time-based record of vital signs and level of consciousness
Time-based record of drugs administered their dosage and route of administration
Type and amounts of intravenous fluids administered
Any unusual events including post-anesthesia or post procedural complications
Post anesthesia visits
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: Anesthesiology Guidelines based on ASA

The ASA classifications are as follows:
ASA I				No organic, physiologic, biochemical, or psychiatric disturbance.
Normal health patient.
ASA II	Mild-moderate systemic disturbance; may or may not be related to reason for surgery (i.e., hypertension, diabetes mellitus)
ASA III	Sever system disturbance (i.e., heart disease, poorly controlled hypertension)
ASA IV	Life threatening system disturbance (i.e., congestive heart failure, persistent angina pectoris)
ASA V	Moribund patient, little chance for survival, surgery is last resort (i.e., uncontrolled bleeding, ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm)
ASA VI	Declared brain dead patient whose organs are being removed for donor purposes
E	Patient requires emergency procedure (i.e., appendectomy, D&C for uncontrolled bleeding
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: ASA Classification

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of this policy is to detail the type of behavior which is unacceptable and the steps available in the face of such behavior, including mechanisms whereby the patients/visitors that are extreme or persistent in their unacceptable behavior can be terminated from the center.

sections.heading: CONTEXT
sections.text: There has been a dramatic national increase in recent years in the level of violence and abuse faced by healthcare facilities from visitors and patients. Incidents include significant injury, damage to equipment and extreme verbal abuse and threats.

sections.heading: Abusive/Violent Behavior
sections.text: Conduct or comments that are inappropriate, demeaning, or otherwise offensive behavior to create an uncomfortable, hostile and/or intimidating environment.

sections.heading: EXPECTED STANDARDS OF BEHAVIOR
sections.text:
The following are examples of unacceptable behavior towards The Ferguson Clinic’s personnel:
Violence or any act of aggression.
Excessive noise, e.g., recurrent loud or intrusive conversation or shouting.
Threatening or abusive language involving excessive swearing or offensive remarks.
Derogatory racial or sexual remarks.
Malicious allegation relating to personnel, other patients, or visitors.
Offensive sexual gestures of behaviors.
Abusing alcohol or drugs on The Ferguson Clinic’s premises.
Willful damage to The Ferguson Clinic’s property.
Theft.
Threats or threatening behavior, harassment, alarm, or distress.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Any patient or visitors displaying any unacceptable behavior will be asked to leave The Ferguson Clinic’s property. If the patient or visitor refuses to leave, authorities will be contacted immediately.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
If the patient or visitor display unacceptable behavior while in the center, personnel will contact the Office Manager.
The Office Manager will escort the patient/visitor to a quiet area to discuss the problem if the patient/visitor is behaving in a non-threatening manner.
If the patient/visitor does not become calm, the Office Manager will ask the patient to leave the center immediately.
If the patient/visitor refuses to leave, the Office Manager will call 9-1-1.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Abusive Patient/Visitor

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to respond to any emergency or cardiac arrest in such a manner as to save the life of the patient.

sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text: An Advanced Directive is a declaration that is written and signed in advance of a serious/terminal illness or incapacitated state in which an individual, competent at the time of execution, outlines his or her choice for health care.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Advanced Directives are suspended for the duration of care at The Ferguson Clinic.
During the consent process the patient will be notified that The Ferguson Clinic will resuscitate the patient in the case of an emergency and will be transported to the local/nearest hospital.
If a patient has a cardiac arrest, 911 will be called and the patient will be stabilized and then transported to the local/nearest hospital.
If a patient presents an Advanced Directive to the facility, a copy should be made and placed in the patient’s electronic medical record.  The original should be given back to the patient, and the patient should be informed of the policy to not honor their Advanced Directive.
If a patient requests information for assistance in formulating an Advanced Directive, the patient will be referred to his or her attorney or to an acute care hospital where resources are available to assist the patient.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Advanced Directives

Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Adverse Events
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Adverse Events

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
A formal Root Cause Assessment (RCA) will be conducted under the auspices of the Coordinated Quality Improvement Committee for events that meet the definition of sentinel event, adverse event, or near miss as stated above, including:
Events that resulted in an unanticipated death or major permanent loss of function, or.
Events that are one of the following (even if the outcome was not death or major permanent loss of function):
Surgery on the wrong individual or wrong body part
Unintended retention of a foreign object in an individual after surgery or other procedure

sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text:
Sentinel Event – an unexpected occurrence involving death or serious physical or psychological injury, or the risk thereof. Serious injury specifically includes loss of limb or function. The phrase “or the risk thereof” includes any process variation for which a recurrence would carry a significant chance of a serious adverse outcome.
Adverse Event –serious incidents, therapeutic misadventures, iatrogenic injuries, or other adverse occurrences directly associated with care or services provided. Adverse events may result from acts of commission or omission (e.g., administration of the wrong medication, adverse reactions, or negative outcomes of treatment, etc.). Some examples of adverse events include patient falls, medication errors and/or procedural errors/complication. An adverse event can also be categorized as either a sentinel event or near miss.

sections.heading: Adverse Event includes
sections.text:
An unexpected occurrence during a heath care encounter involving patient death or serious physical or phycological injurer illness, including loss of limb or function, not related to the natural course of the patient’s illness or underlying condition.
Any process variation for which a recurrence carries a significant chance of a serious adverse outcome.
Events such as actual breaches in medical care, administrative procedures, or other events resulting in an outcome that is not associated with the standard of care or acceptable risks associated with the provision of care and service for a patient.
All events involving reaction to drugs and materials.
Circumstances or events that could have resulted in an adverse event (near- miss events)
Serious incidents, therapeutic misadventures, iatrogenic injuries, or other adverse occurrences directly associated with care or services provided. Adverse events may result from acts of commission or omission (e.g., administration of the wrong medication, adverse reactions, or negative outcomes of treatment, etc.). Some examples of adverse events include patient falls, medication errors and/or procedural errors/complication. An adverse event can also be categorized as either a sentinel event or near miss.
A distinction is made between an adverse outcome that is primarily related to the natural course of the patient’s illness or underlying condition and a death or major permanent loss of function that is associated with the treatment (including “recognized complications”) or lack of treatment of that condition, or otherwise not clearly and primarily related to the natural course of the patient’s illness or underlying condition. In determinate cases, the event will be presumed reviewable, and the organization’s response will be reviewed under the Policy according to the prescribed procedures and timeframes without delay for additional information such as autopsy results.
Near Miss – (also called a close call) is an event or situation that could have resulted in an accident, injury, or illness, but did not, either by chance or through timely intervention. An example of a near miss would be surgical or other procedure almost performed on the wrong site due to lapses in verification or time out but caught at the last minute by chance. Near misses are opportunities for learning and afford the chance to develop preventive strategies and actions. Near misses will receive the same level of scrutiny as adverse events that result in actual injury.
Root Cause Assessment (RCA) – a process for identifying the basic or contributing causal factors that underlie variations in performance associated with adverse events or near misses. The Root Cause Assessment will be the focused review that is used for all adverse events or near misses requiring analysis since it further refines the implementation and increases the quality and consistency of focused reviews.
Major permanent loss of function – sensory, motor, physiologic, or intellectual impairment does not present on admission requiring continued treatment for life-style change. When major permanent loss of function cannot be immediately determined applicability of the policy is not established until either the patient is discharged with continued major loss of function, or two weeks have elapsed with persistent major loss of function, whichever occurs first.
Rape – as a reviewable sentinel event, is defined as un-consented sexual contact involving a patient and another patient, staff member, or unknown perpetrator while being treated or on the premises of the health care organization, including oral, vaginal, or anal penetration or fondling of the patient’s sex organ(s) by another individual’s hand, sex organ, or object. One or more of the following must be present to determine reviewability.
Any staff witnessed sexual contact as described above
Sufficient clinical evidence obtained by the organization to support allegations of un-consented sexual contact
Admission by the perpetrator that sexual contact, as described above, occurred on the premises.
Any Event – any reaction to material, substance, or drug

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The Quality Improvement Committee will initiate an investigation of a reported sentinel event/near miss immediately after notification and will work with the physician and medical team to determine if a root cause assessment (RCA) should be conducted. An RCA may be conducted for occurrences that do not meet the criteria for a sentinel event if the medical director determines that it is appropriate.
All RCAs will involve The Ferguson Clinic staff whose departments are associated with the event. The Quality Improvement Committee will work with the applicable departments to determine participants in the RCA meeting. When appropriate, staff directly involved in the actual event will participate.
The Quality Improvement Committee will develop a flow of events based on interviews with staff directly involved in the event, which will be presented to the RCA team. The RCA team will review, correct, and discuss the flow to determine the root cause(s). The team will then develop an action plan to address the root cause(s) identified, which will include responsibility for implementation of action items and timelines. This action plan will be completed within 45 days of notification of the event. The Quality Improvement Committee will be responsible for monitoring the action plans for completion of action items and will report this information to the Governing Body and the accreditation body for whom the organization has obtained accreditation from.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Adverse Events

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of the The Ferguson Clinic to inform patients about how to contact medical personnel in an emergency.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
All patients will be told who to contact in the event of an emergency.
There will be a physician on call that will return the patient’s call immediately.
All patients will be instructed to call 911 in case of a life-threatening emergency on the after-hour’s phone message.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: After Hour Phone Message/Emergency Care

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure that the anesthesia provider complete an anesthesia care plan prior to sedating the patient.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Once the pre-anesthesia assessment is completed and signed off by the physician, the anesthesia provider will complete an anesthesia care plan.
The anesthesia care plan will be reviewed by the anesthesia provider with the physician and the anesthesia care plan will be placed in the patient’s medical record.
Subject: Anesthesia Services

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to review the Anesthesia Informed Consent Form with the patient at the pre-op visit or prior to the patient being medicated on the day of the procedure.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
At the patient’s pre-op appointment, the physician or LPN will review the Anesthesia Consent Form with the patient to ensure they understand the risks and complications and are given the opportunity to ask questions.
If the patient does not receive a pre-op appointment the anesthesia provider will review the Anesthesia Informed Consent Form the day of the procedure prior to the patient receiving any medication. The anesthesia provider will ensure the patient understands the risks and complications and gives the patient the opportunity to ask questions.
Once the Anesthesia Care Plan is reviewed the patient will sign the Anesthesia Informed Consent Form and either the physician or the anesthesia provider will witness the patient signature, authenticating that the signature is the actual patient.
The RN while reviewing the medical record will ensure the Anesthesia Informed Consent Form is completed prior to the patient receiving any medication or anesthesia or being admitted to the procedure room.
The completed Anesthesia Informed Consent Form will be placed in the patient medical record.
Subject: Anesthesia Services

Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Assessment and Management of Pain
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Assessment and Management of Pain

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure that each patient is assessed for acute post-operative pain and implement treatment when applicable.

sections.heading: HISTORY
sections.text:
In recent decades, technological advances have refined the clinical assessment and management of adult patients in the acute care setting. However, physicians and/or nurses must rely heavily on knowledge, interviewing techniques, and physical assessment skills to competently assess and manage patients with acute pain, because these skills have not been replaced by technology. Pain is a common reason for patients to seek healthcare and be admitted to hospitals. According to the National Center for Health Statistics, 46 million Americans undergo inpatient surgical procedures each year and experience acute surgical pain. In 2006, pain was a frequent “chief complaint” for adults who presented to emergency departments (EDs), and pain severity was reported as moderate to severe by 45% of patients in the ED.
Such organizations as the American Society for Pain Management Nursing (ASPMN), the American Pain Society (APS), the American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA), and the American Society of PeriAnesthesia (ASPAN) have attempted to improve the quality of pain management in the United States through formulation and publication of pain-related position statements and clinical practice guidelines.

sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text:
“Pain” is defined by the International Association for the Study of Pain as “an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience arising from actual or potential tissue damage or described in terms of such damage”
Although this is a technical description of pain, it recognizes both the physiologic and affective nature of the pain experience. Pain is a highly personal, subjective experience which can only be accurately described by the individual who is experiencing pain.
Acute pain is “the normal, predicted physiologic response to an adverse chemical, thermal, or mechanical stimulus ... associated with surgery, trauma, or acute illness.” Acute pain results from activation of the pain receptors (nociceptors) at the site of tissue damage. This type of pain generally accompanies surgery, traumatic injury, tissue damage, and inflammatory processes.

sections.heading: ASSESSMENT
sections.text:
Accurate assessment of acute pain is essential for the development of an effective pain management plan. The Attending Physician and the Nurse play a pivotal role in the assessment of pain, owing to the nature of their relationship with patients. Pain assessment can be challenging because of the subjectivity and multidimensionality of the pain experience.
The patient’s self-report of pain includes the sensory, emotional, psychological, and cultural components of the pain experience, which cannot be captured on the unidimensional tools typically used in practice. A comprehensive pain assessment includes pain location and quality, aggravating and alleviating factors, timing and duration, pain relief and functional goals, and intensity.
The effectiveness of any previous pain treatment, as well as the effects of pain on quality of life, should also be determined.
The comprehensive pain assessment should be performed when patients suffers from pain post-operatively and at the onset of new acute pain. To determine treatment effectiveness and guide further interventions, subsequent pain assessments should focus on the nature of the pain, pain intensity, and responses to treatment. Pain assessment tools should be valid and reliable for the patient population in which they are used.
Unidimensional pain intensity scales. Unidimensional scales are quick and easy to use, provide rapid feedback about the effectiveness of interventions, and are valid and reliable measures of pain intensity. Because the unidimensional scales measure only intensity, they cannot substitute for a comprehensive pain assessment. The unidimensional pain intensity scales used most often in the clinical setting are:
Numeric Rating Scale (NRS), also known as the Numerical Pain Intensity Scale (NPI).
Visual Analog Scale (VAS); and
Verbal Descriptor Scale (VDS).
Numeric Rating Scale. The NRS can be used graphically (visually) or verbally. When used graphically, the NRS consists of a vertical or horizontal line that is anchored by the number 0 on the bottom or the left side and the number 10 on the top or the right side. Patients are instructed to rate the intensity of their pain on this scale, with “0” indicating no pain, and “10” indicating the worst pain imaginable.
Visual Analog Scale. The VAS is a 10-cm (100-mm) line on which the patient is asked to place a mark that corresponds with his or her current pain intensity. The line is then measured from the beginning to the patient’s mark, and this distance is translated into a pain intensity score ranging from 0 to 10. The format of scale, coupled with the need for a marking implement and for the patient to be able to clearly visualize and mark the line, make the VAS impractical to use in the clinical setting.
Verbal Descriptor Scale. The VDS uses the verbal descriptors “no pain,” “mild pain,” “moderate pain,” “severe pain,” “very severe pain,” and “worst pain possible.” This scale can be administered verbally or visually, and the patient is instructed to pick the words that best describe his or her current pain intensity

sections.heading: TREATMENT
sections.text:
Pain medications can be divided into 3 categories nonopioid analgesics, opioid analgesics, and coanalgesics (or adjuvant analgesics).
To prevent post-operative pain whenever possible, give an analgesic before pain develops. For example, administration of pain medication 30-60 minutes before the procedure starts. This will help to minimize procedure-associated pain.
Pain that increases above the patient’s-controlled baseline level of pain is referred to as “breakthrough pain.” Incidental pain can occur as the patient’s only pain, or it can occur as a breakthrough pain.
Patients receiving OTC analgesics for continuous pain and patients with pain that occurs incidentally are provided with short-acting, “as-needed” (PRN) analgesics. Because some patients do not request PRN medication, the Physician/ARNP or designee must act as a patient advocate and offer the patient these interventions for pain.
Reassessment of the patient’s response to treatment is paramount. After a patient has been given a medication and/or a nonpharmacologic measure has been used, the Attending Ph and the Nurse must check the patient in a timely manner (depending on expected time of action of intervention) to assess for the efficacy of the treatment and for any adverse effects that may have occurred.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.text:
If left untreated, acute pain can result in numerous negative physiologic and psychological sequelae to patients. As patient advocates, Nurses/Physicians or designees must learn how to properly assess pain and how to optimize safe pain management for all patients in their care.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Assessment and Management of Pain

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure that all staff entering semi-restricted and restricted areas of the surgical suite is in operating room attire. Procedure Room attire consists of standard reusable woven fabric or single-use nonwoven scrubs and a surgical hat or hood. All attire should be low-linting. Procedure Room attire which is soiled or wet shall be changed before entering those areas.

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: Surgical attire provides a barrier between staff and patient and staff, through which contamination may pass. Surgical attire also provides protection for staff against exposure to infectious microorganisms and hazardous materials.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
It is recommended by AORN that no rings are worn in the procedure room. All jewelry rings and watches are to be removed prior to handwashing/scrubbing; all other jewelry shall be totally confined within scrub attire or removed. Fingernails should be kept short less than one-quarter (1/4) inch in length and well-maintained. No artificial fingernails or extenders are permitted.
If fingernail polish is used, it should not be chipped and should be removed every 4 days and reapplied.
All surgical scrubs and gowns are always to be worn in the semi-restricted and restricted areas. Gloves, hats/hoods, booties, and masks must be worn and used only in the Procedure Room.
If leaving the Procedure Room during a surgical procedure, the staff must remove their booties, gloves, hats/hoods, masks, and gowns prior to leaving or replace with clean prior to re-entering the Procedure Room.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Attire in the Operating Room

Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Audits and Evaluations

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text: Audits

sections.heading: Audits may be conducted for any one or more of the following purposes
sections.text:
Ensure integrity, confidentiality and availability of information and resources.
Investigate possible security incidents.
Ensure compliance with these policies and procedures that are related to information system security.
Ensure compliance with any law or regulation applicable to The Ferguson Clinic operations, and
Monitor user or system activity where appropriate.
As required by the HIPAA Security Rule, information system activity reviews will be part of the audit process.
Internal review of a random sample of records of information system activity will be conducted on a periodic basis as determined by the Privacy Officer. A random sample of access and activity logs and security incident logs will be reviewed. A more extensive internal audit may be conducted if the results of this periodic review indicate that it is necessary.
As required by the HIPAA Security Rule, the audit process will include periodic evaluations to establish the extent to which these policies and procedures comply with the requirements of the HIPAA Security Rule.
Additional evaluations shall be conducted whenever there are environmental or operational changes affecting the security of ePHI created, received, maintained, or transmitted by The Ferguson Clinic.
During an audit, any appropriate access needed for the performance of the audit will be provided to the Privacy Officer, his or her designee, or an outside auditor. This access may include:
User level and/or system level access to any computing or communications device present on The Ferguson Clinic’s premises, but which may not be owned or operated by The Ferguson Clinic.
Access to information (electronic, hardcopy, etc.) that may be produced, transmitted, or stored on The Ferguson Clinic’s equipment or premises.
Access to work areas (labs, offices, cubicles, storage areas, etc.); and
Access to interactively monitor and log traffic on The Ferguson Clinic’s networks.

sections.heading: Documentation
sections.text:
Records of information system activity shall be maintained as needed and appropriate to facilitate audits, risk assessments and analyses, evaluations, and investigations of suspected security incidents or violations.
The audit program and schedule, all internal and external audit reports and documentation of any additional evaluations shall be maintained for at least six years from the date they were created or last updated.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Audits and Evaluations

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Autoclave Cleaning

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to follow the instructions for cleaning the autoclave to ensure maximum efficiency and sterilizations.

sections.heading: Day of Surgery
sections.text:
Clean door gasket with a mild detergent, water and a cloth or sponge.
Dusting of the outside, basic cleaning/wiping out of the inside using water and removal of any debris from drains, etc. that would prevent proper operation.

sections.heading: Weekly
sections.text:
ONCE PER WEEK clean the Air Trap Jet by moving the clean out wire in and out ten times. (The Air Jet is in the water reservoir. It is best cleaned while the unit is running (20-30 psi), although this is not mandatory).
For Electronic models clean the water sensor in the rear of the chamber with a damp cloth or sponge. (Cleaning the dirt off the sides of the sensor is more important than the tip)
Once per week clean the chamber with Chamber Brite. Sprinkle the Chamber Brite powder along the bottom of a cold autoclave chamber (if the unit is HOT fill with water first).  Run a normal sterilization cycle without drying.
Drain and flush the solution from reservoir and discard. Wipe the inside of chamber with water and a damp cloth or sponge. Fill the reservoir with distilled water. (Check the Chamber Brite package for further details).
For “M” & “MK” models - Turn knob to fill to flush the fill tube at the back of the chamber.
For “Automatic” models -Operate manual water fill button to flush the fill tube.
Clean tray holder and trays with detergent, or a non-abrasive stainless-steel cleaner and water, using a cloth or sponge. DO NOT use steel wool, a steel brush or bleach.
Put a few drops of oil on the door hinge pin and door tightening bolt.
Clean the drains using a manufacturer-approved de-scaler or cleaner

sections.heading: Monthly
sections.text:
Clean inside/drains of autoclave using a de-scaler approved by the manufacturer and following all precautions prescribed by such manufacturer to include the SDS sheets of the approved solutions for your autoclave. Keep in mind that the schedule is a recommendation made by most autoclave manufacturers. You must always be sure to follow your autoclave manufacturers recommendations for the routine cleaning of your autoclaves.
Most autoclave manufacturers have a pre-cleaner, de-scaler solutions, and brushes that can be used on their systems.

sections.heading: Every Two Months
sections.text:
Clean and check the safety valve.
Safety Valve Cleaning Procedure
To prevent the safety valve from becoming blocked, it is necessary every 2 months, under ordinary usage, to allow the steam pressure to escape through the valve.
Begin a normal sterilization cycle according to manual instructions.
Allow a pressure of approximately 30 psi to build up in the chamber.
Turn the unit off.
Remove water reservoir cover. CAUTION This next step will expose you to HOT STEAM CAUTION To avoid being burned by hot steam, do not place your face over the safety valve.
Pull the ring of the safety valve using a screwdriver, hook, or other tool and open the safety valve for only 2 seconds, then release.
For manual units turn the multivalve into the exhaust position and allow all the pressure to vent before opening the door.
For electronic units turn the power on and press the Stop Button to abort and vent the cycle.

sections.heading: DOCUMENTATION
sections.text:
All autoclave cleaning will be documented in the Autoclave Log and reviewed by the Infection Control and Prevention Officer monthly.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Autoclave Cleaning

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Basic Aseptic Technique
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Basic Aseptic Technique
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Basic Aseptic Technique

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to implement the basic principles and practices of aseptic in establishing and maintaining a sterile field when the procedure requires it.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To provide and/or maintain a safe environment for the patient.
To adhere to aseptic practices in the fulfillment of this responsibility.
To minimize wound contamination, aseptic practices are implemented pre-operatively, inter-operatively, and post-operatively.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Scrubbed persons wear sterile gowns and gloves.
The Surgical Hand Scrub policy should be followed before donning sterile gown and gloves according to procedures in the Surgical Hand Washing Policy.
Transfer of microorganisms is reduced by scrubbing hands and wearing sterile gloves.
The scrubbed person should don a sterile gown and sterile gloves from a sterile field.
Scrubbed people should inspect gloves for integrity after donning them.
Once the original gloves are donned, the gown cuffs should be considered contaminated.
Materials for gowns should be selected according to guidelines in the selection of Gowns and Drapes Policy.
Self-gowning and gloving of scrubbed persons should be performed from a sterile field.
The gown worn by a scrubbed person should be considered sterile in front from chest to level of a sterile field.
The sleeves should be considered sterile from above the elbow to the stockinette cuff.
The stockinette cuff should be considered unsterile and should be covered by sterile gloves always.
The neckline, shoulders, areas under the arms and back of the gown should be considered unsterile.
The area of sterility in the front of the gown extends to level of a sterile field because most scrubbed team members work adjacent to a sterile table.
Since the arms of a scrubbed person must move across a sterile field, the sleeves of the gown to two inches above the elbow must remain sterile.
The stockinette cuff portion of the sleeve is a moisture collection area and not an effective microbial barrier.
The neckline, shoulders, areas under the arms, and the back may become contaminated by perspiration or by collar and shoulder surfaces rubbing together during head and neck movements.  The back of a wraparound “sterile back” gown is not considered sterile as it cannot be observed by a scrubbed person.
Sterile drapes will be used to establish a sterile field when indicated.
Surgical drapes should be selected according to guidelines in the Selection of Gowns and Drapes Policy.
Sterile drapes should be placed on the patient and all furniture and equipment to be included in the sterile field.
Before draping, ensure that furniture surfaces are clean and dry.
Drapes should be handled as little as possible.
In the draping process, the material should be held in a compact position above waist level (higher than the OR table), draping from the operative site to the periphery.
In placing drapes, the gloved hands should be protected by cuffing draping material over the hands.
Once placed in position, sterile drapes should not be moved or shifted.
Drapes should not be flipped, fanned, or shaken.
Rapid movement of drapes creates air currents on which dust, lint, and droplet nuclei can migrate.
Shifting the position of sterile drapes may allow potential contamination from unsterile surfaces beneath it.
Drapes, supplies, and equipment extending over or dropping below table level must be considered unsterile because they are out of sight, and their sterility cannot be monitored.
Sterile dressing material should be applied before surgical drapes are removed to avoid contamination of the incision.
All items used within a sterile field should be sterile.
Packaging materials should meet the guidelines for the Selection and Use of Packaging Materials Policy.
Methods of sterilization, storage, and handling of sterile items should meet the guidelines for the Sterilization and Disinfection Policy.
Before dispensing a sterile item to the sterile field, a scrubbed person should check the package integrity, chemical process indicator, and expiration date.
Maintenance of package sterility is event-related and not time-related.  It is dependent upon packaging materials, handling, and storage conditions.
All items introduced into a sterile field should be dispensed by methods that maintain sterility of the item and integrity of the sterile field.
A sterile package should be opened from the far side first and near side last.
All wrapper tails should be secured when supplies are presented to the sterile field to avoid contamination.
Sterile items should be presented to the scrubbed person or placed securely on the sterile field.
Sharp and/or heavy objects should be presented to the scrubbed person or opened on a separate surface.

sections.heading: When dispensing solutions to the sterile field
sections.text:
Entire contents of a bottle should be poured, or the remainder discarded.
Solution receptacles should be placed near the edge of the table or held by a scrubbed person.
Solutions should be poured to avoid splashing of fluids onto the sterile field.
To avoid contamination by a non-sterile item passing over the sterile item, the last flap of the sterile covering should be opened toward a non-sterile person. Unscrubbed personnel should only encounter and reach over unsterile areas.
Once opened, wrappers are considered sterile to within one inch of the edge and contaminated wherever touched by un-scrubbed personnel.
The inner edge of a peel package seal is considered the sterile boundary.
Items tossed onto a sterile field may roll off the edge, becoming contaminated, or cause other items to be displaced.
Sharp and heavy objects may penetrate barriers.
The edge of a bottle cap is considered contaminated once the cap has been removed from the bottle.  The sterility of bottle contents cannot be assured if the cap is replaced.
Reuse of opened bottles may contaminate solutions due to drops contacting unsterile areas and the running back over sterile bottle lips.
Some materials appear resistant to moisture, but under abrasive conditions or prolonged exposure will allow “strike-through”. Strike-through, or penetration by fluids, allows contamination.
A sterile field should be constantly monitored and maintained.
Sterile fields should be prepared as close as possible to scheduled time of use and not covered.
Unguarded sterile fields should be considered contaminated.
Every team member should observe events which may compromise the sterile field and initiate corrective action.
All cables, tubing, etc., for equipment should be secured on the sterile field with a non-perforating device.
Non-sterile equipment brought into the sterile field should be draped with sterile material.
Items of doubtful sterility should be considered non-sterile and discarded.
All personnel moving within or around a sterile field should do so in a manner as to maintain the integrity of the sterile field.
Scrubbed persons should keep arms and hands within the parameters of the sterile field always.
Scrubbed surgical team members should move from sterile-to-sterile areas.
If they must change position, they should turn back-to-back or face to face while maintaining a safe distance between each other and avoiding traffic pathways.
Scrubbed persons should stay close to the sterile field and should not leave the room.
Scrubbed persons should avoid changing levels and should be seated only when the entire surgical procedure will be performed at this level.
Un-scrubbed surgical team members should move from unsterile-to-unsterile areas, maintaining awareness of the need for distance from the sterile field.
Un-scrubbed persons should always approach sterile areas by facing them and should not walk between two sterile fields.

sections.heading: General Procedures
sections.text:
Sterile supplies should never be opened and then covered.  It is virtually impossible to uncover the sterile field without contamination.
Chances for contamination of an unguarded sterile field are numerous. Without direct observation, there is no way to ensure sterility.
Application of the principles of aseptic technique depends primarily on the individual and his/her conscience.
A sterile barrier that has been permeated is considered contaminated.
Only sterile items touch sterile surfaces.
Scrubbed persons move only in areas of similar preparation.
Sterile team members do not wander about the room nor leave the room while in sterile attire.
All sterile equipment and supplies are grouped around the patient.  Establishing patterns of movement within or around a sterile field helps to prevent accidental contamination.
Movement and air currents around a sterile area are kept to a minimum to avoid contamination.
By keeping sterile areas in view during movement around the area, accidental contamination can be prevented.
Wounds can become contaminated and later infected if they are touched by contaminated hands or objects after the operation.  Wounds are covered to reduce the risk of contamination.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Basic Aseptic Technique

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
As an accredited and licensed ASC, we are required to advise patients about our policy regarding blood transfusions.
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to not treat any patient that may require blood transfusions during any procedure performed at this facility.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
All the procedures performed at The Ferguson Clinic will never require blood transfusions.
If in the middle of a procedure it is apparent that blood may be needed, 911 will be called and the patient will be transported to The Queen’s Medical Center for possible blood transfusions.

title: Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetists (CRNA) Duties and Responsibilities

sections.heading: Each CRNA working for or contracted with The Ferguson Clinic will be
sections.text:
Licensed as a Registered Nurse in the State of Hawaii.
Certified by the American Association of Nurse Anesthetists.
The CRNAs are independent anesthesia provider per Hawaii state law.
The CRNA may provide post-anesthetic patient care in the PACU or hospital as necessary, document the problems and treatment initiated, but will inform the supervising or directing Anesthesiologist as early and appropriate as determined by the clinical condition of the patient.
The CRNA may establish airways in emergency situations anywhere in the hospital.
The CRNA is responsible for preparing anesthesia related medication and for verifying functioning of all equipment that would ordinarily be used in a proposed anesthetic.
Evaluations and care delivered by the CRNA will be documented in the patient’s record
Subject: Anesthesia Services
