Section: Use of a Centrifuge

sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text: A machine using centrifugal force for separating substances of different densities, for removing moisture, or for simulating gravitational effects.

sections.heading: USE
sections.text: A centrifuge is used to separate particles suspended in a liquid according to particle size and density, viscosity of the medium, and rotor speed. Within a solution, gravitational force will cause particles of higher density than the solvent to sink, and those less dense than the solvent to float to the top.

sections.heading: Hazards
sections.text:
Mechanical Failure of the Machine
Lab Equipment Failure (tubes, etc.)
Aerosol Generation
Operator Error

sections.heading: Operating Procedures
sections.text:
Check tubes for cracks/chips.
Use matched sets of tubes, buckets, etc.
Tightly seal all tubes and safety cups.
Ensure that rotor is locked to spindle, and bucket seated.
Close lid during operation.
Allow to come to complete stop before opening.

sections.heading: Safe Operation
sections.text:
Use safety cups whenever possible.
Disinfect weekly and after all spills or breakages.
Lubricate O-rings and rotor threads weekly.
Do not operate the centrifuge without the rotor properly balanced.
Do not use rotors that have been dropped.
Contact your centrifuge rep for specific information.
If you suspect leakage occurred from the centrifuge, leave the area, do not open the centrifuge for at least 30 minutes to allow aerosols to settle, then access situation while wearing appropriate PPE and taking necessary precautions.
Source: CDC

sections.heading: CENTRIFUGE USE
sections.text:
There are a few important guidelines for operating a centrifuge, even a small one. Following them can prevent damage to the centrifuge and possible serious injury to you and others.
The work surface must be level and firm. Do not use the centrifuge on an uneven or slanted work surface.
Balance the tubes in the rotor. If you want to run a tube with 10 mL of liquid, put another tube with 10 mL of water in the opposing hole on the rotor. If the liquid has a higher or lower density than water, you must balance the tubes by mass, not volume.
Do not open the lid while the rotor is moving. Even though many centrifuges have a “safety shutoff” if the lid is opened, the only thing this does is stop powering the rotor. The rotor will still spin due to its own inertia for a while until friction slows and eventually stops it.
If you see it wobbling or shaking, turn it off or pull the plug. A little vibration is normal, but excessive amounts can mean danger. FIRST, double check that you correctly balanced the tubes. If the answer is yes and the wobbling still happens, contact the manufacturer or dealer and get the unit serviced. Do NOT continue to run a centrifuge that wobbles visibly when the rotor is spinning.
Wear a face shield and / or safety goggles if you must work anywhere near a centrifuge that’s in use.
Do not bump, jar, or move the centrifuge while the rotor is spinning. Make sure you don’t have the cord dangling from a table edge where someone could catch their foot in it and pull down the centrifuge.
The following procedures for centrifugation shall be used when working with biohazardous materials:
Examine tubes and bottles for cracks or stress marks before using them. Discard any centrifuge tubes that have cracks in them.
When working with biohazardous materials, wipe outside of tubes with disinfectant prior to removal from the biological safety cabinet and before placing in safety cups or rotors
Place all tubes in safety buckets or sealed rotors when centrifuging infectious materials. Inspect the “O” ring seal of the safety bucket and the inside of safety buckets or rotors.
Section: Use of a Centrifuge

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: The Nurse or designee of The Ferguson Clinic shall review all medical charts, intake forms and Advocates notes.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Each appropriate form/documentation for every patient will be filed in the appropriate medical record.
At the end of every day, the applicable forms, charts, and notes will be reviewed by either the Nurse or designee using the Medical Record Review Form.
Each form, medical record and note will be reviewed to assure the proper procedure was followed and the appropriate information was documented.
Any errors will be noted and will be reviewed with the appropriate personnel to assure their understanding of the proper procedure and documentation practices.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Chart Reviews

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to provide a mechanism for the proper disposal of used needles and syringes, and to ensure that sharps containers are emptied in a timely fashion and those gloves are available for staff use in maintaining body substance isolation.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Sharp containers are in all treatment rooms and the operating room.
Following the use of a needle, place needle and syringe in container.
When sharp container is reasonably full (75% -80%), snap it shut and remove.
Sharp containers are disposed of in a bio-hazard infectious waste container.
Replace new sharp container.

sections.heading: The Infection Control and Prevention Officer or designee will
sections.text:
Check each sharp container each working day for fullness or protruding objects.
Replace above as needed.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Checking of Needle/Glove Boxes

title: Guidelines for Cleaning a Chemical Spill

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that chemical spills will be addressed in a manner that ensures the safety of all patients, staff, and visitors and minimizes damage to the facility

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
If a spill occurs, immediately attend to all individuals who may have been contaminated.
Notify other employees in the immediate area of the spill.
If the spilled material is flammable turn, off all ignitions, electrical, and heat sources.
If the spilled material is nonflammable, establish exhaust ventilation if possible.
Use utility gloves, safety glasses, and respiratory protection to clean the area.

sections.heading: For a Spilled Solid
sections.text:
Sweep into a dustpan and place in a solid waste container for disposal.
Do not vacuum, as special filters may be required due to possible toxic fumes.

sections.heading: For a Spilled Liquid
sections.text:
Pour absorbent material around the perimeter of the spill until contained.
Then pour additional absorbent into the center of the spill.

sections.heading: For Inorganic Acids or Bases
sections.text:
Use baking soda on small spills
For other materials spilled, use vermiculite or sand that is non-reactive:
Using a small shovel or scoop, work the absorbent around the perimeter, then into the middle, until the entire chemical is absorbed.
Scoop up the absorbent material and place it in a container that has a lid for disposal.
Flush with large amounts of water.
Do not use water around electrical sources.
Mop up area after absorbent has been removed.
If the spilled material is extremely volatile, let it evaporate and exhaust with the aid of a mechanical system (i.e., vent hood, fan, etc.)
Materials to be used to clean up a spill can be found in the clean room.
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Guidelines for Cleaning a Chemical Spill

Subject: Covid-19
Section: Cleaning and Disinfection
Subject: Covid-19
Section: Cleaning and Disinfection

sections.heading: PROTOCOL
sections.text: For the duration of the Covid-19 pandemic or until further notification, facilities will follow appropriate CDC Guidelines. This will include the following for cleaning and disinfecting.

sections.heading: HOW TO CLEAN AND DISINFECT
sections.text:
Wear disposable gloves to clean and disinfect.
Clean
Clean surfaces using soap and water. Practice routine cleaning of frequently touched surfaces.
High touch surfaces include: Tables, doorknobs, light switches, countertops, handles, desks, phones, keyboards, toilets, faucets, sinks, etc.
Disinfect
Clean the area or item with soap and water or another detergent if it is dirty. Then, use disinfectant.
Use a recommend EPA registered disinfectants: https://www.epa.gov/pesticide-registration/list-n-disinfectants-use-against-sars-cov-2
Follow the instructions on the label to ensure safe and effective use of the product. Many products recommend:
Keeping surface wet for a period of time (see product label)
Precautions such as wearing gloves and making sure you have good ventilation during use of the product.
Diluted household bleach solutions may also be used if appropriate for the surface.
Check the label to see if your bleach is intended for disinfection, and ensure the product is not past its expiration date. Some bleaches, such as those designed for safe use on colored clothing or for whitening may not be suitable for disinfection.
Unexpired household bleach will be effective against coronaviruses when properly diluted. Follow manufacturer’s instructions for application and proper ventilation. Never mix household bleach with ammonia or any other cleanser. Leave solution on the surface for at least 1 minute. To make a bleach solution, mix:
5 tablespoons (1/3rd cup) bleach per gallon of water Or
4 teaspoons bleach per quart of water
Alcohol solutions with at least 70% alcohol may also be used.
Soft surfaces
For soft surfaces such as carpeted floor, rugs, and drapes
Clean the surface using soap and water or with cleaners appropriate for use on these surfaces.
Launder items (if possible) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Use the warmest appropriate water setting and dry items completely. OR
Disinfect with an EPA-registered disinfectant. Use this link for approved products https://www.epa.gov/pesticide-registration/list-n-disinfectants-use-against-sars-cov-2 for use against Covid-19.
Electronics
For electronics, such as tablets, touch screens, keyboards, and remote controls
Consider putting a wipeable cover on electronics.
Follow manufacturer’s instruction for cleaning and disinfecting.
If no guidance, use alcohol-based wipes or sprays containing at least 70% alcohol. Dry surface thoroughly.
Laundry
For clothing, towels, linens, and other items
Launder items according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Use the warmest appropriate water setting and dry items completely.
Wear disposable gloves when handling dirty laundry from a person who is sick.
Dirty laundry from a person who is sick can be washed with other people’s items.
Do not shake dirty laundry.
Clean and disinfect clothes hampers according to guidance above for surfaces.
Remove gloves, and wash hands right away.
Cleaning and disinfecting your facility if someone is sick
Close off areas used by the person who is sick.
Open outside doors and windows to increase air circulation in the area. Wait 24 hours before you clean or disinfect. If 24 hours is not feasible, wait as long as possible.
Clean and disinfect all areas used by the person who is sick, such as offices, bathrooms, common areas, shared electronic equipment like tablets, touch screens, keyboards, and remote controls.
If more than 7 days since the person who is sick visited or used the facility, additional cleaning and disinfection is not necessary.
Continue routine cleaning and disinfection.
When Cleaning
Wear disposable gloves and gowns for all tasks in the cleaning process, including handling trash.
Additional personal protective equipment (PPE) might be required based on the cleaning/disinfectant products being used and whether there is a risk of splash.
Gloves and gowns should be removed carefully to avoid contamination of the wearer and the surrounding area.
Wash your hands often with soap and water for 20 seconds.
Always wash immediately after removing gloves and after contact with a person who is sick.
Hand sanitizer: If soap and water are not available and hands are not visibly dirty, an alcohol-based hand sanitizer that contains at least 60% alcohol may be used. However, if hands are visibly dirty, always wash hands with soap and water.
Additional CDC guidance related to appropriate cleaning and disinfection:
https://www.cdc.gov/infectioncontrol/guidelines/disinfection/index.html.
Subject: Covid-19
Section: Cleaning and Disinfection

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Reusable components of anesthesia shall be terminally cleaned and sterilized after each use. Single-use components shall be discarded after each use

sections.heading: OBJECTIVE
sections.text: To minimize the risk of cross-infection of patients.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
All surfaces of anesthesia machine and table shall be wiped down with germicide.
Blood pressure cuffs and tubing shall be wiped down with germicide.
Oxygen humidifiers are disposable and shall be changed once a month or when water level is less than half.
Reusable anesthesia equipment that encounters mucous membranes, blood, or body fluids should be cleaned and then processed by disinfection, pasteurization, or sterilization between 	each patient use.
Clean and then disinfect or sterilize reusable items such as laryngeal mask airways (LMAs), masks, breathing circuits, connectors, self-inflating bags, airways, forceps, and laryngoscope blades.
Remove all organic material from equipment to provide optimal conditions for disin­fection/sterilization.
Single-use items (e.g., suction catheters, breathing circuits, endotracheal tubes, stylets) should be used once and discarded according to facility policies and procedures and applicable local, state, and federal regulations.
Reprocessing items intended for single use creates the potential for patient   exposure to harmful residual toxins from disinfecting/sterilizing agents. Verifying safe and effective product function is difficult.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Cleaning of Anesthesia Equipment

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that only brushes designated for use in cleaning instruments and devices be used for that purpose only and be cleaned appropriately.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Reusable brushes create a risk for cross-contamination. Reusable brushes will be cleaned and decontaminated (soaked in enzymatic cleaner per manufacturers guidelines, rinsed and dried) at least daily or when heavily soiled.
Brushes that show wear will be discarded; and
Brushes used to clean instruments and devices with lumens must be the correct size. If the brush it too large, it will not properly fit into the lumen; if shoved into the lumen it could damage the instrument or device and possibly become stuck within; if too small, the brush will not make complete contact with the lumen’s surface and prevent thorough cleaning.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Cleaning of Brushes

sections.heading: Patient’s case will remain open
sections.text:
If the patient perceives a need for The Ferguson Clinic’s services; or
If the Ferguson Clinic can maintain contact with the patient, via telephone calls, follow-up appointments or correspondence.

sections.heading: The patient’s case will be closed for any of the following reasons
sections.text:
When appropriate follow-up has been completed, or after three unsuccessful attempts to follow up.
Patient did not desire follow-up. This may have been indicated by the patient in writing on the Patient Satisfaction Survey or verbally to a staff member. In both cases, the statement is recorded in the patient chart.
Once the medical record has been closed, documentation will be placed on the record stating date closed and will be highlighted.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Closing a Patient’s File

Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Code Responses

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure all personnel know their roles and responsibilities if a Code or Mock Code is called.

sections.heading: DEFINITIONS
sections.text:
Code Red
In the event of a fire - RACE
Remove people from immediate danger
Alarm- Dial 911 and give name and location of fire
Contain the fire (close doors)
Evacuate the facility
Code Blue
To communicate and mobilize the immediate arrival of emergency equipment and specialized personnel to an adult or child whose heart or respiration has stopped.
Code Orange
To communicate and activate a response that will protect patients, staff, and others in case of a hazardous material spill or release, identify unsafe exposure conditions, and help to safely evacuate an area if necessary, within the health care facility or on its grounds
Code Silver
To communicate and mobilize a response to protect patients, visitors, staff, and property in the event of a combative person. If the person has a weapon “Code Silver” should be called.
Code Grey
To call a response team to assist staff in managing and/or de-escalating a potentially threatening situation and gain the cooperation of a person with a weapon or who has taken hostage(s) within the facility or its grounds. Non-adjacent properties should call 911
Amber Alert
An Amber Alert should be initiated when a child or infant is missing or is known to have been abducted

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Each code will be designated with a response team. The designation will made by the Nurse and the Safety Officer.
Code Blue: MD – RN – Anesthesia Provider - Surgeon
Code Orange: MA
Code Silver and Grey: MD, Office Manager, RN/MA
Amber Alert: Front Desk Personnel will contact law enforcement
Training of staff will require mandatory attendance at least annually for all Codes, including but not limited to Malignant Hyperthermia and disasters drills,
All staff is required to attend at least two fire drills annually.
All staff attendance during drills and mock codes will be documented and placed in the Code Drill binder kept in the Office Manager’s office.
A laminated card will be developed with the name of the code and who is responsible for what.
Once a quarter a “mock drill” will be completed including 1 medical code drill and 1 non-medical code drill.
Fire Drills will remain 1 per quarter.
Emergency Drills will remain 1 semi-annually.
An assessment tool will be completed for each code drill assessing weaknesses and strengths.
An action plan will be implemented to improve the weaknesses.
All drill documentation including the assessment tool and action plan will be placed in a binder for review.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Code Responses

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that no information will be given to any other individual, group, or entity without the written consent of the patient.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
All patients will be protected from disclosure of information that violates their right to privacy except where required by law, e.g., child abuse, suicide prevention; staff will follow appropriate Center policies when indicated to report dangerous situations.  Professional ethics require responsibility for maintaining the confidentiality of other private information.
Center personnel shall be responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of private information. Disclosure by and between employees of private information shall occur only as necessary to carry out job functions.
Information shall be released to third parties only upon written authorization from the patient. The patient must sign a Notice of Privacy Practices Acknowledgement or an Authorization to Release Records form. The Authorization to Release Records will be time limited and will not exceed 90 days from the date of signature. A staff person of the Center will witness the written authorization. The patient must show proof of identity.
No confidential information will be released to anyone, even the patient, over the telephone or via facsimile unless it falls under the Notice of Privacy Practices’ Treatment, Payment or Health Care Operations.
If questioned about a patient’s records and there is no existing signed Notice of Privacy Practices Acknowledgment Authorization to Release Records, the correct response is, “Due to confidentiality, I cannot verify the patient has ever been seen or treated at the Center, nor can I tell you that the requested records are on file.”  The patient can then be contacted by the Center staff to let her know a request for records was made and the patient may determine if she would like to provide a written authorization to release the information to the party making the request
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Confidentiality

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Patients with an active respiratory infection are not treated at The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
If a physician or member of the staff suspects that a patient may have an active respiratory.
Infection, that patient will be taken to an exam room and kept separate from others.
The patient will be given a mask.
A physician will be called to examine the patient.
If found to be contagious, the patient will be sent to an appropriate facility, as determined by the physician; and
The room will be closed, and not used again, until it has been cleaned entirely with an appropriate hospital-grade disinfectant.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Contagious Disease and Tuberculosis

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Corrective action at The Ferguson Clinic is progressive. That is, the action taken in response to a rule infraction or violation of standards typically follows a pattern increasing in seriousness until the infraction or violation is corrected.
The usual sequence of corrective actions includes an oral warning, a written warning, probation, and finally termination of employment. In deciding which initial corrective action would be appropriate, the Medical Director and/or designee will consider the seriousness of the infraction, the circumstances surrounding the matter, and the employee’s previous record.
Though committed to a progressive approach to corrective action, The Ferguson Clinic considers certain rule infractions and violations of standards as grounds for immediate termination of employment. Where it pertains to patient confidentiality, these include but are not limited to:
Divulging the organization’s business practices, and misrepresentations of The Ferguson Clinic to a patient, a prospective patient, the public, or personnel.
Breaching patient confidentiality.
Failure to log off computer to ensure patient’s information is protected.
Falsification of a patient’s medical records; and
Sharing one’s login or password.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Corrective Action

Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Sponge, Sharps, and Instrument Counts

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that all sponges, sharps and instruments will be counted before and after surgery on all procedures that present with the possibility that a foreign object could be retained to support patient safety practices in the perioperative setting.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text: It is the responsibility of the RN/RN Circulator or Surgical Tech, if applicable to properly perform the sponge, sharps, and instruments counts.

sections.heading: Sponge/sharps/instruments counts will occur as follows
sections.text:
Prior to the skin incision.
When closure of peritoneum is initiated or any first layer of a cavity or.
When closure of fascia is initiated or layer before subcutaneous; and
As soon as skin closure is initiated
Instances in which additional sponge/sharps/instrument counts should occur include:
Intraoperative additions of sponges/sharps/instruments
Change in RN/RN Circulator
Change in Surgical Tech, if applicable
The initial count should be recorded on the count sheet by the RN/RN Circulator/Surgical Tech as reference to ensure a sponge/sharp/instrument is not retained. Intraoperative additions of sponges/sharps/instruments should be recorded on the count sheet and added to the initial
count, Example:  10 initial count 4x4 Raytec sponges + 10 added sponges = 20 totals.
Instances of when counts may not be required include cystoscopy and ophthalmology procedures.
The procedure for counting sponges should include the following guidelines:
The RN/RN Circulator/Surgical Techs should audibly count the sponges/sharps/instruments and visualize each sponge.
Each sponge/sharps/instrument should be separated to confirm all sponges/sharps/instruments are present and not sticking together, and the correct number of sponges/sharps/instruments if pre-packaged by a manufacturer see below.
All sponges/sharps/instruments must be counted.
All sponges used during the procedure, except for dressing sponges, must be radiopaque, e.g., X-ray detectable, to facilitate locating in the case of a retained sponge.
The binding strip of packaged sponges should only be broken when the sponges are to be counted and used, according to policies.
Sponge/sharps/instrument counts should be performed in the same sequence each time; the Medical Director in cooperation with the RN/RN Circulator/Surgical Tech defines the sequence.
A typical sequence for intraoperative counts is as follows: surgical field, Mayo stand, back table and round basin, kick bucket(s), discarded sponges/bagged sponges/sharps/instruments.
A standardized method for conducting the counts aids the surgical team members in knowing that the counts will always be done the same way for every procedure to contribute to accuracy and efficiency.
The RN/RN Circulator/Surgical Tech should never assume a manufacturer’s prepackaged item is correctly counted. A package of sterile sponges that contains an incorrect number of sponges should be removed from the sterile field, bagged, labeled as incorrect and the number of sponges in the bag, and placed away from the rest of the sponges in the Procedure Room.
However, do not remove the bag from the Procedure Room until the procedure has been completed. A count is also incorrect if there are too many sponges, as well as if there are too few. If the sponge count(s) are incorrect, the possibility exists that a sponge or sponges from the package with the incorrect number of sponges were added to the sterile field. Keeping the incorrect prepackaged sponges in the Procedure Room contributes to reducing the possibility of errors during intraoperative sponge counts.
In the event of a stat emergency procedure in which time is critical and the patient’s life takes precedence, an initial sponge count may not be able to be performed. It is recommended that a count be performed upon the end of the emergency.
Sponges should not be altered, such as cutting to a smaller size. Altering the sponge increases the chance of an incorrect count occurring, or the cut portion of the sponge retained by the patient.
All counted sponges, including sponges that have been bagged, should not be removed from the Procedure Room, until the completion of the procedure, and all counts including sharps and instruments have been verified as being correct. Linen and waste containers should not be removed from the room, until all counts have been completed and verified as being correct.
Miscellaneous items that will be used on and/or placed within the patient should have a radiopaque marker and be included in the count indicated as “miscellaneous items.” A common miscellaneous item is towels; the towels should be included in the counts and kept separate from other non-counted items.
It is recommended that radiopaque counted sponges should not be used for patient skin preps. If counted sponges are used, special attention should be given to preventing them being thrown in the waste container. Unmarked sponges used for skin prep should be thrown into the waste container to avoid being mixed in with counted sponges.
Contaminated sponges/sharps must be handled according to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) Blood borne Pathogens Standard. Recommendations include wearing personal protective equipment in the handling of contaminated sponges and using bags that are leak-proof for the disposal of the sponges and approved sharp containers for the disposal of sharps is to be used to reduce the staff’s exposure to infectious pathogens.
All sponges/sharps/instruments, counted and non-counted, should be removed from the Procedure Room at the end of the procedure. Removal of the sponges/sharps/instruments aids in preventing wrong counts during subsequent procedures in the room.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Sponge, Sharps, and Instrument Counts

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to complete a DVT Risk Assessment on all patients that are requesting to have surgery.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The surgeon and patient will fill out the ASPS DVE Risk Assessment form at the pre-operative visit. This will be placed in the patient’s medical chart. Any patients with a Caprini score >7 will not be eligible for a surgical procedure at The Ferguson Clinic.
All patients will be given written information on risk factors and signs and symptoms of DVT/PE.
All patients undergoing general anesthesia or hair grafting will have compression placed before the procedure and left in place until at least the postoperative visit or 24 hours if they meet the criterion for no postoperative day-one after the procedure.
All patients undergoing general anesthesia or other procedures of > 1 hour that do not permit frequent ambulation will have sequential compression devices placed during the procedure.
No procedures requiring chemoprophylaxis as determined by the operating surgeon will not undergo surgical procedures at The Ferguson Clinic.
All patients must be ambulatory before discharge from The Ferguson Clinic.
At the preoperative appointment and upon discharge (including the responsible adult), all patients will be instructed to ambulate once hour.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: DVT Risk Assessment

Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: Deeper than Expected Sedation

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text:
Anesthetic is used to facilitate the performance of medical or surgical procedures, for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, while providing anxiolysis, sedation, amnesia and/or pain relief for patient comfort. This protocol provides guidelines for the administration of anesthetic to adults. For purposes of this protocol, adults are defined as 18 years and older. This protocol applies to all procedures, and in all patient populations.
Because the level of a patient’s sedation is defined by the patient’s psychological and physiologic state, and the ability to maintain a patent airway and spontaneous ventilation, this protocol does not delineate what constitutes the levels of sedation based solely on the route, dose or specific drug used. Therefore, providers are directed to the definitions below for use in determining when the intended plan for sedation/analgesia can be expected to render a patient sedated at the minimal (anxiolysis), moderate (conscious), or deep level.
It is the responsibility of the Medical Director, Physicians, Anesthesia Providers and Nurses to ensure that departmental policies and practices comply with the established criteria in this or an equivalent approved protocol.
Because sedation is a continuum, it is not always possible to predict how an individual patient will respond. Hence, practitioners intending to produce a given level of sedation should be able to rescue patients whose level of sedation becomes deeper than initially intended.
Rescue of a patient from a deeper level of sedation than intended is an intervention by a practitioner proficient in airway management and resuscitation. The qualified practitioner corrects adverse physiologic consequences of the deeper than intended level of sedation (such as hypoventilation, hypoxia, and hypotension) and returns the patient to the originally intended level of sedation.
Recognizing that sedation exists on a continuum and that patients may unavoidably or unintentionally slip into a deeper than intended level of sedation or analgesia:
All providers will be qualified to rescue patients from deep sedation and competent to manage a compromised airway and to provide adequate oxygenation and ventilation.
Staff responsible for drug administration and patient monitoring will demonstrate knowledge of medications used for sedation/analgesia and will maintain competence in patient assessment and monitoring, CPR, and basic airway management.

sections.heading: DRUG SELECTION
sections.text: In view of the diversity of procedures, and of the age and drug sensitivity of individual patients, these guidelines cannot fully address the specific drugs or dosages to be used for individual circumstances. Drug choices and dosages must be individualized to the patient. The responsible physician/dentist selects appropriate medications to produce the intended degree of sedation/analgesia. Sedative/analgesia drugs and doses will be titrated to achieve optimal patient comfort and outcomes with minimal risk of untoward effects.

sections.heading: EQUIPMENT
sections.text:
The following minimum equipment must be present, in working order and ready for use:
Oxygen, including a positive pressure delivery system capable of administering a five-liter flow rate for at least 60 minutes.
Suction
Emergency airway equipment
Non-Invasive blood pressure monitors and manual blood pressure cuff
Pulse oximeter
Cardiac Monitor
Emergency ventilation and drug boxes
A cardiac arrest cart with defibrillator

sections.heading: PATIENT MONITORING AND DOCUMENTATION
sections.text:
The patient monitor must be able to observe and respond appropriately to the patient’s clinical condition and response to medication, including, at a minimum:
Changes in vital signs
Level of consciousness
Oxygen saturation
Compromise of a patent airway
Adverse drug reaction
Minimum monitoring and documentation throughout the sedation period shall include:
Baseline blood pressure, pulse and respiratory rates, level of consciousness and oxygen saturation.
Ongoing pulse rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, and level of consciousness at least every fifteen minutes and more frequently as indicated by the patient’s clinical needs and the expected effects of the drug(s) and dosage(s) in use.
Blood pressure will be monitored and documented minimally at pre-and post-procedure and at intervals throughout the procedure as indicated by the patient’s clinical needs and the expected effects of the drug(s) and dosage(s) in use.
Electrocardiographic monitoring should be used always.
A pain score will be obtained at the beginning and end, and at appropriate intervals. An age and developmentally appropriate pain scale will be used for this assessment.

sections.heading: ADVERSE REACTIONS
sections.text:
Based on the information above, if it is determined through all the means outlined above that a patient is having an adverse reaction to sedation it will be the responsibility of the Physician and the Anesthesia provider to take immediate action to reverse the adverse reaction. By means of administering a reversal drug, additional oxygen enough to stabilize the patient and have the patient transported to The Queen’s Medical Center.
The nurse manager or designee will call 911 while stabilization is in progress. A copy of the patient’s medical record will be given to the emergency medical technician and the patient will be transported to The Queen’s Medical Center.
The Queen’s Medical Center will send a copy of the discharge summary to The Ferguson Clinic and the discharge summary will be placed in the patient’s medical record.
An incident report will be completed by all parties. The Director of the Hawaii Department of Health and The Association for Ambulatory Health Care (AAAHC) accreditation has been obtained will be contacted to report the adverse reaction and its outcome.
The physician will follow up with the patient as applicable and see the patient back in the office for the normal follow up.
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: Deeper than Expected Sedation

Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Device and Media Controls

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: Device and media controls are designed to facilitate the control of the flow of ePHI onto and from the premises. The control of the flow of information is enhanced by following carefully designed steps when disposing of media, when re-using media, by documenting how, when, and where hardware and software is accounted for, and backing-up ePHI and storing ePHI within acceptable practices.

sections.heading: DISPOSAL
sections.text:
The Privacy Officer will oversee the shredding of all documents and reports that contain security and system configuration information.
Any information should be reviewed prior to destruction to ensure compliance with The Ferguson Clinic’s record retention policies and with applicable legal and regulatory requirements.

sections.heading: ACCOUNTABILITY
sections.text:
Pursuant to the policy and procedure entitle “Information Systems Inventory”, a complete inventory of hardware and software, their movements, and any person responsible for those movements is maintained.
If the Ferguson Clinic’s personnel or another person wishes to remove ePHI from the premises of The Ferguson Clinic, prior approval by the Privacy Officer is required, and the removal and subsequent return of any electronic media containing ePHI shall be logged. The log shall be maintained by the Privacy Officer.

sections.heading: REMOVABLE MEDIA CONTROLS
sections.text:
In general, the use of removable media is discouraged. The Ferguson Clinic provides a network that provides for transmission of information between company computers requiring minimal need for the use of removable media for transport.
Information stored on removable media must be protected to the same degree as information stored on The Ferguson Clinic information systems. Such media must be appropriately labeled to identify it as PHI, and it must never be left unattended in unsecured areas.
Confidential data stored on removable media must not be transported outside of The Ferguson Clinic’ facility unless approved by the Privacy Officer. If transported off the premises, ePHI must be logged and must be either in direct control of an authorized The Ferguson Clinic personnel member or under physical lock with access granted only to an authorized The Ferguson Clinic’ personnel member.
If ePHI is stored on a mobile computing device and there is a breach of confidentiality of the ePHI, The Ferguson Clinic will follow the policy and procedure entitled “Notification of Breach of Unsecured PHI”.

sections.heading: MOBILE COMPUTING DEVICES
sections.text:
PHI must never be stored on mobile computing devices unless the devices have the following minimum-security requirements implemented:
The device must be running an operating system that requires secure login via a password.
The files stored on the device must be subject to safeguards that have been approved by the Privacy Officer.
The device must have other security requirements, as required by the Privacy Officer and the Medical Director’s sole discretion.
Mobile computing devices must never be left unattended in unsecured areas.

sections.heading: MEDIA RE-USE
sections.text:
The Privacy Officer shall ensure that the hard drives of computers, other media of computer equipment, and all removable storage media are appropriately wiped clean of ePHI prior to any re-use of the same.
ePHI is removed from hard drives or electronic media prior to re-use by the IT person/department.
The effective removal of ePHI from hardware or electronic media is verified prior to allowing re-use.

sections.heading: DATA BACKUP AND STORAGE
sections.text:
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Device and Media Controls

Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Disposal of PHI

sections.heading: PURPOSES
sections.text: The purpose of the policy is to comply with requirements pertaining to the destruction of documents and other materials containing PHI and the receipt, tracking and removal of hardware and electronic media that contain ePHI.

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text:
Applies to all personnel at The Ferguson Clinic
Employees
Temporary Staff
Consultants

sections.heading: POLICY AND PROCEDURE STATEMENT
sections.text:
Destruction of Paper Copies and Original Documents (Day-to-Day Disposal)
Any printed material (e.g., faxes, printed emails, informal notes about patients, patient identification stickers/labels) containing PHI must not be discarded in trash bins, unsecured recycle bins or other publicly accessible locations. Instead, this information must be personally shredded or placed in secure shredder bins. The Ferguson Clinic shall provide users with access to shredders or secured shredder bins for proper disposal of confidential printouts containing PHI.
The user may elect to use either shredding or secured shredder bins if the destruction is in accordance with this policy. It is the individual’s responsibility to ensure that the document has been secured or destroyed. It is the organization’s responsibility to ensure that their personnel are adhering to the policy.
After documents have reached their retention period according to the State of Hawaii, all PHI must be securely destroyed.
Disposal of patient identification forms must be discarded in shredding bins ONLY.
Documentation of PHI Disposal
To ensure that it is in fact performed, The Ferguson Clinic’ personnel or a bonded destruction service must carry out the destruction PHI.
If personnel undertake the destruction of the records, personnel must log the information in the Disposal Log that is in the Privacy Officer’s desk.
If a bonded shredding company is utilized for the final destruction of the records, the company must provide a report which will contain the following:
Method of destruction
Description of the disposed records
Date of Disposal
The signature of the person disposing and supervising the disposing of the records.
This report will be placed in the Disposal Log
Hardware – Biomedical and Non-Biomedical
All personnel will follow procedures related to receipt, removal, storage, re-use, and disposal of hardware.
Hardware shall be controlled and accounted for always through the Medical Director and/or Privacy Officer.
All hardware shall be assigned an owner.
There shall be a record of the movements of all hardware containing ePHI, the owner and the designated individual(s) responsible for the movement.
The movement of hardware shall be authorized and logged by the Privacy Officer prior to hardware and electronic media entering or leaving the facility.
The Privacy Officer shall be accountable for hardware while in transit between facilities.
Hardware shall be properly logged and disposed of when no longer used.
ePHI shall be removed from hardware before it is made available for reuse.
A retrievable, exact copy of ePHI shall be created before any movement of hardware.
Electronic Media – Biomedical and Non-Biomedical
Electronic media containing PHI shall be physically destroyed when no longer used or no longer needed.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Disposal of PHI

title: Email, Instant Messaging, and Internet Use

sections.text:
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Email, Instant Messaging, and Internet Use

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
All messages created, sent, or received using the center’s email system, including personal messages, are the property of The Ferguson Clinic. The center retains the right to access, retrieve, and disclose the contents of all messages, including personal messages. Personnel may not retrieve or read any email message that was not sent to them unless authorized by the designated email recipient or the Medical Director.
Users should be aware that deleted emails are recoverable through the computer backup systems that are in place.

sections.heading: EMAIL MAY BE USED FOR THE FOLLOWING PURPOSES
sections.text:
Communications for work-related purposes within The Ferguson Clinic.
Communications for work-related purposes with people outside of The Ferguson Clinic.
Incidental and occasional personal use of email. Excessive use of email for personal and non-business-related communications is discouraged.

sections.heading: UNACCEPTABLE USE OF EMAIL INCLUDES
sections.text:
Excessive distribution of jokes, gossip and rumors, junk, or chain mail.
Email which would be likely to harass, insult or discriminate based on age, sex, race, religion, national origin, political beliefs, disability, or other criteria. (Users need to be aware that email may render them and/or the company liable for harassment or discrimination claims, and possibly defamation actions).
The distribution of information that infringes copyright laws.
Using email to further personal business activities, or excess use for personal non-business activities; and
Using email to further any illegal activity.
Furthering any activity in breach of the employee’s terms and conditions of employment.
Sending emails so they appear to be from another person; and
Distribution of confidential information to third parties without authorization.
Any users that have material on their computers that may conflict with the above guidelines should remove such material immediately. Users should regularly purge mailboxes and deleted items.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Email, Instant Messaging, and Internet Use

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to maintain an emergency cart(s) in accordance with the following procedure.

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of this policy is to ensure a properly stocked emergency cart will always be readily available.

sections.heading: GENERAL INFORMATION and DESIRED OUTCOME
sections.text:
An emergency cart is a cart that facilitates coordination of emergency equipment.
A Licensed Staff Member (MD, DO, RN, CRNA) as designated by the Medical Director is responsible for checking the crash cart, oxygen cylinder levels, AED, and documenting compliance on crash cart checklist.
Each emergency cart is equipped with a numbered lock and kept locked unless in use.
If the lock is not intact, the cart is to be checked and personnel will replace any
missing supplies. Medication trays and numbered locks are controlled by the RN.
The integrity of the lock and lock number of the cart is checked every day and recorded
AED load checks will be performed every day with the defibrillator plugged in and unplugged.
All carts will be opened and checked for contents once monthly and following each use.
Sterile items will be checked for package integrity and expiration date. Items
with expiration dates expiring within the month will be replaced.
The medication drawer will not be opened if it is sealed and intact.
Laryngoscopes will be checked prior to placement on the cart and monthly.
Oxygen cylinders are replaced when the tank has < 500 psi.
The RN and Anesthesia Provider (when applicable) will check the emergency cart for proper drug storage, stock level, and logged.
Drawers of crash carts are to be clearly labeled to identify contents by general
categories (drugs, cardiac/chest procedures, circulation, breathing, and airway).
Special procedure trays are kept on the bottom shelf.

sections.heading: EQUIPMENT (essential)
sections.text:
Drawers are organized and arranged from top to bottom in the following order
medications, airway, circulation, IV solution and tubing, miscellaneous.
The RN will set up the cart(s) to include adult, pediatric, or newborn equipment, as applicable using the appropriate supplies listed.
The pediatric equipment indicated will be maintained for those adults that may require a pediatric supply as per the physician or anesthesiology provider.

sections.heading: The cart is inspected for the following External contents
sections.text:
Portable suction apparatus with connecting tubing.
Portable monitor/defibrillator with charged batteries, multi-function cable, multifunction pads (pedi, adult or both as appropriate), pacer cable (if pacer capable machine), EKG electrodes, appropriate sized paddles (adult, pediatric), defibrillation gel, monitor paper, blood pressure cuff (adult carts), SpO2 probe.
Sharps container.
Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation record forms.
Emergency Crash Cart Check Sheet.
List of cart contents.
Emergency drug information sheets.
Cardiac board
Cart contents according to appropriate appendix.
Adult Supplies – See attached
Pediatric Supplies – See attached
Replacement of Stock
Medications
Other supplies

sections.heading: ADULT EMERGENCY DRUGS
sections.text:
(Based on ACLS guidelines)
ADENOSINE Antiarrhythmic in PSVT: 6 mg. rapid IV push, if no response in 1-2 minutes, administer dose of 12 mg., repeat 12 mg. dose a second time if required.
AMIODARONE Antiarrhythmic: Loading dose: 150mg. Amiodarone IVP (up to 300mg. IVP for cardiac arrest from VF/VT after multiple shocks) for life threatening arrhythmia.
Loading dose of 150mg. Amiodarone may be mixed in 100cc D5W and infused over 10 minutes or given IVP. Follow with IV infusion upon arrhythmia resolution. IV infusion: 450mg. Amiodarone in 250ml D5W. Initial infusion 1mg/min-x 6 hours (33ml/hr x 6 hours). Decrease infusion to maintenance dose of 0.5mg./min (17ml/hr).
ATROPINE Agent used for symptomatic bradycardia, PEA: 0.5-1 mg. IV push, repeat at 3-5 min. Intervals to max. Total dose of .04 mg./kg. May be given via endotracheal route.
Stocked 1 mg./10 ml bristoject.
DEXTROSE Antihypoglycemic: 10 to 25 gms. IVP. Repeat dose as required in severe cases depending on symptoms and blood glucose levels.
DOBUTAMINE Vasopressor: IV infusion: 500 mg. Dobutamine in 250 ml IV solution. Usual dose 2- 5mcg/kg/min. May titrate to upper dose of 20mcg. /kg/min. Primarily stimulates B-1
receptors in the heart and is used for inotropic support with mild chronotropic effect. Adequate hydration of patient imperative in blood pressure support. When mixing more than 500mg. Dobutamine in IV solution, equal volume must be removed (e.g., 1gm./40ml Dobutamine, remove 40ml from IV solution).
DOPAMINE Vasopressor, IV infusion: Usual dose in code situation is 5-20mcg. /kg/min. Renal
perfusion dosing 2-5mcg. /kg/min increase of cardiac output 5-10mcg. /kg/min and peripheral vasoconstriction 10-20mcg. /kg/min. As approaching 20mcg. /kg/min assess urine output. Extravasation treatment is with phentolamine. Adequate hydration of patient imperative in blood pressure support. Premix drip of 400 mg. Dobutamine in 250 ml IV solution.
EPINEPHRINE Adrenergic agent of choice for cardiac arrest, vasopressor used in Pulseless VT/VF, Asystole and PEA: 1 mg IV every 3-5 min. Or more frequently. May be given via endotracheal route. Stocked 1 mg./10 ml 1:10,000. If using for hypersensitivity reaction 0.1-0.3 mg. SQ, SIVP. IV infusion mix 1 mg. in 250 ml IV solution. Start at 1 mcg. /min and titrate up as needed (1mcg. /min = 15cc/hr.). Stocked as1 mg./1 ml 30ml multidose vials. Bristojects are 1mg/10ml.
ETOMIDATE Induction of rapid hypnosis (non-barbiturate) usual dose 0.2-0.6mg./kg. IVP
immediately prior or during intubation if needed.
FLUMAZENIL Antidote for benzodiazepine: Initial dose 0.2mg. administered IV over 15 seconds. If desired level of consciousness is not obtained after waiting additional 45 seconds,
second dose of 0.2mg. may be administered. Repeat at 60-second intervals as necessary to a maximum total dose of 1mg. Individualize the dose based on patient response. Use with caution in patients with a history of seizures.
LIDOCAINE Antiarrhythmic: Bolus: 1-1.5 mg./kg IV push initial bolus. Repeat doses of
0.5-1 mg./kg. at 5-10 min. intervals if needed until total dose of 3 mg./kg. for cardiac arrest from VF/VT that persists after multiple shocks. May be given via endotracheal route. Follow with IV infusion if effective in arrhythmia resolution. IV infusion: use-premixed bag of 2 gm. in 500 cc D5W. 1-4 mg./min. (1mg./min = 15cc/hr of premix Lidocaine drip).
MAGNESIUM In cardiac arrest: 1-2 gm. (diluted in 10ml D5W or NS) IVP in torsades de pointes or when it is suspected that the arrhythmia is caused by a hypomagnesemic state.
NALOXONE Antidote for opiate narcotics: Initial dose of 0.4mg. to 2mg. IV; may repeat IV at 2 to 3-minute intervals. If no response is observed after 10mg has been administered, question the diagnosis of narcotic induced or partial narcotic induced toxicity. May also be given IM, SQ or via endotracheal tube.
NOREPINEPHRINE Vasopressor, IV infusion: mix 4 mg. in 250-cc IV solution (in D5W only). Usual dose 2-12 mcg/min to titrate for blood pressure control. Monitor urine output.
Adequate hydration imperative in blood pressure control.
PROCAINAMIDE Antiarrhythmic: Bolus: 100 mg IV every 5 min. (or at a rate of 20mg./minute IVSP) until one of the following observed: 1) arrhythmia suppressed; 2) hypotension ensues; 3) QRS complex is widened by 50% of its original width; 4) a total dose of 17 mg/kg has been given. If effective administer IV infusion. IV infusion: 1 gm. Procainamide in 250 ml IV solution. Stocked 1 gm./10 ml. Range: 1-4 mg./min. (1mg/min = 15cc/hr).
SODIUM BICARBONATE Alkalinizer used in metabolic acidosis: Should be used, if at all, only after application of definitive and better substantiated interventions and drugs. 1 mEq/kg IV push. Repeat doses are usually 0.5 mEq/kg, based on ABG results. Stocked 1 mEq/1 ml 10ml and 50ml Bristojects.
VASOPRESSIN Antidiuretic hormone, vasoconstrictor: 40U as a single, one-time bolus. Alternative agent to epinephrine, vasopressin is used in persistent or recurrent VT/VF for vasoconstriction and increasing blood flow to the brain and heart during CPR. Use epinephrine to follow up in 10 minutes if there is no response to vasopressin.
VERAPAMIL Calcium channel blocking agent, antiarrhythmic: Indications include PSVT, W-P-W syndrome. Initial dose 5 - 10mg. IV push given over 2 minutes. Repeat with 10mg. in 30 min. if initial dose is not adequate. Do not administer concomitantly with IV B-adrenergic blocking agents since both may depress myocardial contractility and AV conduction. Stocked 5 mg./2 ml.

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of the Emergency Preparedness Management Plan is to describe how The Ferguson Clinic plans for and manages a program to ensure an effective response to emergencies.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to prepare for and respond to disasters or emergencies affecting the environment of care.

sections.heading: FORMS TO BE USED
sections.text: Hazard Vulnerability Analysis

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text: The Emergency Preparedness Management Plan applies to the facility, all patients, staff, and visitors of The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To identify specific procedures in response to a variety of disasters based on a hazard vulnerability analysis that is performed by this organization.
To notify external authorities of emergencies.
To notify personnel when emergency response measures are initiated.
To manage space, supplies, and security.
To assign available personnel in emergencies to cover all necessary staff positions.
To evacuate the building when the environment cannot support adequate patient care and treatment.
To establish an alternate care site when the environment cannot support adequate patient care and treatment.
To manage patients during emergencies (including scheduling, modifying, or discontinuing services; control of patient information; and patient transportation).
To identify an alternate source of essential utilities.
To establish a back-up communication system in the event of failure during disasters and emergencies.
To identify facilities for radioactive or chemical isolation and decontamination and identify alternate roles and responsibilities of personnel during emergencies.RESPONSIBILITIES:
The Medical Director receives information on the Emergency Preparedness Program from the Safety Officer in collaboration with the Quality Improvement Committee.
The Office Manager and/or Safety Officer are responsible for ensuring that emergency preparedness objectives are met.

sections.heading: Employee Responsibilities
sections.text:
Understand his/her role and responsibilities in the event of an emergency.
Participate in emergency preparedness drills.

sections.heading: PROCESSES OF THE EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS PLAN
Mitigation Activities
sections.text:
Based on a hazard vulnerability analysis that has been performed, a variety of disasters/potential emergencies have been identified.
Mitigation activities have been designed to lessen the impact of the disaster/potential emergencies identified.
Mitigation activities include developing systems for response, maintaining emergency equipment and supplies, training staff, and conducting drills.
Emergency drills are conducted based on the hazard vulnerability analysis that identifies potential risks. Emergency Drills are performed semi-annually.
Drills are conducted on events such as: utility failures, tornadoes, bomb threats, a hostage situation, lost child.

sections.heading: Preparation for an Emergency
sections.text:
A variety of policies have been developed that address specific roles and responsibilities of personnel during emergency procedures.
Each policy identifies the appropriate response that is to be undertaken by the employee who identifies a disaster or potential emergency.
The Physicians and the Office Manager are to be notified whenever a disaster or potential emergency exists.

sections.heading: External Notification
sections.text:
Emergency plans identify when external authorities are notified.
The police or fire departments can be notified by calling 911.

sections.heading: Internal Notification
sections.text:
Specific Codes for Emergencies have been defined, and staff will be notified when an emergency plan is to be put into action.
An Emergency Call List is maintained for notification of staff after hours.
Space Management
The Physician(s) and the Office Manager are responsible for managing the facility during a declared emergency.
If an emergency requires outside assistance from the police or fire department, the responsibility for managing the facility will be transferred to the outside authority.
The Physician(s) or designee will determine facility closure and initiate the process of closure.  Staff will be told to terminate patient visits and to reschedule.

sections.heading: Total and/or Partial Evacuation
sections.text:
The Internal/External Disaster Policy outlines the procedures to be followed.
In the event of partial evacuation, staff will direct patients to the waiting room, the conference room, or the break room.
In the event of a total evacuation, staff will take patients to the designated meeting area Main Parking Lot.

sections.heading: Alternate Site
sections.text:
If the facility is closed, patients will be rescheduled.
In the event of a patient emergency, 911 will be called and the patient will be transferred to The Queen’s Medical Center.

sections.heading: Fire, explosion, wildfire, or gas leak
sections.text: Contact the local fire department by dialing 911.

sections.heading: Bomb threat, civil disorder, or hostage situation
sections.text: Contact the police department by dialing 911.

sections.heading: Extreme weather, tornado watch or warning
sections.text: Move all personnel, patients, and visitors to the conference room or break room, which has been designated.

sections.heading: Missing child
sections.text: Block all exits, call 911 to notify authorities, and search building.

sections.heading: Furnace/HVAC failure
sections.text: Discontinue service if internal temperature is below 55 degrees or above 80 degrees.

sections.heading: Power failure
sections.text:
Maintain supply of flashlights/ extra batteries.  Assist patients in exiting the facility and reschedule appointments when notified.
Emergency Lights.
Battery back-up.

sections.heading: Loss of water
sections.text: Use bottled water and disinfectant wipes for hand washing until notified to close the facility or until water is restored.

sections.heading: Loss of communications
sections.text: Use cellular phones, walkie/talkies, or runners.

sections.heading: Equipment/Utility failure
sections.text: Maintain contact list of service representatives and manufacturers’ names and phone numbers.

sections.heading: Loss of Suction
sections.text: Back up suction equipment would be utilized until suction was completed.

sections.heading: Laser Malfunction
sections.text: Laser procedures would be discontinued until the Laser could be fixed.

sections.heading: Recovery
sections.text:
The Physician(s) or designee is responsible for determining the appropriate time and response for operations to continue.
The Office Manager will contact all personnel to return to work.

sections.heading: Orientation and Education Program
sections.text:
The Office Manager or Safety Officer will train staff during orientation and annually thereafter on their roles during an emergency.
Training can be accomplished through classroom activities, one-on-one discussions or through the completion of a self-study packet that includes information on general/worker safety, emergency preparedness, fire safety, hazardous materials and waste, medical equipment, security, and utilities.
All training is documented in the employee’s personnel file.

sections.heading: Performance Monitoring
sections.text:
The Office Manager is responsible for coordinating the performance-monitoring process for the Emergency Management Plan.
Performance standards are monitored by the Office Manager and/or the Safety Officer in collaboration with the QIP Committee.
Summaries of findings and recommendations, based on trends, performance measures, and performance improvement activities will be documented quarterly by the QIP Committee.
Specific information will be communicated to staff when issues or opportunities to reduce the risk of a potential emergency exist.

sections.heading: Performance Standards Include
sections.text:
Staff knowledge and skills.
Level of staff participation.
Monitoring and inspection activities.
Emergency and incident reporting.
Inspection, preventive maintenance, and testing of equipment.

sections.heading: Annual Review
sections.text:
The Office Manager and/or the Safety Officer, in collaboration with the QIP Committee, are responsible for the annual review of the Emergency Preparedness Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
The annual review will be compiled at the end of the year based on information from a variety of sources including, but not limited to:
Critiques of emergency drills.
Staff orientation and training.
QIP Committee minutes.
Performance monitoring activities.
Other summaries of activities, including the findings of regulatory agencies.
The annual review will be presented to the Governing Body in a narrative report that covers the Emergency Preparedness Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Emergency Preparedness Plan

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
Plan Components- The contingency plan shall consist of three components
Data Back Up Plan
Disaster Recovery Plan
A disaster recovery plan is developed to provide for the restoration of any data lost because of a system interruption caused by emergencies, disasters, or other occurrences.
The disaster recovery plan will coordinate with the Safety Officer who oversees the Emergency Preparedness & Disaster Plan of The Ferguson Clinic.
Emergency Mode Operation Plan
The emergency mode operation plan shall contain processes to enable continuation of critical business processes in the event of a disaster or emergency that damages information systems containing ePHI and other PHI in all forms.
The emergency mode operation plan will be activated when an emergency that may impact critical business processes is reasonably anticipated, as well as in an actual emergency. The emergency mode operation plan will specifically address the restoration of operations.
Testing and Revision
Each component of the contingency plan shall be tested as determined necessary by the Privacy Officer, but no less than biannually, with results of the tests sent to the Board of Directors or appropriate committee for review. The results of such testing shall be documented and retained for six years. Based upon the results of testing, the procedures set forth in each of the plan components shall be revised as needed.
Insurance
Coverage will be maintained on information systems and data to adequately protect The Ferguson Clinic from severe financial loss due to malicious activity and natural disasters.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Emergency/Disaster Contingency Plan

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Employee Health
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Employee Health

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to minimize or prevent the spread of infection by providing guidelines to employees regarding their return to work.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text: Employees should not report to work if they have any of the following:

sections.heading: Upper Respiratory Infections
sections.text:
Restrict personnel from direct care of high-risk patients until acute symptoms resolve.
Individual evaluation is necessary.

sections.heading: Group A Streptococcal
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient contact until 24 hours after adequate treatment has been initiated.

sections.heading: Hepatitis B
sections.text:
Remove the employee from duty when serological markers confirm physician diagnosis of Hepatitis B.
The employee may return to work with MD release and when serological markers indicate immunity (antibodies to HBc and HBs are present and HBsAG and HBeAG are negative).

sections.heading: Hepatitis A
sections.text:
Remove the employee from direct patient care when diagnosis of Hepatitis A has been made by the physician.
The employee may return to work with a physician release and when serological markers indicate immunity (total anti-HAV).

sections.heading: Influenza
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient care until symptoms resolve.

sections.heading: Infectious Mono
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient care.  The employee may return to work with release from a Physician.

sections.heading: Measles
sections.text:
Remove the employee from duty until seven (7) days after the rash appears and is released by the physician.
Post-exposure – Remove the employee from duty from the fifth (5) through the twenty-first (21) day after exposure.

sections.heading: Mumps
sections.text:
Remove the employee from duty until nine (9) days after onset of parotitis and is released by a
physician.

sections.heading: Pertussis
sections.text: Remove the employee from duty from the beginning of catarrhal stages through the third (3) weeks after onset of paroxysms or until seven (7) days after start of effective therapy.  Return to work with a physician release.

sections.heading: Rubella
sections.text: Remove the employee from duty until five (5) days after the rash appears and is released by a physician.

sections.heading: Varicella
sections.text:
Remove the employee from duty until all lesions are dry and crusted and is released by a physician.
Post-exposure – Remove the employee from duty from the tenth (10) through the twenty-first (21) day after exposure.

sections.heading: Acute Diarrhea
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient care pending investigation and until symptoms resolve and refer to a physician.

sections.heading: Salmonella
sections.text:
Remove the employee from direct patient care until stool cultures are salmonella free on two
consecutive specimens collected not less than 24 hours apart.

sections.heading: Other enteric pathogens
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient care until appropriate treatment is started and symptoms resolve.  A physician release is required.

sections.heading: Herpetic Whitlow
sections.text: Restrict the employee from direct patient care in all areas until lesions are healed.

sections.heading: Staphylococcus skin lesions
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient care until lesions have resolved.

sections.heading: Zoster (Shingles)
sections.text: Appropriate barrier should be used.

sections.heading: Scabies or Lice
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient care until treated.

sections.heading: Conjunctivitis
sections.text: Remove the employee from direct patient contact until treated and discharge ceases.

sections.heading: Acquired Immune Disease Syndrome
sections.text:
Refer the employee to and consult with a physician.
Complete medical and epidemiological assessment of the employee to determine fitness and
safety to perform patient care or other job-related duties.
Consultation with the physician to determine whether changes should be made in the
employee’s work assignment.
The employee will be counseled on risks of caring for other patients and be required to practice
good hygiene and barrier techniques.
Current guidelines and practices of the Center for Disease Control and the Public Health Service
will be followed.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Employee Health

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Equipment Management Plan
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Equipment Management Plan

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of the Equipment Management Plan is to control and reduce the risk of medical equipment used for the diagnosis and treatment of patient care.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of the The Ferguson Clinic to promote the safe and effective use of medical equipment.

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text: The Equipment Management Plan applies to all fixed and portable medical equipment used within the facility.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To establish written criteria for identifying, evaluating, and taking inventory of medical equipment to be included in the management plan before the equipment is used.
To assess and minimize clinical and physical risks of equipment use through inspection, testing, and maintenance.
To monitor and act on equipment hazard notice recalls.
To report incidents in which a medical device relates to the death, serious injury or serious illness of any individuals.
To monitor, investigate, and report on equipment management problems, failures, and user errors that have or may have an adverse effect on patient safety and/or the quality of care.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
The Physician(s) and/or designee are responsible for selecting all medical equipment.  The QIP Committee is responsible for ensuring the proper functioning and maintenance of all equipment that must do with the safety of staff and patients.
The Governing Body is responsible for acquiring all medical equipment.
The Medical Director and/or RN is responsible for the implementation of the Equipment Management Plan.

sections.heading: PROCESSES OF THE EQUIPMENT MANAGEMENT PLAN
Medical equipment is inventoried by the Office Manager to assess
sections.text:
Equipment function.
Physical risks associated with use.
Maintenance requirements.
Equipment incident history.

sections.heading: Incident Reporting and Investigation
sections.text:
Any equipment management problems, failure or user error should be reported to the Medical Director.
All hazard notices and equipment recall are to be sent to the Medical Director.
Equipment malfunctions will be tracked by the Office Manager and reported to the QIP Committee quarterly.
The Office Manager or designee will report to the manufacturer and/or the FDA any equipment that is connected to the serious injury, illness, or death of any individual.

sections.heading: Inspect, Test and Maintain Equipment
sections.text:
All electrical equipment in patient care areas must be inspected by a biomedical engineer prior to initial use and annually thereafter.
Maintenance records should be kept on medical equipment to provide contact information on the manufacturer, service representative, and date of service and description of service.
Critical equipment such as a defibrillator, oxygen canisters, surgical equipment and cardiac monitors will be checked prior to the first scheduled surgery of the day.
Logs will be kept that reflect this check, and the individual doing the testing will initial upon completion.
If a piece of critical equipment (i.e., defibrillator, autoclave) malfunctions, surgery will be canceled until a loaner is obtained.
Alarms on medical equipment with alarms will be tested as applicable.
Sterilizers will be monitored based on manufacturer’s instructions.
Each pack/tray is monitored to ensure the proper temperature was reached.
Spore testing is performed per load.

sections.heading: Orientation and Training
sections.text:
The Medical Director and Office Manager is responsible for training all employees who will be using medical equipment during orientation or prior to use on the following:
Capabilities, limitations, and special applications of the equipment.
Basic operating and safety procedures.
Manufacturer’s directions are to be followed always.
All manuals for equipment will be kept in use.
Emergency procedures in the event of equipment failure.
Specific procedures in the event of equipment failure.
When and how to perform emergency clinical interventions when medical equipment fails.
Availability of backup equipment.
How to obtain repair services.
Information and skills necessary to perform the necessary maintenance.
How to fill out an incident report on equipment failure, malfunction, or user error.

sections.heading: Performance Monitoring
sections.text:
The Office Manager is responsible for coordinating the performance-monitoring process for the Equipment Management program.
Performance standards will be monitored by the Medical Director in collaboration with the QIP Committee.
Summaries of findings and recommendations, based on trends, performance measures, and performance improvement activities will be documented quarterly by the QIP Committee.
Specific information will be communicated to staff when issues or opportunities to reduce the risk of equipment hazards exist.

sections.heading: Performance Standards relate to one or more of the following
sections.text:
Staff knowledge and skill.
Level of staff participation.
Monitoring and inspection activities.
Emergency and incident reporting.
Inspection, preventive maintenance, and testing of equipment.

sections.heading: Annual Review
sections.text:
The Office Manager, in collaboration with the QIP Committee, is responsible for the annual review of the Equipment Management Plans’ objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
The annual review will be compiled at the end of the year based on information from a variety of sources including, but not limited to incident reports equipment failure and user errors, product safety recall notices; staff orientation and training; QIP Committee minutes; performance monitoring activities; and other summaries of activities, including the findings of regulatory agencies.
The annual review will be presented to the Medical Director and Governing Body in a narrative report that covers the Equipment Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.RISK ASSESSMENT FOR EQUIPMENT

sections.heading: MANAGEMENT PROGRAM CRITERIA
sections.text:
Written evaluation criteria to assess patient risk are applied to all equipment based on type to determine whether the equipment should be included in the equipment management program.  The primary categories for assessment are:
1) Equipment function    2) Physical risks associated with equipment    3) Equipment maintenance requirements
4) Equipment incidents and inherent risks.
Equipment Functions
Therapeutic
Life support
Surgical
Diagnostic
Surgical monitoring
Additional physiological monitoring and diagnostic
Analytical
Analytical laboratory
Laboratory accessories
Computer and related
Miscellaneous
Patient related and other
Non-patient related
Physical Risk
Patient death
Patient or operator injury
Inappropriate therapy or misdiagnosis
No significant risks
Maintenance Requirement
Extensive
Above average
Average
Below Average
Minimal
Equipment Incidents and Inherent Risks
Very high inherent risks
High inherent risks
Average inherent risks
Minimal risk
No significant risks

sections.heading: EQUIPMENT MANAGEMENT ASSESSMENT SCORE (EMAS)
sections.text:
The Equipment Management Assessment Score (EMAS) is determined by summing the individ­ual scores for each of the four characteristics according to the following:
EMAS = Equipment Functions + Physical Risk + Maintenance Requirement + Equipment Incident/Inherent Risks

sections.heading: EQUIPMENT MANAGEMENT PRIORITY CATEGORIES
sections.text: Using the EMAS, clinical equipment is categorized into the following categories for testing and additional management actions:

sections.heading: Critical
sections.text:
Equipment with an EMAS from 22 to 25 points, inclusive.  This equipment is given the highest priority for testing,
calibration, and repair.  Equipment in this category must have its preventive maintenance completed no later than one month after its scheduled date.

sections.heading: High Priority
sections.text:
Equipment with an EMAS from 19 to 21 points, inclusive.  Every effort will be made to test, calibrate, and repair this equipment, but only after critical priority equip­ment requirements have been complete.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Equipment Management Plan

sections.heading: GUIDELINES
sections.text: Surgical procedures are performed per the individual surgeon’s preference.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Fat Grafting only performed for autologous use (same patient to same patient)
All Fat Grating procedures involve same methods of fat preparation which could be a minimum of gravity separation.
Other methods may include autologous lipocyte micronized injection.

sections.heading: BASIC PRINCIPLES
sections.text:
Use of anatomic cannula such as a Tulip, or equivalent to harvest fat.
Fat is then micronized and then prepared with platelet rich plasma.
Fat deposited in appropriate planes into selected patient site that has been previously sterility prepped.
Small aliquots of fat are deposited until the desired affect is obtained.
All RNs and Physicians are trained in process of preparing fat for grafting.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Fat Grafting
