Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Faxing Private Health Information
POLICY:  Personnel of The Ferguson Clinic will protect the confidentiality of Protected Health Information (PHI) when transmitting or receiving it by facsimile (fax).
PURPOSE:  Fax machines provide a useful mechanism for rapidly and cost-effectively conveying information and documents within the organization and to outside entities with which The Ferguson Clinic does business. Nonetheless, the transmission of PHI by fax poses significant privacy risks associated with misdirected faxes and the delivery to or receipt of faxes in unsecured locations. The purpose of this policy is to describe the procedures that should be used to help to preserve the privacy and security of PHI transmitted to or from The Ferguson Clinic by fax.

sections.heading: Sending Faxes
sections.text:
Personnel will transmit PHI by fax only when the transmission is time-sensitive and delivery by regular mail will not meet the reasonable needs of the sender or recipient.
Personnel will take reasonable steps to ensure that a fax transmission is sent to and received by the intended recipient. When the fax transmission includes PHI, “reasonable steps” include, but are not limited to, the following:
Personnel will confirm with the intended recipient that the receiving fax machine is in a secure area or that the intended recipient is waiting by the fax machine to receive the transmission.
Fax machines will be pre-programmed with the fax numbers of those recipients to whom PHI is frequently sent so errors associated with misdialing can be minimized or avoided. Pre-programmed fax numbers will be tested frequently to confirm they are still valid.
When a fax number is entered manually (because it is not one of the pre-programmed numbers) any personnel entering the number will visually check the recipient’s fax number on the fax machine prior to starting the transmission.
Personnel will use The Ferguson Clinic standard fax cover sheet that contains the following PHI statement:
This facsimile is intended only for the use of the named addressee and may contain information that is confidential or privileged. If you are not the intended recipient, or you are not the employee responsible for delivering the facsimile for the intended recipient, you are hereby notified that any dissemination, distribution or copying of this facsimile is strictly prohibited. If you have received this facsimile in error, please notify the sender immediately.
The name, business affiliation, telephone number and fax number of the intended recipient as well as the number of pages contained in the transmission will also appear on the cover sheet.
Fax confirmation sheets will be checked immediately or as soon as possible after the fax has been transmitted, to confirm the material was faxed to the intended fax number. If the intended recipient notifies the sender that the fax was not received, the sender will use best efforts to determine whether the fax was inadvertently transmitted to another fax number by checking the fax confirmation sheet and/or the fax machine’s internal logging system.
If any personnel become aware that a fax was sent to the wrong fax number, the personnel will immediately attempt to contact the recipient by fax or telephone and request that the faxed documents, and any copies of them, be immediately returned to The Ferguson Clinic or destroyed. The personnel’s supervisor or the Privacy Officer will also be notified of the misdirected fax.
Those recipients who regularly receive PHI via fax will be periodically reminded to notify The Ferguson Clinic of any change to the recipient’s fax number.
Fax confirmation sheets will be attached to and maintained with all faxed materials.
Sensitive PHI (such as HIV/AIDS results or status or substance abuse and mental health treatment records) should never be sent by fax.
When faxing PHI, all personnel will comply with all other The Ferguson Clinic privacy policies.

sections.heading: Receiving Faxes
sections.text:
Personnel who are intended recipients of faxes that contain PHI will take reasonable steps to minimize the possibility those faxes are viewed or received by someone else. These “reasonable steps” include, but are not limited to, the following:
Fax machines that receive faxes that include PHI will be in Secure Areas. If any personnel receive a fax containing PHI on a fax machine that is not in a Secure Area, the recipient of the fax will promptly advise the sender that the receiving fax machine should not be used for the transmission of such information.
Fax machines will be checked on a regular basis to minimize the amount of time incoming faxes that contain PHI are left on the machines. Personnel who monitor the fax machines, or personnel who sees such a fax on the machine, will promptly remove incoming faxes and deliver them to the proper person.
If personnel receive a fax addressed to someone other than the personnel and the person to whom the fax is addressed is someone at The Ferguson Clinic, the personnel will promptly notify the individual to whom the fax was addressed and deliver or decide to deliver the misdirected fax as directed by the intended recipient.
If any personnel receive a fax addressed to someone other than the personnel and the person to whom the fax is addressed is NOT affiliated with The Ferguson Clinic the personnel will promptly notify the sender and destroy or return the faxed material as directed by the sender.
Personnel who routinely receive faxes containing PHI from other individuals or organizations (either internal or external sources) will promptly advise those regular senders of any changes to the organization’s fax number.
Personnel who receive faxes that contain Sensitive PHI (such as HIV/AIDS results or status or substance abuse and mental health treatment records) will promptly advise the senders of such faxes that it is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic not to accept transmissions of sensitive PHI by fax.

sections.heading: Enforcement
sections.text: Personnel who do not comply with this policy will be subject to disciplinary action. Depending on the facts and circumstances of each case, and in accordance with any applicable corrective action policy, The Ferguson Clinic may reprimand, suspend, dismiss, or refer for criminal prosecution any personnel who fail to comply with this policy.

sections.heading: Definitions
sections.text:
Protected Health Information (PHI) means information that relates to the past, present or future physical or mental health or condition of an individual, the provision of health care to an individual or the past, present or future payment for the provision of health care to an individual and identifies or could reasonably be used to identify the individual.
Sensitive Protected Health Information (Sensitive PHI) means Protected Health Information that pertains to (i) an individual’s HIV status or treatment of an individual for an HIV-related illness or AIDS, (ii) an individual’s substance abuse condition or the treatment of an individual for a substance abuse disorder or (iii) an individual’s mental health condition or treatment of an individual for mental illness.
Secure Area means a location that is not accessible to the public.
RESPONSIBILITY:         Privacy Officer
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Faxing Private Health Information

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to take every precaution to minimize the potential of fires in the facility.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
A fire is produced when fuel (oil, paper, wood) combines with oxygen and heat.
Heat can be generated by electrical current.
Another common fire hazard is flammable liquids, such as alcohol, petroleum, distillates, methyl methacrylate, etc.
The most important concern is that vapors become flammable when they mix with air and encounter an ignition source.
Flammable vapors spread quickly.
If the air is warm and still, flammable vapors spread even more quickly.
Mixing of incompatible chemicals may cause a fire or an explosion.
The reactivity section of the safety data sheet (SDS) provides information specific to each product.
When using chemical products, be sure to know their ingredients.
Do not use flammables near heat or fire.
Use adequate ventilation with flammables.
Do not smoke around flammable liquids.
Do not use sparking tools around flammables.
Flammables should be stored away from all ignition sources.
Flammables should be stored in approved, tight, metal containers.
Remove clothing immediately if it encounters a flammable liquid.
Clean all spills immediately.
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Fire Safety

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Patient nutrition and staff consumption of food and drink will be in accordance with nationally recognized guidelines and OSHA regulations.

sections.heading: PATIENTS
sections.text:
Sips of water or juice may be given using manufactured sealed water bottles or juice bottles.
No nausea or vomiting will be present when patient is discharged. Any nourishment offered to patients are “individual serving” portions, stored according to manufacturer instructions and disposed of on discharge

sections.heading: CLINIC STAFF
sections.text:
OSHA regulations prohibit the consumption of food and drink in areas at moderate or high risk of exposure to bloodborne pathogens and/or hazardous chemicals.

sections.heading: DEFINTION
sections.text: General Anesthesia is a drug-induced loss of consciousness during which patients are not arousable, even by painful stimulation. The ability to independently maintain ventilatory function is often impaired. Patients often require assistance in maintaining a patent airway, and positive pressure ventilation may be required because of depressed spontaneous ventilation or drug-induced depression of neuromuscular function. Cardiovascular function may be impaired.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure when administering general anesthesia that they meet the guidelines set forth by the American Society of Anesthesia providers (ASA) to ensure the safety of each patient.

sections.heading: SCOPE OF SERVICE
sections.text:
Unrestricted general anesthesia shall only be administered by anesthesia professionals within their scope of practice (anesthesia providers, certified registered nurse anesthetists and anesthesia provider assistants). If the patient loses consciousness and the ability to respond purposefully, the anesthesia care is a general anesthetic, irrespective of whether airway instrumentation is required.
Of special note, for purposes of this document the following definitions are relevant:
Anesthesia Professional: An anesthesia provider, or certified registered nurse anesthetist (CRNA).
Non-anesthesia provider Sedation Practitioner: A licensed physician (allopathic or osteopathic); or dentist, oral surgeon, or podiatrist who is qualified to administer anesthesia under State law; who has not completed postgraduate training in anesthesiology but is specifically trained to administer personally or to supervise the administration of deep sedation.
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: General Anesthesia

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to provide a process whereby a patient/client can share their complaints and/or grievances without concern of reprisal. All grievances will be responded to within 14 days of receipt.

sections.heading: PATIENT GRIEVANCE GUIDELINES
sections.text:
A grievance is a request for an investigation of a complaint about a possible risk to the health, safety, or well-being of a patient; or a situation where the patient is unnecessarily at high risk.
The situation, event, or condition involves a person receiving service at The Ferguson Clinic.
The grievance/complaint is to provide an opportunity for discussion and possible resolution of problem(s) between patients, patient’s parents and/or legal guardians and providers of care.
If you, the grievant, have a complaint/grievance regarding The Ferguson Clinic services, you may exercise your right through the complaint/grievance process.
The purpose of the grievance/complaint is to address concerns alleging that The Ferguson Clinic services were not provided or that they did not meet recognized levels of care or was treated in an unprofessional manner by The Ferguson Clinic personnel.

sections.heading: PATIENT ROLE AND RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
Carefully review The Ferguson Clinic statement of Patient Rights and Responsibilities
Make every attempt to work out the concern informally with facility staff
Understand and try to use the facility complaint/grievance process first
May follow-up in writing to confirm a complaint/grievance that was submitted via phone or in person; however, a written confirmation is not required
May designate, in writing, another individual to act on his/her behalf
May talk to the Office Manager for assistance.
May withdraw a complaint/grievance at any time.
Read carefully what The Ferguson Clinic can and cannot do through the complaint/grievance process.
Save copy of complaint/grievance forms filed
Keep communication open between patients and The Ferguson Clinic staff.
Help patients feel comfortable taking their concerns to an appropriate person without fear of mistreatment or retaliation.
Facilitate a resolution of the concern as quickly as possible
Assist in the handling of the complaint/grievance by acting as expert investigator, facilitator, referral agent, coordinator and/or counselor and educator. To resolve a complaint/grievance, The Ferguson Clinic may gather information by telephone, electronic medical records review, and/or interviews with involved parties.
Immediately start an investigation upon receipt of a complaint.
Mail the grievance packet, which includes this complaint/grievance guideline, the complaint/grievance form, patient rights and responsibilities and complaint/grievance timetable.
Keep patient identity confidential throughout the process, unless otherwise specifically authorized. If the complaint/grievance cannot be pursued without disclosing the patient’s or grievant’s identity, he/she is notified.
If disclosure is not allowed, he/she will be advised in writing that The Ferguson Clinic will not be able to continue the process, and other alternatives will be provided.
Notify grievant that complaint/grievance can be made anonymously but The Ferguson Clinic will be unable to report back to them unless contact information is provided.
Grievant will also be informed that the staff involved may be able to identify the grievant even when anonymity is maintained.
A representative of the Quality Improvement Committee (QIC) will contact the grievant upon receipt/review of the complaint/grievance for further information.
Notify the grievant when the complaint/grievance investigation has been completed, and when it will be referred to the Governing Body for consideration.
Respond in writing if we determine that the problem is not a The Ferguson Clinic issue or more appropriately handled by another agency.
Send the grievant a final report. The report will be of a general nature and will not detail all the specifics of the investigation. The report will not identify a practitioner, name of another patient, and/or individual without his/her permission. It will indicate if the complaint/grievance was resolved or if any recommendations were made to the facility.
The report will contain further options if the grievant is not satisfied with the results of the process.
Notify the grievant that the confidential information in the complaint/grievance response report pertaining to the grievant may be disclosed by the grievant in any context, including litigation, provided no other patient or provider is identified.
Maintain the confidentiality of all correspondence obtained for the investigation.

sections.heading: What the Ferguson Clinic CAN and CANNOT DO
sections.text:
We CAN
Educate the staff and/or you about issues related to the complaint/grievance
Advocate for the rights of all patients
We CANNOT
Override State or Federal licensing/certification requirements
Assist with legal action taken against our facilities or staff member

sections.heading: COMPLAINT/GRIEVANCE TIME FRAMES
sections.text:
Acknowledgement of Complaint/Grievance
Send a written acknowledgement, within (five) 5 business days that the grievance has been received, stating the concerns/complaint.
Investigation, Review, and Initial Resolution
Advise grievant that The Ferguson Clinic has up to fifty (50) calendar days for intake, investigation/review, and resolution of complaint/grievance.
Health Care Professional Comment/Response Period
Advise the grievant that once the review is completed the Quality Improvement
Committee is required to advise the health care professional involved and/or personnel of the determination and offer them an opportunity to comment prior to the release of the final response to the grievant. Fourteen (14) days are allowed for the submission of comments.
Final Report/Letter Due to Grievant
Final report due to grievant no later than (five) 5 business days after the health care professionals and/or personnel’s submission of comments, if any.
Total Days to Complete Process
Complete the complaint/grievance process within ninety (90) days of receipt of the
complaint/grievance.
Should you have any questions regarding the grievance process, please contact us at: 808.521.1999

sections.heading: OBJECTIVE
sections.text: To avoid use of contaminated materials.  All personnel involved in the tasks of cleaning, packaging, sterilization or disinfection, and handling and storage share responsibility and accountability for the goal of freedom from infection.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
All sterilized packs are labeled with contents before being placed in the sterilizer.
All sterilized packs are dated when removed from the sterilizer.

sections.heading: The safe storage of sterile items depends on four factors
sections.text:
Whether or not the item was sterile initially.
The type of wrapper or barrier used.
The amount and type of handling the product receives after sterilization.
The actual conditions of storage.
A sterile package should be handled as few times as necessary between sterilization and use.
Everyone handling or transporting the package must be aware of any condition that might lead to contamination.
If such a condition is encountered the package should be regarded as unsterile and not used until rewrapped and re-sterilized.
When locks on instrument trays are found broken, the tray should be considered unsterile and not used until re-sterilized.
Storage of sterile items that will be longer than one or two days should be in closed cabinets to minimize dust collection.  (Otherwise, dust from the surface of the package can contaminate the package contents during the opening.)
Sterile items should be stored separately from clean items and away from soiled or used items.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Guidelines for Gowning and Gloving

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Guidelines for Surgical Hand Scrubbing
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Guidelines for Surgical Hand Scrubbing

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to provide guidelines for Surgical Hand Scrubbing. One of the following accepted forms of surgical hand scrub must be followed:
Anatomical timed scrub.
Counted stroke method.
Alcohol Scrub Lotion (Avaguard).

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To remove debris and transient microorganisms.
To reduce the resident microbial count to a minimum.
To inhibit rapid rebound growth of microorganisms.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
All personnel should be in surgical attire before beginning the Surgical Hand Scrub.
Personnel should be in surgical scrubs, cap, shoe covers and face mask.
All rings and watches and bracelets should be removed.
Fingernails should be free of polish and should not extend past the fingertips.
Acrylic nails and extensions are not to be worn in the Operating Room.
Cuticles, hands, and forearms should be free of open lesions and breaks in skin integrity.
An antimicrobial Surgical Hand Scrub will be used.
If the approved scrub agent cannot be used due to skin sensitivity, an alternate surgical scrub agent will be provided.
If this agent cannot be used due to skin sensitivity, a liquid non-medical soap scrub followed by an application of an alcohol-based hand cleanser should be used for five minutes in multiple applications, rubbing until dry with each application; and
Hand scrub agents are stored in clean, closed packages.
Disposable hand scrub packages are thrown out when finished

sections.heading: Guidelines for the Surgical Hand Scrub procedure are as follows
sections.text:
Moisten hands and forearms thoroughly with the approved surgical scrub agent to a point two inches above the elbow.
Rinse hands and arms thoroughly, keeping the hands up and allowing the water to drip from the elbow.
Select a pre-packaged sterile brush, open the package, remove the brush, and nail cleaner and discard the package.
Clean all nails and subungual spaces under running water, holding the brush in the palm of one hand.
Discard the package.

sections.heading: Follow either the Counted Brush Stroke or Anatomical Timed Scrub
Anatomical Brush
sections.text:
One and one-half minute to each hand
One (1) minute to each arm

sections.heading: Counted Brush Stroke
sections.text:
Thirty (30) strokes to nails
Twenty (20) strokes each to surface at the fingers, hand, and arms
Scrub the nails, starting at the fingertips, holding the nails perpendicular to the brush.  Avoid spraying soap and water on the scrub attire.
Scrub vigorously all four planes of each finger, spreading fingers and thumb apart, including the outer aspects below the thumb and little finger.
Scrub the palm and back of the hand using a circular motion.
Divide the area from the hand to two inches above the elbow into three sections - lower, middle, and upper.
Scrub each section on all four planes using a circular motion.
Start with the lower section first and continue until you have completed scrubbing two (2) inches above the elbow.
Do not rinse soap from hands and arms.
Add soap and water as necessary to maintain lather.
Transfer the brush to the scrubbed hand and follow the same procedure on the other hand and arm giving careful attention to each area.
Hands should be held higher than the elbows and away from the surgical attire.
Rinse the fingers, hands, and arms thoroughly to two inches above the elbow.
Discard the brush or sponge in the sink or appropriate container.
Hold hands and arms up and out from scrub clothes and proceed into the operating room.

sections.heading: Avaguard
sections.text:
Avaguard antiseptic hand prep is designed to replace water-aided antimicrobial hand scrub brushes and antimicrobial soaps. The scrubless, waterless way to prepare hands for surgery, it is fast-acting and effective, providing proven, persistent antimicrobial protection for up to six hours without water or scrubbing.
Avagard hand antiseptic product has obtained FDA approval, which includes a complete review by FDA of all safety and efficacy data.
Avaguard antiseptic hand prep provides rapid antimicrobial kill of a broad spectrum of microorganisms, including antibiotic-resistant strains, with greater than 99% microbial kill in 15 seconds {1} (in vitro*). In human testing set forth by the FDA, Avagard antiseptic hand prep surpasses FDA criteria for surgical scrub formulations at all measured time points.
Avaguard antiseptic hand prep is also uniquely formulated to maintain and protect the condition of your skin. An advanced, emollient-rich lotion base helps prevent dry, cracked hands, while protecting and maintaining skin barrier integrity.

sections.heading: Avaguard Guidelines
sections.text:
Clean under nails with a 3M™ Avagard™ Nail Cleaner. No pre-scrub required.
Dispense one pump (2 ml) into the palm of one hand. Dip fingertips of the opposite hand into the hand prep and work under fingernails. Spread remaining hand prep over the hand and up to just above the elbow.
Dispense one pump (2 ml) and repeat procedure with opposite hand.
Dispense final pump (2 ml) of hand prep into either hand, reapply to all aspects of both hands up to the wrists. Allow to dry. Do not use towels!

sections.heading: Tips to Remember
sections.text:
Do not use water.
Be sure hands are dry prior to using Avaguard antiseptic hand prep.
Per OSHA Blood borne Pathogen Rule, wash hands with soap and water after the surgical procedure; and
Questions? Call the 3M Customer Helpline at 1-800-228-3957
Back through the door to enter the operating room, keeping arms above the waist and away from the body.  Do not touch the door or anything else with clean hands and arms.
Step away from the sterile gown package and bend forward slightly from the waist.
Unfold the towel full length, hold the top half of the towel securely and blot dry the opposite fingers and hands.  Using a rotating motion, move up the arm to the elbow.  Do not retrace any area.
Grasp the lower portion of the towel with the dried hand and blot dry the moist hand and arm with the same rotating motion.  Discard the towel.

sections.heading: The Avaguard guidelines may be utilized for subsequent scrubs
sections.text:
Prior to each case.
In cases of gown contamination where there would be a need to change gown and gloves (i.e., waiting for the surgeon or contamination during a procedure).
The Infection Control and Prevention Officer is responsible for the training of staff for orientation and annual review.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Guidelines for Surgical Hand Scrubbing

file: Handling and Storage of Sterile Supplies.idml
title: Handling and Storage of Sterile Supplies

sections.heading: OBJECTIVE
sections.text: To avoid use of contaminated materials.  All personnel involved in the tasks of cleaning, packaging, sterilization or disinfection, and handling and storage share responsibility and accountability for the goal of freedom from infection.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
All sterilized packs are labeled with contents before being placed in the sterilizer.
All sterilized packs are dated when removed from the sterilizer.

sections.heading: The safe storage of sterile items depends on four factors
sections.text:
Whether or not the item was sterile initially.
The type of wrapper or barrier used.
The amount and type of handling the product receives after sterilization.
The actual conditions of storage.
A sterile package should be handled as few times as necessary between sterilization and use.
Everyone handling or transporting the package must be aware of any condition that might lead to contamination.
If such a condition is encountered the package should be regarded as unsterile and not used until rewrapped and re-sterilized.
When locks on instrument trays are found broken, the tray should be considered unsterile and not used until re-sterilized.
Storage of sterile items that will be longer than one or two days should be in closed cabinets to minimize dust collection.  (Otherwise, dust from the surface of the package can contaminate the package contents during the opening.)
Sterile items should be stored separately from clean items and away from soiled or used items.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Handling and Storage of Sterile Supplies

Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Hazard Classes and Storage

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to properly classify and store all hazardous materials.

sections.heading: FORMS TO BE USED
sections.text:
Safety Data Sheets
Hazardous Product and Chemical Inventory

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
The Safety Officer will ensure that all hazardous materials are properly classified according to the following taxonomies.
Biohazard	Risk to human life
Carcinogen	A chemical that can cause cancer in humans or animals
Compressed Gas: A gas or gases in a container with an absolute pressure exceeding 40 psi at
70°F, or gases in a container exceeding 104 psi at 130°F
Corrosive 	A material with the characteristic of causing irreversible harm to human skin or 				steel, by contact (many acids are classified as corrosive)
Flammable	Any liquid that has a flash point of 100°F or below, and any solid that can sustain 				fire and ignitereadily
Non-Hazardous: A chemical that is neither a physical nor health hazard
Oxidizer	A substance that yields oxygen readily to stimulate the combustion of an organic 				materials
Poison, Moderate: A substance that has an inherent tendency to be harmful
Poison, Severe: A substance that causes critical reaction to health
Radioactive	The phenomenon exhibited by certain elements spontaneously emitting radiation 			because of changes in the nuclei of atoms of the elements
Water Reactive: A chemical that can react with water to release a gas that is flammable or 				presents a health hazard

sections.heading: Storage
sections.text:
Chemicals/hazards should be stored according to their potential hazard.  This information can be found on the SDS of each product.
Water reactive chemicals require dry storage.
Strong oxidizers must be separated from other chemicals.
Compressed gas cylinders must be secured and properly supported.
Flammables require special storage.

file: Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan.idml
title: Hazardous Material and Waste Management Plan

sections.text:
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of the Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan is to describe how hazardous materials and waste are to be handled, stored, used, and disposed of to minimize the threat of exposure to staff, patients, and visitors.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of the The Ferguson Clinic to establish and maintain a plan to safely control the disposal of all hazardous materials waste.

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text: The Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan apply to all staff, patients, and visitors of the The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To select, handle, store, use, and dispose of hazardous materials and wastes from receipt or generation through use or final disposal.
To establish written criteria, in accordance with applicable law and regulation, to identify, evaluate, and inventory hazardous materials and wastes used or generated.
To manage chemical wastes, and regulated medical or infectious wastes, including sharps
To monitor and dispose of hazardous gases and vapors.
To provide adequate and appropriate space and equipment for safe handling and storage of hazardous materials and wastes.
To report and investigate all hazardous material or waste spills, exposures, and other incidents.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text: The Safety Officer are responsible for ensuring that the objectives of the Hazardous Materials and Waste Program are met.

sections.heading: PROCESSES OF THE HAZARDOUS WASTE MANAGEMENT PROGRAM
sections.text:
Selecting, handling, storing, using and disposal of hazardous waste
Infectious waste containing significant amounts of blood or other potentially infectious material shall be separated from general waste to assure that these wastes receive appropriate handling and treatment. It is segregated from general waste at the point at which the material becomes a waste.
Infectious medical waste must be discarded directly into containers or plastic bags that are marked with the universal biological symbol or placed in red or orange colored plastic bags that are clearly identifiable and distinguishable from general waste.
Sharps must be placed directly into impervious, rigid, and puncture-resistant containers to eliminate the hazard of physical injury.
Sharps containers must be marked with the universal biohazard symbol and sealed before handling.
Sharps containers are disposed of when three-quarters (75-80%) full.
Hazardous waste must be bagged, labeled, and sealed prior to storage.
The biohazard container is located in the dirty room. It is labeled with the Biohazard label.
When infectious waste is transported off-site for treatment, all materials must be placed within rigid or semi-rigid containers to maintain the integrity of the packaging and prevent spillage. Infectious waste must not be compacted prior to treatment.
An infectious waste handler, approved by the State of Hawaii to dispose of all infectious waste materials, picks up the waste and provides a receipt that identifies when the materials were picked up and incinerated.
All containers that hold saturated blood or other potentially infectious waste (OPIM) will be labeled with the universal biohazard label and posted where appropriate (refrigerators, waste containers, etc.  Written criteria to inventory and evaluate hazardous materials used or generated
A Safety Data Sheet will be kept within the facility of each chemical and cleaning solution used within the facility.
Maintenance of the Safety Data Sheets is the responsibility of the Safety Officer.
Blood and OPIM:
Blood and blood products may be discharged directly to the sanitary sewer.  Feces, urine, and other body fluids may be also discharged into the sanitary sewer.
This facility contracts with Hawaii Bio-waste Systems, Inc. to dispose of all infectious waste materials.
Management of chemical waste, radioactive waste and regulated medical waste or infectious
waste, including sharps
All policies and procedures relating to chemical and physical hazards shall be reviewed by the Office Manager and Medical Director annually.
All sharps, hypodermic needles, syringes, suture needles, knife blades, and opened glass ampoules of medicine will be disposed of in plastic containers.
All radioactive materials are disposed of in accordance with the Nuclear Regulatory Commission regulations.
Access to hazardous, medical, reactive, or waste holding areas is limited to authorized staff.
Staff wears appropriate personal protective equipment when handling hazardous materials and waste.
Staff utilizes appropriate shielding devices to protect patients and themselves against radioactive exposure.
Use and storage of gasoline within the facility are prohibited.
Monitoring and disposal of hazardous gases and vapors
All compressed gas cylinders will be stored on carts or chained to the wall and protected from extreme heat and cold.
Only authorized individuals will have access to storage areas, and they will be trained in the proper handling, use, and transport of cylinders and valve protection caps.
Handling and Storage of Hazardous Materials and Waste
Staff is to wear personal protective equipment when handling hazardous materials and waste.
Flammable materials such as paints, solvents, adhesives, and other chemicals necessary for the maintenance of the physical plant will be disposed of in accordance with applicable safety and environmental regulations.
Flammable materials that ignite easily under normal conditions will be stored in a cool, dry, well-ventilated storage space.
Flammable materials will be kept in an area separate from oxidizing agents (material susceptible to spontaneous heating, explosives, etc.).
A fire extinguisher will be kept in the area.
Oxidizers will not be stored close to liquids of low flash point.
Acids and acid fume-sensitive materials will be stored in a cool, dry, well-ventilated area (preferably wooden).
Toxic materials or materials that can decompose into toxic components are kept in a cool well-ventilated place that protects them from the sun, moisture, acids, fumes, and heat.
Corrosive materials are isolated from other materials and stored in cool, well-ventilated areas.
Reporting and Investigating
All incidents related to chemical spills, inappropriate handling of hazardous materials and wastes are documented on an Incident Report form.
All employee exposures to hazardous materials and wastes are reported as an employee injury, and the Medical Director and/or Safety Officer is to be notified.
The Office Manager or Safety Officer investigates and reports all hazardous materials and waste spills to the QIP Committee.
Orientation and Training
The Safety Officer or designee provides for continuing education on the following:
Precautions for selecting, handling, storing, using, and disposing of hazardous materials and wastes.
Emergency procedures for hazardous materials and waste spills or exposure.
Health hazards of mishandling hazardous materials.
Reporting procedures for hazardous materials and wastes incidents, including spills or exposures, to the Medical Director.

sections.heading: Staff is Trained
sections.text:
During orientation.
Whenever a new chemical is introduced into the workplace.
Whenever infectious waste management practices change.
An employee does not follow appropriate procedures.
Training can be met by classroom activities, one-on-one discussions or through the completion of a self-study packet on hazardous materials and waste management issues.
All training is documented in employee personnel files.

sections.heading: Performance Monitoring
sections.text:
The Office Manager is responsible for monitoring performance standards.
Summaries of findings and recommendations, based on trends, performance measures, and performance improvement activities will be documented quarterly by the QIP Committee.
Specific information will be communicated to staff when issues or opportunities to reduce the risk of hazardous material and waste exists.
Performance measures of actual or potential risks will relate to one or more of the following:
Staff knowledge and skills.
Level of staff participation.
Monitoring and inspection activities.
Emergency and incident reporting.
Inspection, preventive maintenance, and testing of equipment.

sections.heading: Annual Review
sections.text:
The Office Manager is responsible for the evaluation of the Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Program’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
The annual review will be compiled at the end of the year based on information from a variety of sources including, but not limited to hazardous incidents, chemical spills, staff orientation and training, QIP Committee minutes, and performance monitoring activities.
The annual review will be presented to the Medical Director and Governing Body in a narrative report that covers the Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Hazardous Materials and Waste Management Plan

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Impaired Physician or Allied Health Care Professional
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Impaired Physician or Allied Health Care Professional
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Impaired Physician or Allied Health Care Professional

sections.heading: AUDIENCE
sections.text: This policy applies to all physicians employed, appointed, affiliated or owners of The Ferguson Clinic. Physicians and Allied Health Care Professionals and Licensed Medical Personnel become a subject to this policy if, and when, there is reason to conclude that the individual is impaired or is exhibiting a behavior pattern suggestive of impairment. The direct observation of chemical substance abuse or observations of aberrations in performance and/or behavior may be cause for this conclusion.

sections.heading: DEFINITIONS
sections.text:
The following are definitions, explanations, qualifications, or stipulations regarding certain terms used in this policy.
Chemical Substance Abuse
Is the personal use of any chemical substance that is specifically proscribed by law or by regulation pursuant to legal authority (e.g., Schedule I drugs); the personal misuse of any legally controlled substance; or the personal misuse of any normally legal chemical substance (e.g., alcohol) in a manner that produces the likelihood of the development of impairment.
Chemical Substance Abuse
Is the self-administration of any chemical substance for any reason other than its intended use?
Emergency
Is the one in which there may be a potential adverse effect on a Ferguson Clinic patient or employee.
Impairment by Substance Abuse
Refers to any condition, resulting from substance abuse that interferes with the individual’s ability to function at work as normally expected.
Impairment for Other Reasons
Refers to other categories of impairment including major debilitating illnesses, depression, dementia, or other psychopathology that may interfere with the individual’s ability to function at work as normally expected.
Physician
As used throughout this Policy includes medical doctors and allied health care professionals.
Major symptoms of alcohol and drug abuse may include declining work performances as manifested by unavailability, missed appointments, lapses in judgment, incomplete medical records, poorly communicated nocturnal phone orders, mood swings, unexplained absences, embarrassing behavior, signs of intoxication or self-medication, and/or withdrawal from professional activities. Family problems and change in character or personality are further accompaniments of a substance abuse disorder.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
It is the responsibility of all employees to report observations leading to the above conclusions to
the Governing Body.

sections.heading: ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
Governing Body - Owners
Upon receiving information from the physician’s or allied health care professional co-workers,
employees, patients, or visitors, or upon making personal observations regarding the physician’s
fitness for duty, the Governing Body has the responsibility to act to ensure the safety of all patients.

sections.heading: This may include the following
sections.text:
Verify the accuracy of the observations suggesting impairment.
Explain to the individual in question that these observations have been made.
Report to the Governing Body any physician’s or allied health care professional’s or licensed medical personnel conduct that requires that immediate action be taken to protect the life of any patient or to reduce the substantial likelihood if immediate injury or damage to the health and safety of the patient, employee, or other persons in the facility.
Arrange for any physician or allied health professional deemed to be impaired by drugs or alcohol to be escorted to the Emergency Room to be seen for a medical evaluation, including a request for laboratory testing for the presence illegal drugs or alcohol in body fluids, in addition to mandatory psychiatric evaluation by the on-call staff psychiatrist. Any testing of drugs or alcohol is voluntary.
Arrange for immediate medical leave for evaluation and treatment.
Immediately suspend all or any portion of the clinical privileges.
Notify the affected physician or allied health care professional of the medical leave or suspension, with confirmation by certified mail, return receipt requested.

sections.heading: TESTING GUIDELINES
sections.text:
Any physician or allied health professional being evaluated for a reported condition or impairment may be asked to undergo laboratory testing for the presence of illegal drugs or alcohol in body fluids or breath as a part of the medical evaluation for fitness of duty.
If the physician or allied health professional refuses, this information will be communicated immediately to the Governing Body.
A screening test positive for chemical substances will be confirmed by the best currently available
laboratory techniques. If the accuracy of a positive confirmatory test is disputed by the individual, the confirmatory test on a different aliquot of the same sample will be repeated in a qualified laboratory which may be chosen by the individual.
If the test result is not disputed or if the additional confirmatory test is positive, the result will be taken as definitive evidence of chemical substance abuse in the case of illegal chemical substance. The entirety of the available evidence will be used to determine the presence or absence of chemical substance abuse if the substance involved is one for which a bona fide medical indication exists.
The cost of the chemical abuse testing undertaken during investigation for substance abuse and/or impairment will be borne by The Ferguson Clinic.
The cost of chemical substance testing performed as part of a treatment program, including maintenance monitoring, will be part of the cost of the program and will be the responsibility of the individual.
Any physician or allied health care professional being evaluated for a reported condition of
impairment other than from chemical or substance abuse may be asked to undergo physical or psychiatric evaluation as a part of the medical evaluation for fitness for duty.
If the physician, allied health professional or licensed medical professional refuses, this information will be communicated to the Governing Body.
The testing required will be specifically tailored to each case, and the information sought will be specified.
The cost of such testing undertaken during investigation for other than substance or chemical abuse impairment will be borne by physician, allied health care professional or licensed medical professional.
The cost of future testing or treatment involved with the rehabilitation of an impaired physician, allied health care professional or licensed health care professional will be borne by the individual.

sections.heading: DOCUMENTATION
sections.text:
Documentation of the report of impairment of behavior suggestive of impairment, medical and
psychiatric evaluation reports, and other correspondence pertaining to these events and the
treatment and rehabilitation of any The Ferguson Clinic’s physicians, allied health professionals or
licensed medical professionals and will be treated as confidential.
The contents of the file will be released to The Ferguson Clinic Governing Body only upon written
authorization of the affected physician or allied health professional, except as required by the state
and federal law.
Activity restrictions imposed because of actions under this policy will be communicated to
individuals ONLY on a need-to-know basis. The overriding consideration will be the safety of
patients, employees, or persons at The Ferguson Clinic.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Impaired Physician or Allied Health Care Professional

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Incapacitated Physician and Allied Health Care Professionals
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Incapacitated Physician and Allied Health Care Professionals

sections.heading: AUDIENCE
sections.text: This policy applies to all physicians employed, appointed, affiliated with The Ferguson Clinic’s Physicians, Allied Health Care Professionals and Licensed Medical Professionals become a subject to this policy if, and when, there is reason to conclude that the individual is incapacitated or is exhibiting a behavior pattern suggestive of incapacitation. The direct observation of aberrations in performance and/or behavior may be cause for this conclusion.

sections.heading: DEFINITIONS
sections.text:
The following are definitions, explanations, qualifications, or stipulations regarding certain terms
used in this policy.
Heart Failure
Is a condition in which a problem with the structure or function of the heart impairs its ability to
supply enough blood flow to meet the body’s needs. The phrase is often incorrectly used to
describe other cardiac related illnesses, such as myocardial infarction or cardiac arrest. Heart failure
is often undiagnosed due to lack of a universally agreed definition and challenges in definitive
diagnosis. Treatment commonly consists of lifestyle measures, medications, devices or even
surgery. If left alone the mortality rate is 10%.
Stroke
Sometimes called a cerebrovascular accident (CVA) is the rapidly developing loss of brain function(s) due to disturbance in the blood supply to the brain, caused by a blocked or burst blood vessel. As a result, the affected area of the brain is unable to function, leading to the inability to move one or more limbs on one side of the body, inability to understand or formulate speech, or an inability to see on one side of the visual field. A stroke is a medical emergency and can cause permanent neurological damage, complications and even death.
Cardiac Arrest
Is the cessation of normal circulation of the blood due to failure of the heart to contract effectively, and if this is unexpected can be termed a sudden cardiac arrest or SCA?
A cardiac is different from (but may be caused by) a heart, where blood flow to the muscle of the heart is impaired. Brain injury is likely if cardiac arrest goes untreated for more than five minutes. If a shockable rhythm is not present after CPR and other interventions; clinical death is inevitable.
Respiratory Failure
Is nearly any condition that affects breathing functions or the lungs themselves and can result in the failure of the lungs to function properly? In respiratory failure, the level of oxygen in the blood becomes dangerously low, and/or the level of CO2 becomes dangerously high. If this persists pressure is placed on the heart and pulmonary artery resulting in death.
Emergency
Is the one in which there may be a potential adverse effect on a The Ferguson Clinic’s patient or employee.
Incapacitation for Other Reasons
Refers to other categories of incapacitation including major debilitating illnesses that may interfere with the individual’s ability to function at work as normally expected.
Physician
As used throughout this policy includes medical doctors and allied health care professionals.
Major symptoms of incapacitation include shortness of breath, inability to communicate, unconsciousness and death.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the responsibility of all Ferguson Clinic’s employees to immediately call 911 and communicate to the Medical Director immediately.

sections.heading: ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
Governing Body /Owners
Upon receiving information from the physician’s or allied health care professional, co-workers,
employees, patients, or visitors, or upon making personal observations regarding the incapacitated
physician or allied health care professional. The Governing Body will immediately seek medical
attention for the physician and/or allied health care professional.
Ensure that the patient is safe and if the physician was in the process of a procedure, immediately
communicate to the patient that his/her procedure will either be:
Continued by another physician on staff.
Discontinued and brought back later.
Sent to the hospital for continued treatment.
Report to the Governing Body any physician’s, allied health care professional is or licensed medical
professional’s condition that requires immediate action be taken to protect the life of any patient or to reduce the substantial likelihood if immediate injury or damage to the health and safety of the patient, employee, or other persons in the facility.
Arrange for any physician, allied health professional or licensed medical professional deemed to be incapacitated to the Emergency Room to be seen for a medical evaluation.
Arrange for immediate medical leave for evaluation and treatment.
Immediately reschedule all patients or have another physician on staff do those procedures. However, communication to the patient to get their permission would be necessary to do the latter.

sections.heading: DOCUMENTATION
sections.text:
Documentation of the report of incapacitation including medical reports, and other correspondence pertaining to these events and the treatment and rehabilitation of any The Ferguson Clinic physician, Allied Health Professional or Licensed Medical Professional will be treated as confidential.
The contents of the file will be released to The Ferguson Clinic only upon written
authorization of the physician, allied health professional or licensed medical professional except as required by the state and federal law.
Activity restrictions imposed because of this emergency will be left to the treating physician with a
letter allowing the physician, allied health care professional or licensed medical professional to
return to work and under what conditions.
Under this policy will be communicated to individuals ONLY on a need-to-know basis. The
overriding consideration will be the safety of patients, employees or persons at The Ferguson Clinic and affected physicians, allied health care professionals and licensed medical professionals.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Incapacitated Physician and Allied Health Care Professionals

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Incident Reporting

sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text:
An incident report is a tool that documents any event that may or may not have caused injuries to a person or damage to a company asset. It is used to capture injuries and accidents, near misses, property and equipment damage, health and safety issues, security breaches and workplace misconduct.
An incident report can be used in the investigation and analysis of an event. It includes the root cause and corrective actions to eliminate the risks involved and prevent similar future occurrences.
Incident reports can also be used as safety documents which indicate potential risks and uncontrolled hazards found in the workplace.

sections.heading: An incident report can be used by
sections.text:
An authority to create a report of an incident.
An employee to report an incident he/ she has witnessed.
A member of the practice to raise awareness about an incident that has occurred in the workplace.

sections.heading: Four Types of Events
sections.text:
Near Misses.
Adverse Events.
Sentinel Events.
No Harm Events.

sections.heading: Five Elements of an Incident Report
Accurate
sections.text: All data must be clear and specific. Most inaccuracies are due to typos and simple grammar and spelling errors (e.g., incorrect details of names of people involved, date and time of the incident, contact numbers, etc.). Provide more specific details of what you are referring to and avoid any vague statements that may cause confusion. Lastly, always proofread your report before submission to see errors that you might have overlooked

sections.heading: Factual
sections.text: An incident report should be objective and supported by facts. Avoid including emotional, opinionated, and biased statements in the incident report. It should provide both sides of the story and should not favor one side. However, if there is a need to include statements from witnesses or patients, make sure to quote them

sections.heading: Complete
sections.text: Ensure that all essential questions (what, where, when, why and how) are covered in the incident report. Record not only the people who were injured and what caused the accident to happen, but also include details such as people who witnessed and reported the incident or those who will investigate. Anticipate what other significant details will be needed for any future study and investigation.

sections.heading: Graphic
sections.text: Photos, diagrams, and illustrations should be included as supporting evidence. Take many photos of the injury, damage, and surrounding environment. This supplement the facts stated and provides more clarity to be easily understood by the recipient.

sections.heading: Valid
sections.text:
Upon completion, those who are involved in the incident (e.g., victim, witnesses, manager, reporter, etc.) should sign off to testify and validate all the information that was mentioned in the incident report. This confirms that the incident report is truthful and unquestionable.
The incident report should be submitted to the QI Committee to further study and look for deeper causes. A review shall be completed, not to find fault but to determine the root cause and develop corrective actions to prevent similar incidents from happening. An investigation also helps fulfill regulatory requirements (such as OSHA 300 Forms in the United States) and determines the costs involved with property or equipment damage (if any).

sections.heading: Storing Incident Reports
sections.text:
Incident reports should be properly stored as they are an important record of every organization. Incident Reports will either be scanned and placed on the Ferguson Clinic’s server that is password protected, or in paper form, placed in a file in a locked draw of the Office Manager.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Incident Reporting

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Infection Control Plan
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Infection Control Plan
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Infection Control Plan

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To establish processes by which the practice will address surveillance, prevention, identification control, and reporting of infections
To give direction to practice personnel in the care and services they provide in relation to infection control practices
To educate practice personnel, patients, families/caregivers and others in the prevention and control of infections
To provide for surveillance systems to track the occurrence and transmission of infections
To comply with all applicable state and federal regulations

sections.heading: PRIORITIZED RISKS
sections.text: All patients infected with HIV, HBV, and other blood borne pathogens.

sections.heading: Environment of Care
sections.text: Cleaning, disinfection and sterilization practices, exam room assessment, cleaning of exam room; disinfection and storage of patient supplies; medication preparation/storage.

sections.heading: Operative Procedures
sections.text: Break in aseptic technique; blood spill, disinfection & sterilization practices; use of multi-dose vials, contamination of instruments, post-op wound infections, nosocomial infections.

sections.heading: Patient, Visitor, Employee Population
sections.text: Cold/flu, open wounds, break in skin, and contagious diseases.

sections.heading: STRATEGIES TO MINIMIZE REDUCE OR ELIMINATE PRIORITIZED RISKS
Hand Washing
sections.text:
Hand washing will be performed to prevent cross-contamination between patients and personnel.
Alcohol-based hand cleaner is available in each room.
OSHA officer will perform random assessments of hand washing quarterly.

sections.heading: Needles, Syringes and Sharps
sections.text:
After use, needles and other sharps will be removed in the procedure and operating room and placed into a puncture proof container. Needles must not be bent, broken, or clipped.
Per the State of Hawaii and OSHA rules and regulations, safety needles are to be used for all injections.

sections.heading: Other
sections.text:
Laboratory specimens will be transported in a zip-lock bag or other leak-proof container.
Eating, drinking, smoking, applying make-up or lip-balm or handling contact lenses will be avoided in work areas where there is a reasonable chance of exposure.
Sterile technique will be employed for IV insertion and whenever appropriate to prevent infection.
Multi-use vials will be swabbed with alcohol before and after use and kept until outdated if solution is not cloudy.
Sterile supplies are kept separate from non-sterile supplies.
Patient care items are not placed under sinks (only cleaning supplies).
Oxygen tubing and cannulas will be cleaned and disinfected after each patient use.
Staff is to report any potential risk of safety/infection control to the Infection Control and Prevention Officer.

sections.heading: Personal Protective Equipment
sections.text:
Gloves are to be changed between patient contacts.
Sterile gloves are to be worn for sterile procedures.
Utility gloves, apron, mask, and goggles are to be worn for housekeeping chores involving potential blood contact and for instrument cleaning and decontamination procedures.
Utility gloves may be decontaminated and reused, but will be discarded if they are peeling, cracked, or discolored, of if they have punctures, tears, or other evidence of deterioration.  Gowns:
The use of gowns is required when splashes to the skin and/or clothing is likely. The gowns will be made of or lined with fluid-proof or fluid-resistant material and will protect all areas of exposed skin.
The type and characteristics will depend on the task and degree of exposure anticipated.

sections.heading: Mask/Protective Eyewear
sections.text: Masks, protective eye wear, or face shields are required when contamination of mucosal membranes, eyes, mouth, or nose is possible, such as splashes or aerosolization of material. They are not required for routine care.

sections.heading: Labels
sections.text:
Biohazard labels will be used to prevent accidental injury or illness to personnel exposed to hazardous or potentially hazardous conditions.
Labels will state BIOHAZARD or the hazard symbol.
Labels will be affixed as close as possible to respective hazards.
Labels will be used to identify equipment and containers containing hazardous agents.
If labels are not used, other means will be used, such as red bagging

sections.heading: The following guidelines will be implemented and taught to staff
sections.text:
All equipment, environmental and working surfaces shall be cleaned and decontaminated after contact with blood or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM).
Blood/body fluid spills can be mopped or wiped up with hot soapy water and then disinfected with bleach or hospital disinfectant spray. Disposable gloves must be worn.
An appropriate disinfectant will be used to clean floors, toilet bowls, sinks, countertops, and soiled furniture.
Rooms will be kept well aired to decrease the risk of colds, flu, and other airborne communicable diseases.
Humidifiers and air conditioners can harbor infectious organisms and will be cleaned and serviced regularly.
All bins, pails, cans (e.g., wastebaskets) intended for reuse which have a reasonable likelihood of becoming contaminated with blood and OPIM will be inspected and decontaminated immediately, or as soon as feasible upon visible contamination.
Single Use Disposable Medical Devices will not be reused, except for those not requiring maintenance of sterility.

sections.heading: EDUCATION OF PERSONNEL
sections.text:
Personnel will be provided training on the basics of transmission of pathogens to patients and staff, blood-borne diseases, the use of the Universal Precautions, hand washing, infectious waste management and other infection control procedures when their work activities may result in an exposure to blood, OPIM, or when differentiation between body fluid types is difficult or impossible. Medtrainer/Steris Online or 3M online Infection Control and Prevention training is utilized.
Infection control in-services will be scheduled no less than annually.
Attendance will be mandatory and will be documented.
Records of in-service attendance will be maintained.

sections.heading: MONITORING AND EVALUATION OF INFECTION CONTROL
sections.text:
The Infection Control Plan will be monitored and evaluated by the Quality Improvement
Committee (QIC)
Infection control data will be collected, analyzed, and trended. Information obtained will be given to the Quality Improvement Committee (QIC) and used to improve patient care as well as to improve the practice’s performance in the implementation of its Infection Control Plan.
The Quality Improvement Committee (QIC) will be responsible for the oversight and reporting of the Infection Control Plan to the Physician(s) and the Governing Body and other appropriate authorities.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Infection Control Plan

sections.heading: PROTOCOL
sections.text: For the duration of the Covid-19 pandemic or until further notification, facilities will follow appropriate CDC Guidelines, CMS directives, and the Department of Health guidelines.

sections.heading: GENERAL PROTOCOLS
sections.text:
Patient Appointments
Screening Protocols: All patients will be screened at the time of their appointment, during the reminder phone call, and when they arrive for their appointment per the Patient Screening Policy.  All
COVID-19 Rapid Testing: All patients scheduled for surgery will be tested using the FDA and CDC approved Covid 19 antigen rapid test upon presentation for this surgery.  No patient will be taken to the procedure room until the Covid 19 antigen rapid test is read and is negative.  Only the medical director and a member of the nursing staff will have direct contact with the patient until the Covid 19 antigen rapid test is read and is negative.
Masks: Patients will wear a face mask to their appointment and if one is not available a mask will be provided upon their arrival. A cloth or homemade cloth mask is acceptable for patients and visitors. The day of surgery patients will wear a mask upon presentation to the clinic until induction of anesthesia except during airway evaluation by the surgeon or anesthesia provider. Upon emergence a mask will be placed as soon as airway/oxygenation is deemed stable or during oral intake.
Sanitizing: Hand sanitization procedures is required of all patients and visitors upon entering the facility.
Patients will not come into the office upon their arrival. They are to call the office and the staff member will check them in remotely and a staff member will contact them when it is time for them to come into the facility and directly into the testing or exam area.
Per CMS guidelines, will set limitations on visitation. Limitations will include restricting the number of visitors and or drivers per patient to one and only if the patient needs assistance, or a guardian or parent, to accompany them to the appointment. This includes limiting visitors under the age of 18 to patients only. All other visitors and drivers are to remain outside of the facilities or in a vehicle and wait off-site until contacted at the conclusion of the visit.

sections.heading: Social distancing
sections.text:
Maintenance of strict social distancing of at least six feet for staff, providers and for patient scheduling, positioning and movement within the facilities, when feasible. Possible use of telemedicine/telehealth capabilities will be utilized when appropriate.
Employees should physically distance when they take breaks together. Stagger breaks and do not congregate in the break room, and do not share food or utensils.
All Managers are designated as a “physical distance monitor” similar to a safety monitor to ensure social distancing practices are consistently followed.
Frequent and Adequate Hand Sanitization and Handwashing is Required
Sanitizing: Hand sanitization procedures with an alcohol-based hand sanitizer that contains at least 60-95% alcohol is required of all staff, contractors, and providers upon entering the facility. In addition, hand sanitization is required throughout the day.
Hand Washing: Hand washing should occur frequently and prior to donning a mask, gloves and removal of gloves or mask and when hands are soiled. Effective hand washing with soap and water is to occur for at least twenty seconds.
For the correct way to wash hands for 20 seconds, please watch the following CDC video: https://youtu.be/eZw4Ga3jg3E
Wash your hands frequently. Viruses like Covid-19 spread through the air or when you touch someone, shake someone’s hand, or touch a hard surface that has the virus on it and then you touch your face. Hand washing works to interrupt this process.
It does not work to wash your hands and then touch your cell phone that you have been handling all day. Put your cell phone away for your work hours, then wash your hands prior to using it on your breaks.
Do not touch your face, especially your eye, nose, and mouth.

sections.heading: Respiratory Hygiene, and Cough Etiquette
sections.text: Proper respiratory etiquette will include covering coughs and sneezes and not touching eyes, noses, or mouths with unwashed hands or gloves.

sections.heading: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
sections.text:
Effective immediately we are implementing the following parameters for all patient appointments for all sites:
PPE for Patient care: All staff and providers providing direct patient care will wear a facemask and gloves for all patient appointments, the same facemask will be worn for the day.
PPE for patients: All patients, and those who accompany them, are to wear a facemask.

sections.heading: Facemasks
sections.text:
Facemasks should be worn even when staff are in non-patient care areas such as breakrooms. If the mask is removed, for example, in order to eat or drink, the staff member should separate themselves from others.
Staff must take care not to touch their facemask. If they touch or adjust their facemask, they must immediately perform hand hygiene.
Staff should leave the patient care area if they need to remove the facemask.

sections.heading: Extended Use of Facemasks
sections.text:
Extended use of facemasks is the practice of wearing the same facemask for repeated close contact encounters with several different patients, without removing the facemask between patient encounters.
The facemask should be removed and discarded if soiled, damaged, or hard to breathe through.

sections.heading: Eye Protection
sections.text: Tight-fitting goggles may be preferable to face shields for eye protection.

sections.heading: Standard (Universal) Precautions
sections.text: Minimum infection prevention precautions apply to all patient care, regardless of suspected or confirmed infection status of patient, in any health care setting (e.g., hand hygiene, cough etiquette, use of PPE, cleaning and disinfecting environmental surfaces). See CDC: Standard Precautions.

sections.heading: Transmission Precautions
sections.text: Second tier of basic infection control, used in addition to Standard Precautions when patients have diseases that can spread through contact, droplet, or airborne routes, requiring specific precautions based on the circumstances of a case. Transmission precautions are required for cases of suspected COVID-19. See CDC: Transmission-Based Precautions.

sections.heading: Cleaning and Disinfecting
sections.text:
Exam lanes: equipment, testing equipment and exam chairs should be wiped down with disinfectant after each patient
Rooms and instruments should be thoroughly disinfected after each patient encounter per the Cleaning and Disinfecting Policy. Wear disposable gloves to clean and disinfect.
Waiting areas and exam rooms: Eliminate the following items to reduce the spread of germs:
Magazines, books, etc.
Coffee/refreshment service
Regular sanitizing of hard surfaces, clean reception desk, waiting area chairs counters, restrooms, breakrooms, doorknobs, keyboards, remote controls, patient-care equipment, and workstations will be completed per the Cleaning and Disinfecting
Policy. Wear disposable gloves to clean and disinfect.
Prior to implementing the start-up of any invasive procedure, all areas along phases of care (e.g. clinic, preoperative and procedural areas, workrooms, recovery room, patient areas, scopes, sterile processing, etc.) should be terminally cleaned according to evidence-based information.

sections.heading: Tonometer Tip Cleaning
sections.text: The virus causing Covid-19 is an enveloped virus, unlike adenoviruses that are much more resistant to alcohol. If a tonometer tip is cleaned with alcohol and allowed to dry in room air, 70% alcohol solutions should be effective at disinfecting tonometer tips from SARS-CoV-2. However, alcohol will not effectively sterilize the tip against adenoviruses. Use single-use, disposable tonometer tips if available. Tips cleaned with diluted bleach remain a safe and acceptable practice.

sections.heading: Cell Phones
sections.text: Staff cell phones should only be handled by the phone owner and not placed on any common public surface. Unless you are in an administrative role, your cell phone should be placed with your personal belongings and used only during breaks and lunch.

sections.heading: Catering
sections.text: Any food catering into is only permitted by approval of the Medical Director.

sections.heading: Dress Code
sections.text: Freshly laundered clothing should be worn each day by employees.

sections.heading: Visitors
sections.text: Casual visits to the clinic from friends or relatives of employees will not be allowed.

sections.heading: Representatives
sections.text:
Until further notice, pharmaceutical and equipment sales representatives will not be allowed to enter any facility without approval of the Medical Director. When a representative arrives, please contact the Lead RN to meet the representatives outside of facility areas and ask them to leave any samples and literature with the Lead RN.
In addition to the above,  will ensure patient health and the prevention of virus spread by applying the following principles:
Screening and testing patients before visits and monitoring their health prior to starting surgery as part of the pre-operative procedure
Requiring staff to self-monitor and screen for viral symptoms daily
Continuing to use PPE per the latest Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recommendations for all procedures. Follow the Personal Protective Equipment: Surgery Center Policy
Following waiting room spacing guidelines, social distancing, face masking and other recommended procedures for patients and visitors prior to entering the facility
Ensuring heightened disinfection to prevent and mitigate risk of spread
Prior to implementing the start-up of any invasive procedure, all areas along phases of care (e.g. clinic, preoperative and procedural areas, workrooms, recovery room, patient areas, scopes, sterile processing, etc.) should be terminally cleaned according to evidence-based information.
Additional resources available for facilities to help improve infection prevention and control:

sections.heading: CDC Resources
sections.text:
CDC Resources for Health Care Facilities: https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019ncov/healthcare-facilities/index.html
CDC Updates: https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/whats-new-all.html
CDC FAQ for COVID-19: https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/infectioncontrol/infection-prevention-control-faq.html
CDC Interim Infection Prevention and Control Recommendations for Patients with Suspected or Confirmed Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID19)  https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/infection-control/controlrecommendations.html

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
Strict handwashing will always be observed between different patient contacts.
Anesthesia personnel will abide by the scrub suit dress code of the Operating Suites (OR).
Disposable anesthesia patient care supplies will be used whenever possible.
Reusable items will be properly high-level disinfected or steamed, gas or cold sterilized prior to reuse.
Anesthesia machines will be cleaned with a hospital approved germicide at the completion of each case and when soiled during a procedure.
Patients with active airborne communicable disease processes (MRSA, TB, Varicella, etc.) will be cared for and appropriately isolated, based on the specific organism suspected or diagnosed.
Standard precautions will always be observed and there will not be any direct contact with a patient’s blood, body fluids and/or excretions.
Appropriate Hand Hygiene will be performed by provider
All 5 maximal sterile barriers will be used:
Sterile gloves
Sterile gown
Cap
Mask
Large sterile body drape
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: Infection Control

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Whenever possible, the patient should bring someone who can interpret for them if they have limited ability to communicate in English. By bringing a friend or family member to interpret for them, they are authorizing the disclosure of private health information to that third party for that healthcare encounter. This should be confirmed with the patient at the beginning of the encounter and documented in the chart.
If the patient does not have a friend or family member who can interpret for them, The Ferguson Clinic can provide an interpreter who is on staff with The Ferguson Clinic.  If is not possible, The Ferguson Clinic may retain a private, commercial interpreter service to provide such services and that service will be deemed a “business associate” for HIPAA purposes and must agree to the Privacy Rule business associate agreement requirements.
If the Ferguson Clinic regularly serves a distinct nationality in its community, a translator or translation service should be in place to effectively communicate with patients from the community, i.e., forms should be translated into that language whenever possible.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Interpreter Services

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to complete a pre-operative checklist/report, intra-operative and post-operative checklist/report for each patient receiving any procedure at any time.

sections.heading: FORMS USED
sections.text:
Intra-Operative Checklist/Report
Post- Operative Checklist/Report

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The RN will be responsible to complete the above reports for each patient receiving a procedure at The Ferguson Clinic.
The physician will complete an Operative Report that will document an accounting of actual events that occurred during the procedure. The physician will review the Intra-Operative and Post-Operative report to ensure that the Operative Report is complete and concise.
The Pre-Operative Checklist, Intra-Operative Checklist/Report, Post-Operative Checklist/Report, and the Operative Report will be placed in the patient’s electronic medical record.
Section: Surgical Services
