sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to be screening all patients for potential reactions to anesthetic and to ensure that they are prepared to treat Malignant Hyperthermia if it presents itself.

sections.heading: PREVENTING MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA – AN ANESTHESIA PROTOCOL
sections.text: This protocol is intended as a guide for anesthesia practitioners and other health care professionals to be prepared to recognize, treat, and prevent malignant hyperthermia (MH).

sections.heading: ROUTINE PRE-OPERATIVE QUESTIONING
sections.text:
All patients about to undergo anesthesia should be asked these specific questions as part of a medical history:
Is there a family history of MH?
Have there been unexpected deaths or complications arising from anesthesia (including the dental office) with any family members?
Is there a personal history of a muscle disorder (e.g., muscle weakness)?
Is there a personal history of dark or cola-colored urine following anesthesia?
Is there a personal history of unexplained high fever following surgery?

sections.heading: INTRA-OPERATIVE PREPAREDNESS
sections.text: The following precautions should be taken to detect MH in its early stages, when it is usually amenable to treatment without sequelae:

sections.heading: Planning
sections.text:
A written treatment plan should be posted in a conspicuous place. A plan is available from MHAUS.
A kit or cart containing drugs necessary for the treatment of MH should be immediately available to all operating rooms. Each kit should contain 12 vials of dantrolene, and an additional 24 vials or agreement with Pharmacy to obtain immediately, bacteriostatic water for injection, and bicarbonate.
A refrigerator unit near the operating room should be stocked with iced saline. Ready access to an ice machine is important.
All operating and recovery room personnel should be trained in the recognition and treatment of MH. Periodic dry runs of an MH emergency are recommended. In-service materials can be provided by MHAUS.

sections.heading: DURING SURGERY
sections.text:
Evaluate any unexpected hypercarbia, tachycardia, tachypnea, or arrhythmia (e.g., arterial, and venous blood gases). Avoid suppressing tachycardia with beta blockers until MH has been ruled out.
Core temperature should be monitored in all patients given anesthesia for 30 minutes or more. Acceptable core temperature sites include distal esophagus, nasopharynx, axilla, rectum, bladder, and pulmonary artery. Skin temperature may not adequately reflect core temperature during MH episodes. Consider MH in the differential diagnosis of any temperature rise.
Stop inhalation anesthetic and succinylcholine if masseter rigidity occurs. If surgery must continue, immediately switch to non-triggering anesthetics.
Do not give triggering agents to patients with Duchenne Dystrophy, Central Core Disease, Myotonia and other forms of muscular dystrophy.
Sudden cardiac arrest in a young male with normal oxygenation should be considered as secondary to hyperkalemia and so treated.
TREATING THE KNOWN OR SUSPECTED MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA-SUSCEPTIBLE PATIENT
As per The Ferguson Clinic’s patient selection criteria, patients will not be a candidate if they are a known or suspected Malignant Hyperthermia patient. In the case that it is decided by the anesthesia provider and the physician to perform surgery on a known or suspected MH patient the following will be completed.

sections.heading: Anesthesia machine
sections.text:
Remove vaporizers, if possible. Otherwise, drain and disconnect or tape in the off position.
Flow 10 L/m O2 through circuit for at least 20 minutes. If fresh gas hose is replaced, 10 minutes is adequate. During this time, a breathing bag should be attached to the Y-piece of the circle system and the ventilator set to inflate the bag.
Use new or disposable breathing circuit.
Take a preoperative creatine kinase (CK) and CBC.
Place a cooling blanket on the table.

sections.heading: DANTROLENE PROPHYLAXIS
sections.text:
Dantrolene prophylaxis should be considered on an individual patient basis but is not recommended for most MH susceptible. When used, dosage is 2.5 mg/kg IV starting 30 minutes prior to anesthesia. For consultation, contact MHAUS.
NOTE: Dantrolene can worsen muscle weakness in patients with muscle disease and should be used with caution. For procedures with local anesthesia only, dantrolene prophylaxis may be omitted.

sections.heading: INTRA-OPERATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
sections.text:
Techniques of choice
Spinal, epidural, regional or local, if possible.
Safe drugs
Local: No local anesthetics trigger MH; thus, any type of regional anesthesia is safe for MH susceptible.
General: Benzodiazepines, opioids, barbiturates, propofol, ketamine, nitrous oxide, etomidate. Pancuronium, atracurium, vecuronium, pipecuronium, mivacurium, doxacurium, or curare may be used for relaxation. Neostigmine and atropine are used for reversal by some; others disagree on their safety.
Unsafe drugs/MH triggers
Halothane, Enflurane, Isoflurane, Desflurane, Methoxyflurane, Ether, Cyclopropane, Sevoflurane, Ether.
Succinylcholine
Monitoring
Essential: blood pressure, central temperature, ECG, pulse oximeter, and capnograph or capnometer
Strongly suggest respirometer
Use arterial line, CVP or other invasive monitors as appropriate for the surgery

sections.heading: POST-OPERATIVE PROCEDURE
sections.text:
If the anesthetic course has been uneventful:
Continue to monitor temperature and ECG for 1 to 2 hours. No further dantrolene is necessary.
If MH has occurred:
Recover patient in an ICU for 24-48 hours.
Continue IV dantrolene for 48-72 hours, titrated to alleviation of muscle rigidity, tachycardia, acidosis, and CK levels. Suggested dantrolene dosage is at least 1 mg/kg q 6 hours IV. After that, 1 mg/kg may be given q 6 hours orally x 24 hours.
Monitor the patient’s coagulation status, watching for DIC.
Look for myoglobinuria and renal failure and treat as needed.
Use potassium-containing solutions with caution.
Monitor potassium and CK levels q 6 hours at least.
Monitor urine output.
Register patient with North American MH Registry. Forms are available by contacting North American MH Registry, Pennsylvania State University, College of Medicine, Dept. of Anesthesia, PO Box 850, Hershey, PA 17033-0850, (717) 531-6936.
Alert family to the dangers of MH in other family members.
Refer for testing at nearest center (list available from MHAUS).
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: Malignant Hyperthermia

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to standardize the medical record and to document all patient information accurately, and promptly.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Only authorized personnel will have access to Medical Records. Clinical personnel will only have access to information on those patients to whom they are actively providing care and service. Office personnel will only have access to information on those patients to whom they are actively providing service, following up on phone calls, etc. Access to records for individuals from outside the organization, such as surveyors, reviewers, or consultants will be approved by the Privacy Officer or Office Manager on a per occurrence basis and a Business Associate Agreement will be completed.
A medical record is initiated and maintained for every patient receiving care. Entries into the medical records will be made only by The Ferguson Clinic staff and/or by contracted personnel who have a written agreement with the practice. All entries will be documented by at least the first initial and last name of person making the entry.
All entries will reflect the date care was provided, including a month, day, and year. Late entries will be documented and include the date the entries are made, the dates and documentation that was originally omitted, and the signature and title of individual making the late entry.
All records will be maintained in a consistent format.
All Medical Records are to be reviewed and authenticated by a Licensed Health Care Professional as soon as possible.
The medical records thoroughly document all tests, care, and procedures. All test results and reports are read, initialed, and communicated to the patient. All communication with the patient whether in person or by other means should be recorded on the Chart Notes in the medical records.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Medical Record Standardization

Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Medical Records

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: The Medical Records of The Ferguson Clinic shall be maintained in a manner consistent with professional medical practices. Only authorized personnel will have access to Medical Records.  The Privacy Officer shall be responsible for the development and maintenance of the center’s Medical Records.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The medical record is the property of The Ferguson Clinic and is maintained for the benefit of the patient, Medical Staff, and other health care workers. All required records, either as originals or accurate reproductions of the contents of such originals, shall be maintained in such form as to be legible and readily available upon request of the physician or any other person authorized to make such a request.
All patient records will be secured. During personnel occupation of The Ferguson Clinic, patient record files cabinets will be unlocked. When the Ferguson Clinic is unoccupied the patient file cabinets will remain locked.
Written consent of the patient or legally qualified representative is required for release of information from the medical record.
The Privacy Officer or designee is responsible for supervising and maintaining the medical records system. This includes, but is not limited to the following activities:
Supervising staff in the collection, processing, maintenance, storage, timely retrieval, and distribution of medical records.
Retention of active medical records.
Retirement of inactive medical records.
Timely entry of data into the medical records within 48 hours.
Maintaining the confidentiality, security, and physical safety of the medical records.
Maintaining the unique identification of each patient’s medical record.
Obtaining authorization from the patient, or the patient’s legally authorized representative, prior to the release of patient records.

sections.heading: Orienting and training staff regarding the medical records system
sections.text:
Patients will not be discussed by medical or non-medical personnel outside of the organization.
Comments and conversations relating to patients made by nurses or other personnel will be made in confidential settings.
A patient’s medical record will not to be released to other individual(s) without a written release of information signed by the patient and/or his/her representative.

sections.heading: Components of the medical record include, but are not limited to
sections.text:
Demographic information: patient’s name, address, phone number, date of birth, gender, and the name and phone number of any legally authorized representative.
Documentation and findings of assessments.
Conclusions or impressions drawn from medical history and physical examination.
The diagnosis or diagnostic impression, or condition.
Informed Consent for procedures and treatments.
Diagnostic and therapeutic orders.
Relevant diagnoses and therapeutic tests, and results obtained.
Progress notes made by authorized individuals, including the date, staff person, and care or service provided.
All reassessments.
Consultation reports.
List of medications with dosages, OTC drugs, and supplements which will be updated each visit.
Every medication prescribed.
Known drug allergies, adverse drug reactions, patient’s response to medication, and allergies to foods and medications if applicable.
All relevant diagnoses established during care.
Referrals or communications made to external or internal care providers and community agencies.
Treatment summaries and other pertinent documents to promote continuity of care.
Medical record information will be released only upon the written request of the patient, the patient’s legal representative, or a court order. A signed Authorization of Records Release Form must be obtained if the patient or legal representative did NOT sign the Notice of Privacy Practices Acknowledgement.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Medical Records

file: National Fire Protection Association Rating Codes.idml
title: National Fire Protection Association Rating Codes

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to adhere to National Fire Protection Association Rating Codes when storing, handling, and documenting hazardous materials

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
The Safety Officer will ensure that hazardous materials are documented according to the following national Fire Protection Association Rating Codes:
Flammability 	Combustible—Red
4 = Dangerous 	Flash points below 73°F and boiling points below 100°F.  Extremely 					flammable gas or liquid.  Materials can ignite under almost all normal 					temperatures.
3 = Warning	 	Flash points below 73°F and boiling points at or above 100°F.  Liquids or 					solids that can be ignited at almost all temperatures conditions.
2 = Caution		Flash points at or above 100°F or below 200°F.  Materials that must be 					moderately heated or exposed to relatively high ambient temperatures 					for ignition to occur.
1 =Slightly 	Flash points above 200°F.
Combustible	Possible ignition if preheated
0 =Normally 	May not have a flash point.  May not burn.
Stable
Flammability	Reactive – Yellow
4 = Dangerous 	Can explode at normal temperature and pressure.
3 = Warning	Can explode if substance is shocked, heated in confined conditions or 				mixed with water.
2 = Warning 	Normally unstable material and will undergo violent chemical changes but 			will not detonate.  Can react violently with water.
1 = Caution 	Can react if heated or mixed with water.  It is not a violent reaction.
0 = Stable		Does not react with water.
Health 		Blue
4 = Dangerous 	Can be fatal on minimal exposure.  Need special protective equipment.
3 = Warning 		Protect skin; do not inhale; corrosive; toxic.  Major injury likely unless 				prompt action is taken, and medical treatment sought.
2 = Warning 		Can be harmful if inhaled or absorbed.  Moderate toxic material.  Seek 				medical attention because exposure could cause possible residual injury.
1 = Caution		Slight toxic material that may irritate.  Without treatment, minor injury 				may result.
0 = Very minimal	No hazard.  Some degree of toxicity may be present.
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: National Fire Protection Association Rating Codes

file: Notification of Breach of Unsecured PHI.idml
title: Notification of Breach of Unsecured PHI

sections.text:
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Notification of Breach of Unsecured PHI
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Notification of Breach of Unsecured PHI

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: To establish procedures for the notification of individuals, prominent media, and the State of Hawaii, Secretary of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, or such office that has judicial oversight, as appropriate, following the discovery of a breach of unsecured protected health information (PHI) by The Ferguson Clinic or its Business Associate(s), and to identify when the unauthorized acquisition, access, use or disclosure of unsecured PHI is a breach for notification purposes.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is The Ferguson Clinic’s policy to provide notification of the breach of unsecured PHI in accordance with the requirements of the State of Hawaii, Federal HIPAA and The Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health (HITECH) Act and implementing Regulations.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The Privacy Officer will determine whether there has been a breach of unsecured PHI. PHI is “Unsecured” when it is not rendered unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable to unauthorized individuals through the use of encryption and/or destruction. The Privacy Officer will investigate potential breaches and take steps to mitigate losses and protect against any further breaches.
The Privacy Officer will notify The Ferguson Clinic’s Medical Director regarding any potential breaches.
Determining Whether the Disclosure Poses a Significant Risk of Harm
Not every unauthorized acquisition, access, use or disclosure of unsecured PHI will require notification, only those posing a significant risk of financial, reputational, or other harm.
Notification to Individuals
Timelines for Notification
The Privacy Officer is responsible for breach notification. The Facility will provide the notification required by this Policy without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 calendar days after discovery of a breach. A breach will be treated as discovered by the organization as of the first day on which the breach is known or, by exercising reasonable diligence, should have been known by the organization or any person other than the person committing the breach who is a workforce member, Business Associate, or agent of the organization. The Privacy Officer will contact the Medical Director to review the reporting of any breaches.
Content of Notification
The notification required by this Policy will be written in plain language, must not include extraneous materials, and must include all the following:
A brief description of what happened, including the date of the breach and the date of the discovery of the breach, if known.
A description of the types of unsecured protected health information that were involved in the breach (such as whether full name, social security number, date of birth, home address, account number, diagnosis, disability, or other types of information were involved).
Any steps individuals should take to protect themselves from potential harm resulting from the breach.
A brief description of what the Facility involved is doing to investigate the breach, to mitigate harm to individuals, and to protect against any further breaches; and
Contact procedures for individuals to ask questions or learn additional information which will include a toll-free telephone number, an email address, web site, or postal address.
Methods of Notification by the Organization
The notification required by this Policy will be provided using the following methods and may be provided in one or more mailings as information is available:

sections.heading: Written Notice
sections.text:
Written notification will be sent by first class mail to the individual at the last known address of the individual or, if the individual agrees to electronic notice and such agreement has not been withdrawn, by encrypted electronic mail. The notification may be provided in one or more mailings as information is available; or
If the Facility knows the individual is deceased and has the address of the next of kin or personal representative of the individual, written notification will be sent by first class mail to either the next of kin or personal representative of the individual.

sections.heading: Substitute Notice
sections.text:
Where there is insufficient or out of date contact information that precludes written notification to the individual, a substitute form of notice reasonably calculated to reach the individual will be used. Substitute notice need not be provided in the case in which there is insufficient or out of date contact information that precludes written notification to the next of kin or personal representative of the individual.
When there is insufficient or out of date contact information for fewer than 10 individuals, then such substitute notice may be provided by an alternative form of written notice, telephone, or other means.
When there is insufficient or out of date contact information for 10 or more individuals, then the substitute notice will:
Be in a form of either a conspicuous posting for a period of 90 days on the home page of the web site of the organization, or conspicuous notice in major print or broadcast media in geographic areas where the individuals affected by the breach likely reside: and
Must include a toll-free number that remains active for at least 90 days where the individual can learn whether the individual’s unsecured PHI may have been included in the breach.

sections.heading: Additional Notice in Urgent Situations
sections.text: In any case the Facility deems to require urgency because of possible imminent misuse of unsecured PHI, the Facility may provide information to individuals by telephone or other means as appropriate in addition to notice provided for in this policy.

sections.heading: Notification to the Media
sections.text:
For a breach of unsecured PHI involving 500 or more residents of the State of Hawaii or jurisdiction (city/county), the Privacy Officer will notify prominent media outlets serving the State of Hawaii or jurisdiction. The Privacy Officer will provide the media notification required above without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 calendar days after discovery of a breach.
Content of Notification
The Notification to the Media will include the same information as is required for the Notification to Individuals.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Notification of Breach of Unsecured PHI

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Operating Room Sanitation
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Operating Room Sanitation

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to outline an effective environmental sanitation program within all treatment rooms and operating room, and to control and reduce the possibility of cross infection of the surgical patient and personnel in the operating room.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Patients should be provided with a safe, clean environment free from dust and organic debris.
The Infection Control and Prevention Officer shall visually inspect the operating room for cleanliness before supplies and instrument sets are brought into the room.
During the surgical procedure, efforts should be directed at confining contamination.
During a procedure, areas contaminated by organic debris (such as blood) should receive immediate attention by applying an appropriate agent.
A tuberculocidal hospital-grade chemical germicide helps maintain a safe, clean environment.
Contaminated disposable items should be placed into leak-proof containers.
Personnel should use gloves, instruments or both in counting and containing sponges.
Protective goggles and disposable masks with eye protective shields are available for all staff and surgeons if there is a potential for aerosolization of blood or body fluids.
Unnecessary traffic into and out of the room should be discouraged to minimize human bacterial shedding and to curtail air turbulence.
The exterior surfaces of soiled specimen containers should be cleaned prior to removal from the operating room.
After each case, all items that have encounter the patient and/or sterile field should be considered contaminated, and their disposition should reflect appropriate contamination control measures.
Disposable items contaminated with blood and body fluids should be placed in closable, leak-proof containers or bags that are color-coded, labeled, or tagged.
Gowns and gloves should be removed inside out.
Non-woven fabrics and other waste articles should be disposed of after use.
Disposable anesthesia circuits, masks, and endotracheal tubes should be discarded after use.
Reusable woven fabrics, whether soiled or not, should be placed in laundry bags, which are then securely closed.
Sharps should be handled and disposed of in puncture-resistant containers.
Instruments, basins, trays, and other items should be handled only by   personnel wearing personal protective equipment until the items are decontaminated.

sections.heading: Suctioning Materials
sections.text:
Disposable suction tubing should be used because of the difficulties encountered in cleaning the lumen of reusable suction tubing.
Disposable containers may be disposed of intact, or the suction contents may be emptied into a sanitary sewer system.
The horizontal surfaces of furniture, overhead lamps, and equipment used in the surgical procedure should be cleaned with mechanical friction and an appropriate agent.
Spot cleaning should be done, as necessary.
At the completion of the day’s schedule, the operating room should be terminally cleaned by the assigned cleaning service.
Mechanical friction and an appropriate hospital-grade disinfectant/detergent should be used to clean equipment and the operating room, including:
Surgical lights and tracks.
Fixed and ceiling-mounted equipment.
Furniture, including wheels and casters.
Handles of cabinets and push plates, face plates of vents.
Horizontal surfaces (e.g., tops of counters, shelving, etc.).
The entire floor.
Kick buckets and scrub sinks.
Cleaning equipment should be disassembled, cleaned with an appropriate agent, and dried thoroughly before storage.
The Infection Control and Prevention Officer is responsible and accountable for the enforcement of operating room sanitation practices.
This information will be included in the orientation and ongoing education of all appropriate personnel at The Ferguson Clinic.
Reference:  “Sanitation in the Surgical Practice Setting,” Association of Operating Room Nurses, Standards and Recommended Practices
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Operating Room Sanitation

Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: PACU

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The function of the Post Anesthesia Care Unit (PACU) is to provide the concentrated and comprehensive care necessary in the immediate post anesthetic period for patient who have had surgical procedures. All Ferguson Clinic will conform to these guidelines.

sections.heading: ORGANIZATION
sections.text:
In general, physicians shall be responsible for the patient’s medical care and the nursing staff shall be responsible for the patient’s nursing care. Policies and procedures are formulated, published, and reviewed periodically through a combined effort of two groups.
Medical Supervision
Physician(s)
Medical Director
Anesthesia providers
Nursing Supervision
PACU supervisor or head nurse should be administratively responsible to a designee of the nursing staff but medically responsible to the Medical Director.

sections.heading: ADMISSION CRITERIA
sections.text: The anesthesia provider or physician in charge of the PACU shall determine which patients shall be admitted to the PACU. This may include patient who have had general, regional, or local anesthesia. A suggested ration recovery room beds to anesthetizing locations is 1.5 recovery beds to every active anesthetizing location.

sections.heading: DISCHARGE CRITERIA
sections.text:
Patients may be discharged only after vital signs are stable. All PACU patients will have recorded (at regular intervals) a post anesthesia recovery score (PARS).
Patients may be discharged from the care of the physician or the anesthesia provider if the physician has delegated that privilege and the governing body has approved such delegation, only after an evaluation of the patient’s condition by the physician or anesthesia provider and the physician or anesthesia provider has written a discharge note.
The physician will remain onsite until all patients have been discharged following clinical recovery from their surgery/procedure.
An ACLS certified Licensed Healthcare Professional will always remain onsite while patients are in the facility.

sections.heading: POST ANESTHESIA RECOVERY SCORE
sections.text:
A variety of scores for assessing patient’s readiness for discharge from the PACU are successfully employed. A sample of one developed by Aldrete is published herein)
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: PACU

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Passwords for access to The Ferguson Clinic’s information systems shall comply with this Policy and Procedure.

sections.heading: CONFIDENTIALITY
sections.text:
Passwords shall be changed periodically, as provided below, and in the event of a security breach or other security incident.
Computer passwords shall remain strictly confidential and shall not be divulged, orally or in writing, by a The Ferguson Clinic’ employees. The password’s owner is responsible for the security of that password.
Passwords may not be written down, printed, or stored in unencrypted electronic format.
After three consecutive, failed attempts to log on to The Ferguson Clinic’s network, the employee will be locked out and the IT administrator will be contacted to access.
Passwords for The Ferguson Clinic system are encrypted.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that all orders pertaining to patients be initiated by the Physician in writing or verbally and followed by the Registered Nurse or Licensed Practical Nurse, when applicable. Surgical Technologists, when applicable do not receive any verbal orders that would need to be charted in the patient’s medical record.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The clinical staff which includes registered nurses, licensed practical nurses, surgical technologists, when applicable, will adhere to all physician orders.
Only Registered Nurses can take verbal orders regarding medication.
Medication documentation can only be completed by the Registered Nurses, CRNA’s,
Anesthesiologists or Surgeons.
Documentation of all orders will be completed in the patient’s medical record and signed by the physician.
The Registered Nurse or Licensed Practical Nurse completing those orders will document what was done and be signed by the person completing them.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to safeguard the privacy of all patients, so no patient is caused unnecessary embarrassment or discomfort, or is made the object of scorn, ridicule, or exploitation as the result of his/her use of the facility.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Patient records are the property of The Ferguson Clinic. Any patient may request a copy of his/her record; however, prior to the release of the record, a patient must sign an authorization to release his/her medical information.
ALL PATIENT RECORDS AND INFORMATION ABOUT PATIENTS ARE CONFIDENTIAL.
Employees may not reveal or discuss confidential patient information with anyone unless so authorized.
When patient information is discussed with other health care personnel during treatment, discretion should be used in the place of discussion and in the tone of voice.
All patient information is considered strictly confidential. When employees leave their desk or work area, they should not speak to any unauthorized persons, including fellow employees, friends, relatives, or other patients about their work.  Documents and reports should not be left on desks within view of the public and computers must be logged off prior to leaving desks and workstations.  The records and forms employees work with cannot be given or shown to persons outside the office without official approval.
A violation of these rules can result in IMMEDIATE DISMISSAL or other disciplinary actions.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient Confidentiality

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
Communication will be carried out by telephone, email, or text messaging for making appointments, confirming appointments, or giving referrals/recommendations of other agencies or providers.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Patient Correspondence

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to provide high quality health care services in compliance with the principles of professional practice and ethical conduct.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To ensure reasonable follow-up regarding patient adherence to a plan of care
To reduce the risk of litigation

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
If a patient does not keep his/her follow-up appointment, the following steps should be
taken and documented in the electronic medical record:
Call the patient up to two times to try to reschedule.
Document dates and time of unsuccessful contact in the electronic medical record.
If the patient/parent or legal guardian cannot be contacted, or if he or she repeatedly misses his/her follow-up appointment, a letter should be sent by certified mail to indicate that unless the patient comes into the office for treatment, The Ferguson Clinic cannot be responsible for his/her care.
If the certified mail is returned deemed undeliverable the unopened envelope will be placed in the patient’s electronic medical record.
*Certified Mail:  If the patient does not accept the letter, and other correspondence has not yielded an appropriate response, place unopened, returned certified letter in patient file.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient Follow-Up of Missed Appointments

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Medical Records will be made available to patients upon their request.

sections.heading: Releasing to Patient
sections.text: A patient who requests a copy of his/her Medical Records will be asked to sign an Authorization to Release Information Form” if a Notice of Privacy Practices Acknowledgment was not signed or it does not fall under the “release criteria” outlined in the Notice of Privacy Practices.

sections.heading: Release to Third Parties
sections.text:
All patient files are confidential.  Information therein shall be released to third parties only upon written authorization from the patient. The patient must sign an Authorization to Release Information form if a Notice of Privacy Practices Acknowledgement was not signed before the request. The written authorization will be time limited and not exceed 90 days from the date of the signature.  A staff person will witness the written authorization and the patient will need to show proof of identity.
If patient records are subpoenaed, the Medical Director should immediately contact legal counsel as there may be patient-doctor privileges that would prohibit complying with the subpoena.
No copies of a medical records will be released to other practitioners unless a signed Authorization to Release Information form or a signed Notice of Privacy Practices Acknowledgement is on file with the Center.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Patient Medical Records Release

title: Patient/Parent or Legal Guardian Complaints

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to resolve all patient conflicts whenever possible and to monitor significant patient complaints as a part of the Quality Improvement Plan.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Whenever a patient files a complaint, careful attention must be given to documenting the following on the Patient Complaint Record:
Patient name
Date of complaint
Person filing the complaint if other than the patient
Person hearing complaint
Complaint
Date of service
When the complaint is given to the staff person who is the most appropriate person to address the complaint, that person should document on the Patient Complaint Record the following:
Electronic medical record #
Date of service
Reason for visit
Date of follow up call/action
Staff person speaking with or writing to the patient
Documentation of conversation and/or copy of correspondence
If a staff person believes that a patient’s complaint has been resolved, a statement to that effect should be noted on the Patient Complaint Record log and be submitted to the Quality Improvement Committee and the Office Manager.
Each patient or family making a significant complaint should receive a written response which
substantially addresses the complaint.
Presentation of a complaint will not in itself serve to compromise a patient’s future access to or
quality of care.
All patient complaints will be submitted to the Quality Improvement Committee for review
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient /Parent or Legal Guardian Complaints

sections.heading: OBJECTIVE
sections.text: To recognize and respect the basic human rights of patients

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
A copy of the Rights and Responsibilities of Patients will be posted throughout the facility and given to the patient for signature. A copy will be placed in the patient’s electronic medical record and a copy will be given to the patient.
All complaints will be responded to within 14 days of receipt.
Significant patient complaints will be documented and resolved whenever possible.
Patient complaints will be logged, and any unresolved complaints will be brought to the attention of the Office Manager and the Quality Improvement Committee.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient Rights and Responsibilities

file: Patient Safety in the Procedure Room.idml
title: Patient Safety in the Procedure Room

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure that each patient is always safe in the procedure room.

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text:
To identify and eliminate potential safety hazards, thereby reducing risk to patients which include but not limited to:
Physical Injuries
Patient Selection Criteria
Medication Errors
Wrong Person/Wrong Site
Accidents
Undue Psychological Stress
Patient Positioning
Infections
Adverse Events

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Using the Ferguson Clinic’s Risk Management Plan, each patient will be critiqued, and a value will be placed for either low risk, moderate risk, or high risk.
Once the value is given, the Physician along with the RN and Anesthesia Provider (if applicable) will determine if the risk warrants the procedure being performed.
All staff and contracted staff will always be instructed to read the Risk Management Plan and adhere to the Medical Policy and Procedure to ensure patient safety in the procedure room.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Patient Safety in the Procedure Room

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to identify those areas whereby improvements may be made based on feedback from our patients.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
A Patient Satisfaction Survey will be given to the patients after their visit to the office.
Patient Satisfaction Surveys will be reviewed monthly and analyzed for process improvement ideas by the Office Manager and the Quality Improvement Committee.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient Satisfaction Surveys

sections.heading: PROTOCOL
sections.text:
All staff members will be required to get vaccinated for Covid-19 except on religious grounds or previous reaction/allergy to vaccines.
Any staff member that is not vaccinated, except those that are exempt, will be sent home without pay (PTO may be used) until the vaccination process is initiated.
Any expense that the staff member accrues through the vaccination process, that is not covered by insurance, will be reimbursed by the facility.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
Covid-19 is especially concerning for the elderly and people with chronic medical conditions such as heart disease, diabetes, immunosuppression, and lung disease. These individuals are more susceptible to becoming ill from being exposed to this virus. A significant portion of our patients are among these high-risk groups. To continue to provide responsible medical care to our patients we will implement modifications in our care processes.
As recommended by CDC, staff shall screen and identify visitors and patients at risk for having a COVID-19 infection before and immediately upon arrival to the facility.

sections.heading: PROTOCOL
sections.text: For the duration of the Covid-19 pandemic or until further notification, facilities will follow appropriate CDC Guidelines, CMS directives, Department of Health guidelines and recommendations. This will include promptly identifying and managing patients, staff, or visitors with undiagnosed respiratory symptoms with the following actions being implemented.

sections.heading: General Guidelines
sections.text:
Per CMS guidelines, The Ferguson Clinic will set limitations on visitation. Limitations will include restricting the number of visitors and or drivers per patient to one and only if the patient needs assistance, or a guardian or parent, to accompany them to the appointment. This includes limiting visitors under the age of 18 to patients only. All other visitors and drivers are to remain outside of the facilities or in a vehicle and wait off-site until contacted at the conclusion of the visit.
All protocols and procedures in the Covid-19 Infection Prevention and Control Policy will be adhered to at all times.
Screening Protocols: Patients will be screened at the time of their appointment, during the reminder phone call, and when they arrive for their appointment.
Screening Procedure for Appointment Scheduling
At the time the appointment is scheduled, ask the patient the questions on the screening tool.
Request that patients bring a face mask to their appointment and if one is not available a mask will be provided upon their arrival. A cloth or homemade cloth mask is acceptable for patients and visitors.
Inform the patient that we are requesting that they do not come into the office upon their arrival. Instead, call the office and the staff member will check them in remotely and a staff member will contact them when it is time for them to come into the facility and directly into the testing or exam area.
For the remote check in process, verify their cell number during the scheduling conversation and ensure they have the office phone number.
Inform the patient that, per CMS guidelines, we are restricting the number of visitors and or drivers per patient to one and only if the patient needs assistance, or a guardian or parent, to accompany them to the appointment. All other visitors and drivers are to remain outside the facility or in a vehicle.
Screening during Appointment Reminder Phone Call
At the time the appointment reminder call is made, 2-days in advance, use the questions on the screening tool.
Request that patients bring a face mask to their appointment and if one is not available a mask will be provided upon their arrival. A cloth or homemade cloth mask is acceptable for patients and visitors.
Inform the patient that we are requesting that they do not come into the office upon their arrival. Instead, call the office and the staff member will check them in remotely and a staff member will contact them when it is time for them to come into the facility and directly into the testing or exam area.
For the remote check in process, verify their cell number during the scheduling conversation and ensure they have the office phone number.
Inform the patient that, per CMS guidelines, we are restricting the number of visitors and or drivers per patient to one and only if the patient needs assistance, or a guardian or parent, to accompany them to the appointment. All other visitors and drivers are to remain outside the facility or in a vehicle.
Our reminder call phone notification will be changed to the following protocol. The call will take place two weekdays ahead of the scheduled appointment:
“Hello, this is (your name) from The Ferguson Clinic calling for (patient’s first/last name) regarding your appointment on (date) at (time) with (doctor) at our (location) office.
We are concerned about your health as well as the health and safety of all our patients, providers, and staff and we have implemented new screening procedures for duration of the Covid-19 pandemic.
If you are experiencing any flu-like symptoms with a cough, fever (≥100.4oF or higher), or have a respiratory illness, or if you have been in contact with anyone with a confirmed or suspected Covid-19 infection in the past 2 to 14 days, we will ask that you reschedule until you are asymptomatic or 14 days has passed. [If the answer to any question is “yes” they should reschedule, stay home and speak with their primary care physician.]
Please wear a face mask to your appointment if you have one available, otherwise a mask will be provided for you. A cloth mask or handmade mask is acceptable.
When you have arrived at our parking lot, please do not come into the office. Instead, please call our office and we will check you in remotely. When it is time for you to come into the facility and directly into the testing or exam area, a staff member will contact you. [Remember to verify their cell number in the chart and ensure they have the office phone number.]
Anyone who is accompanying you, other than someone you require for assistance, or for minors; a parent or guardian, will be asked to stay outside of our facility or in a vehicle.
We appreciate your compliance with our new safety protocols. Thank you.” [If leaving a voicemail message leave the appropriate phone number to call back and confirm.]
If there are any positive screening answers from a patient tell them for the health and safety of others, please call us back to schedule an appointment when their symptoms have dissipated for a minimum of 14 days.
Screening at Appointment Arrival

sections.heading: Our intake process will be changed to the following protocol
sections.text:
All patients (and visitors) who enter without a face mask, please request they don a mask.
Ask all patients (and visitors) to use hand sanitizer upon entry.
The patient is to be accompanied only if they need assistance or require a parent or guardian, all other visitors and drivers are to remain outside the facility or in a vehicle.
The full check in process is to include a health screening and state the following:
“Welcome, we are concerned about your health as well as the health and safety of all our patients, providers, and staff and we have implemented new screening procedures for duration of the Covid-19 pandemic.
Are you are experiencing any flu-like symptoms with a cough, fever (≥100.4oF or higher), or have a respiratory illness, or if you have been in contact with anyone with a confirmed or suspected Covid-19 infection in the past 2 to 14 days? [If the answer to any question is “yes” they should reschedule, return home and speak with their primary care physician.] [If the answer is “no” they may continue with their appointment.]
Please keep your face mask on at all times until you leave our facility. We appreciate your compliance with our new safety protocols. Thank you.”
After the full check-in process is completed with the front office, the patient is to proceed directly into the testing or exam area for COVID-19 rapid testing.
Surgical Screening Protocols
Per the above procedures, patients will be screened at the time of their appointment, during the reminder phone call, and when they arrive for their appointment.
Additionally, The Ferguson Clinic will ensure patient procedural health and the prevention of virus spread by applying the following principles:
Ensuring patients have been medically cleared by their primary care physician where applicable
Postponement of Surgery
Phase I: Preoperative
Use CMS guidelines for preoperative assessment process.
Patient readiness for surgery can be coordinated by anesthesiology-led preoperative assessment services.
Use CMS guidelines for timing of re-assessing patient health status.
Special attention and re-evaluation are needed if patient has had Covid-19-related illness.
A recent history and physical examination within 30 days per Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and AAAHC requirement is necessary for all patients. This will verify that there has been no significant interim change in patient’s health status.
Consider use of telemedicine for components of the preoperative patient evaluation.
Some face-to-face components can be scheduled on day of procedure, particularly for healthier patients.
Surgery and anesthesia consents per facility policy and state requirements.
Laboratory testing and radiologic imaging procedures should be determined by patient indications and procedure needs. Testing and repeat testing without indication is discouraged.
Assess preoperative patient education.
Advanced directive discussion with surgeon, especially patients who are older adults, frail, or post-COVID-19.
Phase II: Immediate Preoperative
Use CMS guidelines for pre-procedure interval evaluation since Covid-19-related postponement.
Assess need for revision of nursing, anesthesia, surgery checklists regarding Covid-19.
Phase III: Intraoperative
Assess need for revision of pre-anesthetic and pre-surgical timeout components.
Use AAAHC guidelines for who is present during intubation and extubation.
Use AAAHC, CMS and CDC guidelines for PPE use.
Use AAAHC and CDC guidelines for presence of nonessential personnel including students.
Phase IV: Postoperative
Adhere to standardized care protocols for reliability in light of potential different personnel. Standardized protocols optimize length of stay efficiency and decrease complications.
Phase V: Post Discharge Care Planning
Home setting: Ideally patients shall be discharged home.
Subject: Covid-19
Section: Patient Screening

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to put forth criteria to minimize medical liability issues.

sections.heading: The criteria for selecting a patient are as follows
sections.text:
A patient will be considered with a BMI <40
A patient must have a Signed Informed Consent
Patient must be 18 years and older
Patient must have reasonable results expectations
Patient cannot have past medical history of anesthesia reactions
Patient cannot have past or current bleeding disorder
Patient may not have a Caprini score >7
Patients that have a history of cardiology issues must have a clearance from their PCP/Cardiologist.
Patients must have a caregiver upon discharge that provides a ride home and stays with them for 24 hours.

sections.heading: Contraindications
sections.text:
Active medical disease (for example, newly diagnosed, untreated diabetes or hypertension, acute pharyngitis, or other infectious disease)
Patients under the age of 18.
Untreated or uncontrolled psychiatric disease.
Ongoing substance abuse.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Patient Selection Criteria

sections.heading: Dear (Patient Name)
sections.text:
We find it necessary to notify you that we are withdrawing from rendering services.
With your written approval, I will make your electronic medical records and information regarding treatment you have received available to the provider you select.  Enclosed you will find an “Authorization to Release Medical Information” form for this purpose.
Please call me if you have any questions.
Respectfully yours,
Office Manager
(Note to office staff:  Send this by certified mail, place copy of letter in patient chart, attach certified receipt of letter to the copy and file in patient chart. If the certified letter is returned, place it in the medical chart unopened.)
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient Termination of Services

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to provide high quality health care services in compliance with the principles of professional practice and ethical conduct.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To ensure reasonable follow-up regarding patient adherence to a plan of care
To reduce the risk of litigation

sections.heading: Proper reasons include
sections.text:
When care is no longer needed
When the patient specifically withdraws from the relationship
When the care of the patient is transferred to another physician
When the physician who is withdrawing from treating the patient gives ample notice to the patient
Patients who may present an extra risk, and therefore require extra caution, are individuals who:
Fail to return for follow-up treatments or appointments
Refuse to cooperate with the staff
Appear disturbed
Abusive behavior
Threaten a lawsuit
When terminating services, The Ferguson Clinic must notify the patient in writing.
The practice does not need to state the reason for declining treatment, but the practice may do so.
The practice must inform the patient in writing that as of a specific date (usually within 30 days), the practice will be unable to treat the patient any further.
The practice should provide in writing a recommendation for alternate care.
A copy of the certified* letter should be placed in the electronic medical record, along with the return post card from the post office regarding delivery of the letter.
If the certified letter is returned deemed undeliverable the unopened envelop will be placed in the patient’s electronic medical record.
*Certified Mail:  If the patient does not accept the letter, and other correspondence has not yielded an appropriate response, place unopened, returned certified letter in the patient’s electronic medical record.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient Termination of Services

Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient/Family Education

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to promote and provide interactive patient education to improve the patient’s medical outcomes.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To provide education to meet identified patient’s needs
To enhance the patient’s understanding of his/her health status, options for treatment, and the anticipated risks and benefits of treatment
To encourage patient’s participation in the decisions involving his/her care
To increase the likelihood that patients will follow their therapeutic care plan
To maximize patient self-care skills
To increase the patient ability to cope with his/her health status
To promote healthy lifestyles
To inform patients about their financial responsibilities

sections.heading: FORMS/MATERIALS TO BE USED
sections.text:
Face to face discussions
Print education material - pamphlets, brochures
Instruction Sheets

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Patient education begins upon the first visit and continues throughout the patient’s care.
Assessment of the patient’s learning needs, abilities, and readiness to learn is an important aspect of the patient’s care and considers the patient’s:
Age
Values and beliefs
Cultural and religious practices
Emotional barrier
Physical, cognitive, and communication limitations
Language barriers, primary language, and literacy

sections.heading: Implementation
sections.text:
Provide the patient with information that will enable the patient to learn how to improve outcomes.
Use interpreters and/or communication equipment, if necessary.
Utilize various teaching methods such as audio-visual materials, printed diagrams, written instructions, demonstration, and verbal communication.
Discuss patient responsibilities in their care.
Documentation of Patient Education should include the following but not limited to:
Options (including financial implications)
Nutrition, diet, and rehabilitation techniques, when appropriate
Access to other resources
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Patient/Family Education

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
Equipment Locations
A list of all critical information system equipment will be maintained by the Privacy Officer
Equipment used in support of The Ferguson Clinic’s information technology systems may not be removed from The Ferguson Clinic facilities without prior written authorization from the Privacy Officer and Medical Director.
All removals of information technology systems equipment will be logged.

sections.heading: Physical Access
sections.text:
All physical access to information system equipment must be approved by the Privacy Officer.
The Privacy Officer will maintain an access list indicating all personnel authorized to access critical equipment and server rooms and each person’s level of access by job duties.
Access will be evaluated on an individual basis depending upon The Ferguson Clinic’ employee job duties.
Changes to employee job responsibilities require immediate review of physical access.
A former The Ferguson Clinic employee may not have physical access to non-public areas of the building housing systems, data and records that contain PHI.
No guests including, without limitation, service contractors, auditors, and maintenance personnel may have unattended access to equipment without approval of the Privacy Officer and/or Medical Director.

sections.heading: Maintenance
sections.text: The Privacy Officer will maintain a log documenting all repairs and modifications that are related to the security of information systems equipment.

sections.heading: Contingency Operations
sections.text:
Loss of physical access due to fire, natural disasters, and man-made disasters, or failure of supporting utilities shall be addressed in the Emergency/Disaster Contingency Plan.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Physical Security

file: Physician Protocol for Local Anesthesia Procedures.idml
title: Physician Protocol for Local Anesthesia Procedures

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic for all clinical personnel to follow the Physician Standing Orders that have been established by Medical Director and approved by the Governing Body within the physician’s scope of licensure.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text: The clinical staff, which includes registered nurses, licensed practical nurses, surgical techs, and medical assistants will adhere to all physician standing orders based on their scope of licensure.

sections.heading: Laboratory evaluations
sections.text:
COVID Testing as applicable for NeoGraft and other modalities
Females in reproductive age range:  urine hCG on morning of surgery

sections.heading: Miscellaneous
sections.text:
Medical Clearance for patients over age 65
Medical Clearance for patients with chronic diseases, such as diabetes
Consult with physician of additional orders, as indicated for unusual clinical situations, drug allergies, or chronic diseases.

sections.heading: Day of Surgery
sections.text:
Medications may be given at the discretion of the surgeon and/or anesthesia provider.
These medications may include oral anxiolytics, analgesics, anti-hypertensives, anticholinergics, antihistamines, and anti-emetics.
IV saline lock may be started at the discretion of the MD and/or anesthesia provider.
Patient monitoring during the procedure will at least include pulse oximeter, heart rate, NIPB, and patient comfort level.  Any case involving license anesthesia provider will in addition monitor patient temperature, ECG, and capnography.
Start IV Heplock with saline fluid
ECG, NIPB and Pulse Oximeter monitors placed on all patients receiving anesthetic beyond local.

sections.heading: Post-Operative
sections.text:
Discontinue IV saline lock with anesthesia provider or MD approval once patient is stable in recovery and has no additional IV medications needed.
Apply standard post-op absorbent pads and compression garment.
Review post-operative instructions with patient and patient’s caregiver and provide a copy.
All patients receiving anesthetic beyond local we be recovered until discharge criteria are met, and vital signs are stable. NOT LESS THAN 30 MINUTES POST-OP
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Physician Protocol for Local Anesthesia Procedures

Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Hazard Communications Plan Overview
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Hazard Communications Plan Overview
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Hazard Communications Plan Overview

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
To ensure that all avenues are addressed, the following written hazard communication program has been established.
The Safety Officer has been designated as the person in charge of the Hazard Communication Program and will be responsible for seeing that all aspects of the program are carried out in the fashion intended.  A copy of the program is available in the facility.

sections.heading: In general, the program includes the following elements
sections.text:
Hazard Determination Procedures
List of Hazardous Chemicals
Procedures - Labels and Other Forms of Warning
Procedures - Safety Data Sheets
Methods for Treating Individuals with Suspected Disease or Tuberculosis
Methods to Inform Employees About Hazards of Non-Routine Tasks
Methods to Inform Contractors About Hazards
Training-Procedures/Formats
Hazard Communication Standard - Training Requirements

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Hazard Determination
The Safety Officer will review the information on the SDS and will assure that information concerning the hazards is transmitted to the appropriate personnel.
The Safety Officer will review each SDS to see that it contains the following information:
Identities used on label
Chemical and common names (generic)
Physical and chemical characteristic
Health hazards (signs and symptoms of exposure; medical conditions aggravated by exposure)Primary routes of entry
OSHA permissible exposure limits
Carcinogenicity
Precautions for safe handling and use (spill, leak, and disposal procedures)
Control measures (identifies engineering controls, PPE, and work practices)
Emergency and first aid procedures
Date of preparation of SDS
Name/address/phone number of SDS manufacturer or distributor
The SDS sheets can be obtained online, if they are not available online then the Safety Officer will send a letter or email to the manufacturer for the SDS sheet.
The Safety Officer will write to or email the supplier for any missing information.  If the supplier does not respond within thirty (30) days, a new supplier will be found.  If this is not possible, the Safety Officer will file a complaint with the OSHA Area Office.
The Safety Officer will retain the original SDS in the SDS Book.
Every two years, the Safety Officer will either access the updated SDS sheet via the Internet or will participate in automatic updates with the manufacturer or company that provides those updates. An updated version of the SDS sheet is important to assure that The Ferguson Clinic has been updated if new and significant health information is found.  The Safety Officer will review the new SDS, inform appropriate personnel if changes are found, and file the new SDS form.
The Safety Officer will arrange to have monitoring completed for any chemical in the
workplace that is questionable.
Every attempt will be made by The Ferguson Clinic to provide necessary engineering controls and administrative controls to eliminate all hazards to our employees.

sections.heading: List of Hazardous Chemicals
sections.text:
An inventory must be made of all products and chemicals present in the workplace.  This includes all solvents, cleaning products, disinfectants and sterilants, pharmaceuticals, and laboratory chemicals.  Excluded from this list are any paper products, needles, syringes, etc.
The chemical inventory list will be posted by the Safety Officer in the front of the SDS Book.
Further information, such as physical and chemical hazards of the chemicals, detection methods and protective measures on each chemical noted, can be obtained by reviewing the SDS binder which is in the clean room or designated area.
Whenever changes are made (i.e., addition or deletion of a product), a copy of the SDS is placed on file after it has been reviewed by the Safety Officer.  The Safety Officer will update the chemical inventory list and will notify the appropriate personnel of the changes.

sections.heading: Labels and Other Forms of Warning
sections.text:
All containers in the workplace are to be labeled.  The purpose of labeling each container is to provide an immediate visual warning about the hazards of the chemical in the container.
The Safety Officer is responsible for ensuring that all containers are labeled.  Since chemical manufacturers are required to label their containers, The Ferguson Clinic will rely on the manufacturer’s label as the primary means of labeling.
All products used at The Ferguson Clinic are to be stored in their original containers with their original labels left in place.
All products transferred out of their original container must be labeled with:
Contents
Appropriate hazard warning label
Name and address of the manufacturer
Labels are not to be removed from any container or defaced in any manner.
Empty containers are not to be re-used to store other materials.
Employees should always read product labels prior to use.   Be aware of all potential hazards.  If further information is needed, check the SDS located in the clean room or designated area.
Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is necessary if a chemical can affect the skin, eyes, or lungs.Products exempted from labeling due to labeling requirements of other consumer, federal, environmental agencies or the FDA includes:
Consumer products
Distilled alcoholic beverages
Pesticides and anti-microbial pesticides (cold sterilants and disinfectants)
Medical devices and drugs

sections.heading: Safety Data Sheets (SDS)
sections.text:
An SDS must be filed on site for every hazardous chemical present in the workplace.  Most products come with an SDS enclosed or attached.  Whenever a new product comes into the workplace, the SDS that comes with it should be posted.
If an SDS does not come with the product, an Internet search, email, or letter of request for the SDS should be sent to the manufacturer by the Safety Officer.
When an SDS is received, the product should be placed on the Hazardous Chemicals List.  The following information should be identified:
Product (trade/brand name)
Hazardous chemical
Company (manufacturer)
Operation/area used
SDS is on file
The SDS should then be filed alphabetically by product name.
Treating Individuals with Suspected Contagious Disease or Tuberculosis:
The patient is taken to an exam room and kept separate from others.
Call for the Office Manager and/or physician.
The patient is given a mask.
The physician will examine the patient.
If suspected to be contagious, the patient will be sent to an appropriate facility as determined by the physician or designee.
The room will be closed, and not used again, until it has been cleaned entirely with an appropriate hospital-grade disinfectant.
Sample Letter or Email Request an SDS Sheet from a Manufacturer
Date:
To:	Manufacturer Name
Address
City, State, Zip, or
Email Address
RE:	Request for Safety Data Sheet(s)
Please provide the following Safety Data Sheet(s) for the following product(s) used in the
workplace:
It is my understanding that as the chemical manufacturer or importer, you are required to provide this information at the time of shipment, or upon request, under the OSHA Hazard Communication Standard, 29 CFR 1910.1200.  Please send this information to:
Safety Officer
Honolulu, Hawaii
It is important that I inform my employees of the potential physical or health hazards realized by working with hazardous chemicals.  Your cooperation and immediate response are appreciated.
Sincerely,
Safety Officer
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Hazard Communications Plan Overview

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to call every patient that has a procedure at the facility.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text: A designated employee will be responsible to contact the patient and/or patient’s care giver to ensure the patient is recovering within the guidelines of the procedure.

sections.heading: FORMS USED
sections.text:
•	Post Op Follow-Up Call
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Post Op Follow-Up Call

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to review and give each patient for every procedure performed post-procedure instructions to ensure the patient has the information needed that will assist in a positive outcome.

sections.heading: FORMS USED
sections.text:
Post-Operative Instructions for General Post Op Instructions
Post-Operative Instructions for Surgery Specific Post Op Instructions

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
At the pre-operative visit, the RN or designee will review the post-procedure instructions with the patient. The patient will sign an acknowledgement that they have reviewed it and understand them.
A copy of the post-operative instructions will be given to the patient and to the caregiver the day of surgery.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Post Procedure Instructions

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to see all surgical patients the day after surgery at the facility

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.text:
The surgeon or designated employee will see all surgical patients undergoing general anesthesia the day after surgery at the facility with the exception of patients undergoing septorhinoplasty. This will be recorded in the patient’s medical record.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Post-Op Visit

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: A pre-anesthesia evaluation will be performed by an anesthesia provider on every patient receiving anesthesia services and will be documented in the patient’s medical record. This evaluation, for elective surgery patients, will take place within 48 hours prior to pre-operative medication being administered, unless the circumstances of the situation would dictate otherwise. If the pre-anesthesia evaluation is performed by a CRNA, it must be reviewed and confirmed by the Physician performing the surgery.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Pre-Anesthesia Care Standards
Determine medical, anesthetic, drug, and allergy history.
Perform any examinations that would provide information that might assist in the decision regarding anesthetic risks and management. Indicate physical status (ASA definition).
Order or review pertinent diagnostic tests.
Identify potential anesthesia problems (e.g., difficult airway, ongoing infection, limited vascular access).
Obtain consultation, as necessary.
Formulate an anesthetic plan and discuss risk/benefits of the plan with the patient or the patient’s legal representative.
The pre-anesthesia medical record entry will ordinarily indicate the planned technique of anesthesia (i.e., general, spinal, regional or other). Exceptions to this can be made whenever the precise technique of anesthesia might be more appropriately determined in the operating suite where the precise immediate pre-operative status of the patient can be re-evaluated.
Subject: Anesthesia Services
Section: Pre-Anesthesia Evaluation

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to complete a surgical checklist before, during and after the procedure.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text: The Medical Director and/or the RN will determine who will be responsible for ensuring the checklist is completed on every patient that has a procedure at The Ferguson Clinic.  This completed checklist will be placed in the patient’s medical record.

sections.heading: FORMS USED
sections.text:
Pre-Operative Checklist
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Pre-Operative Checklist

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to communicate verbally and in writing the pre-procedure instructions to all patients for all procedures.
FORMS USED: General Pre-Op Instructions
General Pre-Op Instructions
Pre-Operative Shopping List
Consent for Surgery
Surgery Specific Consent (Informed Consent)

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Each patient will receive pre-operative instructions for each procedure that each patient will have performed. The Nurse or designee will review these instructions with the patient and will ask the patient to sign an acknowledgement that they read and understood each aspect of the pre-operative instructions.

sections.heading: PURPOSE/EXPECTED OUTCOMES
sections.text:
To provide guidelines to meet regulatory agency requirements and the medical staff rules and
regulations about Physician orders.

sections.heading: DEFINITIONS
sections.text:
Medical Record: Medical record where the documentation is retained in paper format.
Favorite Orders: A set of orders that each Physician can build based on individual preferences.
Order Set: A preprinted or electronic set of orders subject to approval by the provider. Order Sets may permit the inclusion of additional orders. Such tools may include a menu of medications or actions and/or a combination of medications and actions to be followed without amendment whenever the physician selects that order.
Preference(s) or Preference Cards: Preference cards identify individual Physician supply and
instrument preferences which do not require a Physician order.
Protocols: Flow process that generally includes branch points (if/then statements), which can be objective or subjective (requiring judgment), that describes the steps of care during a period of care continuum. Patient-care protocols are often interdisciplinary and describe the care and management of a broad patient care problem or issue.
Physician: D.O. or M.D. who can write orders.
Standing Order: An order approved by the applicable Medical Executive Committee that may be executed prior to an individual Physician order. Standing Orders are limited to a subset of orders regarding a patient condition or circumstance that are necessary for timely and efficient care.
Telephone/Verbal Order: An order not directly written by the physician. Telephone orders are those orders received verbally over the telephone. Verbal orders are orders given in a face-to-face situation.
Licensed Health Care Professional: Physician Assistant, Advanced Registered Nurse Practitioner, CRNA, RN, LPN.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: A physician order is required to treat a patient, discharge a patient, transfer a patient to another physician or care facility and for all tests, services, therapies, and procedures. Exceptions may be made when implementing an order based on a medical staff approved Standing Order or facility/department specific medical staff approved protocol.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
All orders, including electronic, verbal, telephone, printed or handwritten, will be documented as a permanent part of the patient’s medical record.
All orders must be dated and timed when documented or initiated. Outpatient orders are not required to be timed.
All orders (including verbal and telephone orders) must be authenticated by the responsible.
Physician within the time not to exceed 48 hours or as required by state regulations.
Authentication must include the time and date of the authentication.
Orders as originally written or initiated cannot be changed or modified once performed.
Orders containing unapproved abbreviations will be clarified with the responsible physician prior to initiating the order.
Clarified orders must be re-written or re-initiated as a new order.
Whenever possible, physician orders will be entered directly by the Physician into the patient’s medical record.
If the physician does not have the ability to access to the patient’s medical record to input orders themselves or if a delay in accepting the order could adversely affect patient care, telephone/verbal orders may be accepted by appropriate licensed health care professional. Faxed physician orders are acceptable provided they are signed, timed.
Licensed health care professional has the responsibility to question any order perceived as being harmful to the patient or not perceived to be a real order.
Orders that are not legible will be clarified with the responsible Physician before they are carried out.
Standing orders must be approved by the Medical Staff and Governing Body.
If a physician refuses to authenticate an order after an intervention has been done, the chain of command will be followed.
Late entries of physician orders are discouraged but will be accepted.
Patients shall be discharged only on the order of the attending or covering physician, except where the patient leaves against medical advice.
Recovery orders may be written/documented by appropriately credentialed physicians.
A “continue patient meds as at home” order is not acceptable.
Telephone and verbal orders may be dictated to one of the following practitioners, within their scope of practice, who must document and then read back the order to the physician for verification prior placing the order.
Nursing – telephone/verbal orders are taken only by registered nurses or licensed practical nurses.
A valid order must be received prior to performing any outpatient procedure, test, or service. It is the responsibility of the medical staff performing the service to ensure all elements are present. These include patient name, date, service to be provided, reason for service (i.e.) diagnosis or condition that supports medical necessity) and ordering physician signature.
Orders written or initiated by the physician licensed outside the United States (Mexico, Canada) will not be accepted.
Orders signed by office personnel and stamped orders will not be accepted.
Orders must be activated within 60 days of the date of the signed/authenticated order and are valid for the length of the ordered therapy or 12 months, whichever is shorter.
Orders must include information about the medical necessity or clinical indications for the service or procedure. A narrative description of medical necessity is preferable.
When the diagnostic reason for the service or procedure is not available and the physician is unavailable to provide such information, it is appropriate to obtain the information directly from the patient. However, an attempt should be made to confirm any information obtained from the patient by contacting the physician.
Faxed or original signed orders are acceptable provided all required elements are present and may be provided in any of the following formats:
Prescription forms.
Order sheets.
Office letterhead.
Office history and physical or progress notes including clear indication that an order is contained therein.
Medical Staff approved pre-printed order forms.

sections.heading: Order Sets
sections.text:
Pre-printed orders are acceptable for outpatient services as described above.
When pre-printed orders are used, they must be authenticated according to the applicable Medical Staff Rules and Regulations and/or policies.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Processing Physician Orders

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITY
