Continued proficiency testing will be performed annually
Section: Proficiency Testing

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to inform patients of the management process used to protect their Protected Health Information (PHI). The following forms will be used: Notice of Privacy Practices and Consent to Use and Disclosure of Protected Health Information.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) is a fundamental privacy document.  The requirements for its preparation and use are detailed in the Privacy Rule, Section 164.520.
A proper NPP will inform the patient of all the basic uses The Ferguson Clinic will make of a patient’s Protected Health Information (PHI) in the ordinary course of providing treatment, seeking payment for care to the patient and managing the practice’s health care operations.  The NPP also will inform the patient of other circumstances in which his/her PHI may be released, such as to comply with court orders, subpoenas, and government investigations.

sections.heading: The NPP advises patients of certain special rights they have
sections.text:
To revoke any authorization or consent they may have given to The Ferguson Clinic to authorize disclosures of their PHI.
To request special limits or conditions on the use of their PHI.
To receive communications from The Ferguson Clinic by more confidential means or at alternate locations.
To inspect and copy their PHI.
To amend their PHI.
This NPP should be acknowledged by all patients receiving service after the compliance date for the Privacy Rule of April 14, 2003. The Ferguson Clinic must make a good faith effort to obtain the patient’s acknowledgment of receipt of the NPP from the patient and/or his/her legal representative/caregiver.  If the patient is unable or unwilling to acknowledge receipt of the NPP, The Ferguson Clinic personnel will document on the NPP acknowledgement that he/she attempted to obtain this acknowledgment, but the patient would not or could not acknowledge its receipt.
The NPP will be conspicuously posted at The Ferguson Clinic and additional copies will be available to patients upon request.  If the NPP is ever revised, the revised NPP must be posted and the patients (under The Ferguson Clinic’ NPP) must be provided a copy of the new NPP.
If the Ferguson Clinic has a website the NPP will be posted on the website.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Protected Health Information (PHI)

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure that the surgical candidates are healthy both physically and mentally prior to performing cosmetic surgery.

sections.heading: Reasons for denying surgery are
sections.text:
Uncontrolled mental problems, such as depression
Unrealistic expectations
During the History and Physical the surgeon(s) will look for signs in demeanor, dress, and speech.
For example, the way a patient carries themselves. Are they confident with only reasonable levels of nervousness about the procedure? Do they seem anxious? Are they making eye contact when talking, or looking around the room or at the ground? Are they easily excitable or overly shy? How are they dressed? Are they dressed in an extremely revealing manner, or overly conservative? Each of these things give a good view of who this person is, and whether they may have underlying social and psychological issues that they may be seeking to fix through plastic surgery as with breast surgery.
Proper motivation for the procedure is essential.
The surgeons will evaluate the patient based on the following criteria:

sections.heading: Good reasons for cosmetic surgery include
sections.text:
Doing it for yourself, not others in your life.
The desire to feel younger due to discord with the way one feels and the way one looks; and dissatisfaction with aging (although with the realization that aging is natural.
Individuals consider the surgery for a decent amount of time, noting that most successful cosmetic surgery patients have considered having the procedure done.

sections.heading: Poor reasons for surgery
sections.text:
Doing it for another person.
Doing it to make oneself feel better after a break-up or death.
Doing it to boost one’s professional or career outlook
Patients who seek surgery to correct scars, burns, or other accident injuries must pass these evaluations showing that they understand what sort of realistic results they can expect.
If the surgeon determines that the patient requires a psychological evaluation, a referral will be given to the patient and surgery will not be scheduled until such time that the patient has been cleared by the mental health professional.
Documentation in the patient’s medical record will be completed by the surgeon.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Psychological Evaluations

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to maintain and clean the freezer based on CDC Guidelines.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
At least once each month, the coils and motor of each freezer will be examined and cleaned based on the manufacturer’s specifications:
The freezer will be unplugged, and a soft brush, cloth or vacuum cleaner will be used with an attachment to remove any dirt or dust from the surface of the coils.
If the motor is accessible it will also be cleaned using a soft brush, cloth, or vacuum cleaner with the attachment hose.
After the cleaning is complete, the freezer will be plugged in and documentation that the power was restored, and the temperature was maintained will be placed on the freezer temperature log as a note for that day.
Cleaning of the coils and motor will take place at the end of the day to ensure freezer is working during normal surgery times.
The refrigerator and freezer will be cleaned monthly to discourage bacterial and fungal growth:
Remove all medications and place them in another functioning unit or place in cooler ensuring temperature is maintained.
Unplug the refrigerator/freezer and wash all inside surfaces and shelves with warm, slightly soapy water.
Dry thoroughly.
Plug in refrigerator/freezer and turn the thermostat back to an appropriate setting. Wait for the unit to reach and stabilize at the proper temperature range before restocking with meds.

sections.heading: Check the Door Seal
sections.text:
Once a month while cleaning the unit, the integrity of the rubber-like door seal on each unit will be checked.
The seal should not be torn or brittle and there should be no gaps between the seal and the body of the unit when the door is closed.
The door should open and close properly and fit squarely against the body of the unit.
Hinges must be correctly adjusted.

sections.heading: Clean the Drain Pan
sections.text:
The drain pan is located at the bottom of the unit. It holds water that collects after the frost melts during the defrost cycle.
Water will evaporate.
Overtime it can smell or become moldy.
Detach kick plate and remove pan.
Clean pan with soapy water.
It is unnecessary to unplug the unit during this task.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Refrigerator/Freezer Cleaning per CDC Guidelines

file: Reporting to the State of Hawaii Department of Health.idml
title: Reporting to the Hawaii State Department of Health

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to report any disease or condition that is found to be contagious, infectious, communicable, and dangerous to the public health to the local health authorities of the State of Hawaii Department of Health.

sections.heading: REPORTING
sections.text:
Health care providers, day care providers, school personnel, day care operators, long-term care personnel, and laboratory personnel are required to report information about certain communicable diseases to the local health department.
The local health authority to be notified is the State of Hawaii Department of Health
Local health department personnel will follow up with the patient to collect additional information such as symptoms, risk factors, and contacts.
Information should be called or mailed to the local health department.

sections.heading: Information to be included in the report
sections.text:
The patient’s name
Age
Sex
Race
Ethnicity
Address
Phone number
Diagnosis
Physician’s name, address, and phone number, in case further information is needed
See Poster for list of diseases and timeframes to report.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Reporting to the State of Hawaii Department of Health

file: Resolution of Conflict in Care and Treatment Decisions.idml
title: Resolution of Conflict in Care and Treatment Decisions

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to resolve all patient/parent or legal guardian conflicts
whenever possible and to monitor significant patient/parent or legal guardian complaints.

sections.heading: PATIENT RIGHTS
sections.text:
Each patient’s cultural, psychosocial, spiritual, and personal values are respected.
Each patient’s relevant psychosocial, spiritual, and cultural values should be documented in the electronic medical record during the initial assessment.
During conversations with patients/parent or legal guardians, issues may arise which will entail moral or religious values (i.e., such as refusal to accept blood or blood products, or the need for ablative surgery which may impinge upon a patient’s religious beliefs).
Accordingly, all staff and Practitioners will be honest and open in pre-operative discussions with patients/parents or legal guardians concerning their needs and desires should untoward problems arise.
If medical standards impinge upon the moral or religious beliefs of a patient, the wishes of the patient and his/her rights will be followed.
When a patient or family member disagrees with a planned course of therapy or fails to follow a plan of care, documentation in the electronic medical record should reflect how these problems were resolved  PROCEDURES:
In instances where a patient refuses medical treatment that could be life threatening or cause substantial harm:
A letter must be written to the patient/parent or legal guardian outlining the discussion.
This letter should be sent to the patient by certified mail return receipt requested.
A copy of the letter and the certified mail receipt should be placed in the patient’s electronic medical record.
If the certified letter is returned to the office, it should be filed in the patient’s electronic medical record unopened.

sections.heading: If a procedure did not go exactly as planned, the physician should
sections.text:
Honestly and objectively present the results to the patient and/or family member.
Mention the risks that were discussed preceding the procedure.
Discuss future treatment options.
Give the patient time to vent their feelings.
Listen to the patient to optimize trust.

sections.heading: The physician should document in the Operative Report
sections.text:
An accurate, clear, and credible account of the events that took place.
Record those verbal discharge instructions were given, and to whom.
Place a copy of the written discharge instructions in the electronic medical record.
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Resolution of Conflict in Care and Treatment Decisions

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure that employees are aware of their right to work in a safe and healthy workplace.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
As an employee, you have the right to a safe and healthful workplace.  You have a right to know the hazards in the workplace and how these hazards are controlled.
Chemicals used in the medical office may be harmful under certain conditions.  Always read the label and follow manufacturer’s directions!

sections.heading: Physical Hazards
sections.text:
Fires may be caused by misuse of flammable chemicals
Explosions may be caused by a gas leak
Long term exposure to some chemicals may cause cancer

sections.heading: Types of Exposure
sections.text:
Skin Absorption	Avoid contact with skin and mucous membranes (wear gloves, face masks, 		eye ware)
Inhalation		Replace caps on bottles; and keep covers on glutaraldehyde (cold soak 				sterilization)
Ingestion		Never eat or drink in areas where chemicals are present

sections.heading: Common Hazardous Chemicals
sections.text:
Glutaraldehyde	Wear gloves and protective eyewear
Disinfectants	Wear gloves; avoid skin contact
Tissue/specimen fixatives : Wear gloves; avoid skin contact
(formalin)
Gases (oxygen)	Avoid sparks, flames, contact with grease or lubricants.
Radiographic Chemicals: Work in ventilated area; use heavy rubber gloves/eyewear.
Gypsum Products (for casts): Work in ventilated area; use protective eye ware
Mercury in Manometers	Cleanup requires commercial mercury cleanup kit

sections.heading: Emergency Procedures
sections.text:
Know where emergency equipment is kept
Fire Extinguishers are checked monthly
Eye Wash Station are checked weekly
Safety Data Sheets are present for all chemicals
Compressed gas cylinders are checked monthly for leaks; and are stored in a cart or chained to the wall

Section: Safety Management Program

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of the Safety Management Program is to develop, coordinate, manage and evaluate safety activities within the organization.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of the The Ferguson Clinic to have a Safety Management Program that is designed to provide a physical environment free of hazards and to manage staff activities that will reduce the risk of property damage, staff, patient, and visitor injury.

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text: This plan applies to the facility, staff, patients, and visitors of The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To develop policies and procedures that provide for a safe, accessible, effective, and efficient environment that is consistent with our mission and complies with all safety-related regulatory agency standards, and local, state, and federal laws.
To document and investigate all incidents of property damage, staff, patient, or visitor injury.
To conduct risk assessments that pro-actively evaluate the impact of buildings, grounds, equipment, occupants, and internal physical systems on patient and public safety.
To conduct hazard surveillance including response to safety recalls.
To enforce and monitor current safe work practices and work conditions for patients, staff, physicians, and visitors.
To provide safety education to all employees of The Ferguson Clinic.
To develop monitors of performance and indicators to identify opportunities to minimize risk and improve safety performance.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
The Office Manager is responsible for the designation of the Safety Officer, and it is the responsibility of the Governing Body to approve such designation.
The Safety Officer is responsible for the development, implementation, and monitoring of the safety management program.
The Medical Director and Governing Body will receive an annual evaluation of all Environment Care management plans from the Office Manager in collaboration with the Safety Officer.
The Office Manager is responsible for acting on recommendations from the Safety Officer.
The designated Safety Officer at this facility is determined by the Medical Director.  The duties include, but are not limited to:
Correct situations posing an immediate threat to life (such as a chemical spill, gas leak, malfunctioning equipment) or threat of damage to building or equipment.
Conduct a hazard surveillance checklist at least two (2) times a year to detect code violations, hazards, incorrect work practices and procedures.
Train and distribute information on safety, security, hazardous materials and waste, emergency preparedness, fire prevention, equipment, and utilities.
Plan, implement, and document all disaster and emergency preparedness and fire drills.
Review and investigate all incidents, and report findings and actions taken to the QIP Committee.
Receive copies of all product/medication recalls and track responses.
Receive alert notices from vendors or the US Food and Drug Administration and other safety recall bulletins.
Coordinate the recall or modification of equipment, pharmaceuticals, instruments, and supplies, and maintain documentation of these activities.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Safety Management Program using established indicators, and report findings to the QIP Committee.
Develop and recommend new procedures and approaches to safety and loss prevention based on reports of incidents, accidents, and other safety information.
Review as necessary, but at least every two years, how safety policies and procedures are distributed, practiced, and enforced.
Annually prepare a written report of the Safety Management Plan in terms of its objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness for the Governing Body.

sections.heading: Employee Responsibilities
sections.text:
Each employee is expected to obey all safety rules, wear the appropriate personal protective equipment, and exercise caution in all work activities.
Employees are expected to participate in the Safety Management Program and observe all established precautionary measures.
Employees must immediately report any unsafe condition to the Medical Director.
Unsafe conditions must be documented on an incident report form and given to the Medical Director and the QIP Committee for review and action plan.

sections.heading: PROCESSES OF THE SAFETY MANAGEMENT PLAN
Risk Assessment
sections.text:
The Office Manager with the QIP Committee is responsible for managing the risk assessment process.
The purpose of performing pro-active risk assessments is to evaluate the impact of proposed changes to new or existing areas of the facility (i.e., whenever there is construction, equipment installation, or a plan for the development of new services).
Results of the risk assessment process are used to create new or revise existing policies and procedures, identify issues to be included in staff training, or monitor programs.

sections.heading: Hazard Surveillance
sections.text:
The Office Manager, or designee, will perform a hazard surveillance survey bi-annually to evaluate the impact of buildings, grounds, equipment, occupants, and internal physical systems on patient and public safety.  The hazard surveillance survey inspection will review the following for safety issues and practices:
Life Safety Code compliance.
Infection control issues.
Workers’ compensation inspections.
Clinical issue.
Information obtained during the hazard surveillance tour is to be analyzed and an action plan established to correct any deficiencies, and the results of the survey are to be reported to the QIP Committee.

sections.heading: Incident Reporting and Investigation
sections.text:
Staff is to notify the Office Manager about any of the following incidents:
Property damage.
Occupational illness.
Staff, patient, or visitor injuries.
Safety issues such as: needle sticks, falls, ergonomics, medication errors, chemical spills and exposures, Tuberculosis, and latex sensitivity.
The Office Manager will investigate the incidents, document the findings and any actions taken, and reports it to the QIP Committee.
The information is evaluated for cause, type, and location to identify trends and patterns that can be used to improve worker safety.

sections.heading: Orientation and Education Program
sections.text: The Safety Officer or designee will train staff during orientation, when an employee changes job positions or work areas, and annually thereafter.

sections.heading: Plans for the orientation and continuing education of staff include
sections.text:
General safety practices.
Area-specific safety.
Specific job-related hazards.
Provision of safety related information.
An annual review of training can be met by classroom activities, one-on-one discussions, or through the completion of a self-study packet that includes information on general/worker safety.
All training is documented in employee personnel files.

sections.heading: Policy and Procedure Development
sections.text:
The Office Manager and Medical Director, in collaboration with the QIP Committee and the Safety Officer, are responsible for the development and review of all safety policies and procedures.
Employees are responsible for following safe work policies and practicing safe work procedures.
All safety policies and procedures are reviewed by the Medical Director and Governing Body every year to evaluate their relevance and effectiveness.

sections.heading: Grounds and Equipment
sections.text:
The building and grounds are the responsibility of the Safety Officer and Building Property Management Company. This responsibility includes the maintenance of the facility and its various systems.
The Office Manager is responsible for assuring the performance of these services.
A program is established to maintain floors, walls, ceilings, exterior building structure, roadway, sidewalks, trash compactor, rodent control etc.
Parking lots and sidewalks are to be kept clear of snow and ice, as needed, to maintain a safe environment for patients, staff, and visitors.

sections.heading: Performance Monitoring
sections.text:
The Office Manager is responsible for coordinating the performance-monitoring process for the Safety Management Program and the other Environment of Care Management Plans.
The Office Manager and QIP Committee monitor performance standards.
Summaries of findings and recommendations, based on trends, performance measures, and performance improvement activities, will be documented quarterly by the QIP Committee.
Specific information will be communicated to staff when issues or opportunities to reduce the risk of safety hazards exist.

sections.heading: Performance Standards relate to the following
sections.text:
Staff knowledge and skills.
Level of staff participation.
Monitoring and inspection activities.
Emergency and incident reporting.
Inspection, preventive maintenance, and testing of equipment.

sections.heading: Annual Review
sections.text:
The Office Manager and Safety Officer, in collaboration with the QIP Committee, are responsible for the annual review of the Safety Management Program’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
The annual review will be compiled at the end of the year based on information from a variety of sources including, but not limited to incident reports on occupational illness, worker safety, patient and visitor injuries, product safety recall notices, hazard surveillance surveys, pro-active risk assessments, staff orientation and training, QIP Committee minutes, performance monitoring activities, and other summaries of activities, including the findings of regulatory agencies.
The annual review will be presented to the Medical Director and Governing Body in a narrative report that covers the Safety Management Program’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Safety Management Program

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that all staff will be attentive to appropriate safety precautions.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Use chemicals only with adequate ventilation.
Containers should be closed when not in use.
Remove all contaminated clothing carefully and disposes of properly.
Wash thoroughly after working with corrosives.
Report spills or leaks immediately to your supervisor.
Broken glass should never be picked up by hand, use tongs, broom, and dustpan.
Follow proper cleanup procedures.
Obtain immediate medical attention for exposure.
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Safety Practices

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to designate a scope of practice for the Center and its staff.

sections.heading: Scope of Practice is
sections.text:

sections.heading: PROTOCOL
sections.text:
For the duration of the Covid-19 pandemic or until further notification in order to promptly identify and manage providers and staff with undiagnosed respiratory symptoms or a Covid-19 diagnosis, the following actions should be implemented:
The signs, symptoms, and risk factors associated with COVID-19 illness:
Per the CDC, people with COVID-19 have had a wide range of symptoms reported – ranging from mild symptoms to severe illness.

sections.heading: These nine symptoms may appear 2-14 days after exposure to the virus
sections.text:
Fever (≥100.4oF or higher)
Cough
Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
Chills
Repeated shaking with chills
Muscle pain
Headache
Sore throat
New loss of taste or smell

sections.heading: Risk Factors for Severe Disease
sections.text: Because COVID-19 is a new disease, more work is needed to better understand the risk factors for severe illness or complications.

sections.heading: Potential risk factors that have been identified to date include
sections.text:
Age
Race/ethnicity
Gender
Some medical conditions
Use of certain medications
Poverty and crowding
Certain occupations
Pregnancy
Additional research will help us confirm if these are risk factors for severe COVID-19 illness and determine if there are other factors that increase a person’s risk.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.text:
All protocols and procedures in the Covid-19 Infection Prevention and Control Policy will be adhered to at all times.
Protocols for Exposure with Suspected or Confirmed COVID-19
Per the state of Hawaii Department of Health guidelines based upon the CDC’s Interim Guidance for Implementing Safety Practices for Critical Infrastructure Workers Who May Have Had Exposure to a Person with Suspected or Confirmed COVID-19 and to ensure continuity of operations of essential functions, CDC advises that critical infrastructure workers may be permitted to continue work following potential exposure to COVID-19, provided they remain asymptomatic and additional precautions are implemented to protect them and the community.
A potential exposure means being a household contact or having close contact within 6 feet of an individual with confirmed or suspected COVID-19. The timeframe for having contact with an individual includes the period of time of 48 hours before the individual became symptomatic.
Critical Infrastructure workers who have had an exposure but remain asymptomatic should adhere to the following practices prior to and during their work shift:
Pre-Screen: Employee’s, contractors and consultants will be required to take their temperature and assess symptoms prior to starting work. Ideally, temperature checks should happen before the individual enters the facility.
Conduct daily COVID-19 symptoms checks of employees, contractors, consultants, suppliers, customers, and visitors entering the worksite; for example, use a brief questionnaire.
Per the Recommended Hawaii Department of Health protocols based upon CDC strategies for employees:

sections.heading: Sick employees need to stay home
sections.text:
Employees who have symptoms of acute respiratory illness need stay home and not come to work until they are free of fever (100.4° F [37.8° C] or greater using an oral thermometer), signs of a fever, and any other symptoms for at least 24 hours, without the use of fever-reducing medication (Tylenol, Ibuprofen/Advil) or other symptom-altering medicines (e.g. cough suppressants). Employees should notify their supervisor and stay home if they are sick.
For any staff member or provider: if you are experiencing flu-like symptoms with cough, fever (greater than 100.4), lower respiratory illness (cough, shortness of breath and difficulty breathing) or you are feeling ill, or if you have been in contact with anyone with a confirmed or suspected coronavirus infection or someone who is feeling ill or If you have had close contact with someone who has traveled outside the country or to a large metropolitan with spikes in positive COVID-19 residents, or someone who has taken a cruise within the past two months, then we are asking you to stay home and take care of yourself at this time and not return to work until you no longer have a fever or are taking medication to control a fever for a minimum of four days and are asymptomatic.
Furthermore, if you have been in contact with anyone with a confirmed or suspected coronavirus infection or you have been contacted by the Health Department as being in contact with a confirmed coronavirus infection, you are fully vaccinated, and followed appropriate infection control as outlined in the proceeding protocols, then you may continue with your work responsibilities and monitor for flulike symptoms such as cough, fever, lower respiratory illness, or you are feeling ill.  If symptoms develop you will be sent home and instructed to follow the protocol for suspected Covid-19 infection.  If you have not been fully vaccinated you must quarantine for a minimum of 6 days and monitor for flulike symptoms such as cough, fever, lower respiratory illness, or you are feeling ill.  If symptoms develop you will follow the protocol for suspected Covid-19 infection.  You may return to work if you remain asymptomatic for a minimum of 6 days and present with a negative PCR Covid-19 test result.
Regular Monitoring: As long as the employee doesn’t have a temperature or symptoms, they should self-monitor under the supervision of their employer’s protocols.
Wear a Mask: The employee should wear a face mask at all times while in the workplace for 14 days after last exposure. Employers can issue facemasks or can approve employees’ supplied cloth face coverings in the event of shortages.
Social Distance: The employee should maintain 6 feet and practice social distancing as work duties permit in the workplace.
Disinfect and Clean Workspaces: Clean and disinfect all areas such as offices, bathrooms, common areas, shared electronic equipment routinely.

sections.heading: In addition
sections.text:
Employees should not share headsets or other objects that are near mouth or nose.
Employers should increase the frequency of cleaning commonly touched surfaces.
Employees should physically distance when they take breaks together. Stagger breaks and don’t congregate in the break room, and don’t share food or utensils.
Surfaces in your workspace will be cleaned and disinfected per the CDC guidelines in the Cleaning and Disinfecting Policy daily by the workspace user and in exam rooms, after each patient and throughout the day.
If an employee becomes sick during the day, they will be sent home immediately.
Information on persons who had contact with the ill employee during the time the employee had symptoms and 2 days prior to symptoms will be compiled. Others at the facility with close contact within 6 feet of the employee during this time would be considered exposed.

sections.heading: Procedures to Address Sick Employees
sections.text:
Staff who are sick will to stay home or go home if they feel or appear sick.
CDC cleaning guidelines in the Cleaning and Disinfecting Policy will be followed to deep clean after reports of an employee with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 illness.
Unexposed staff will stay away from areas being deep cleaned.
CDC - Monitor and Manage Healthcare Personnel
As part of routine practice, HCP should be asked to regularly monitor themselves for fever and symptoms of COVID-19.
HCP should stay home when they are ill.
If HCP develop fever (T≥100.0oF) or symptoms consistent with COVID-19* while at work they should keep their cloth face covering or facemask on, inform their supervisor, and leave the workplace.
Screen all HCP at the beginning of their shift for fever and symptoms consistent with COVID-19*
Actively take their temperature and document absence of symptoms consistent with COVID-19*. If they are ill, have them keep their cloth face covering or facemask on and leave the workplace.
*Fever is either measured temperature >100.0oF or subjective fever. Note that fever may be intermittent or may not be present in some individuals, such as those who are elderly, immunosuppressed, or taking certain medications (e.g., NSAIDs). Clinical judgement should be used to guide testing of individuals in such situations. Respiratory symptoms consistent with COVID-19 are cough, shortness of breath, and sore throat. Medical evaluation may be warranted for lower temperatures (<100.0oF) or other symptoms (e.g., muscle aches, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain headache, runny nose, fatigue) based on assessment by occupational health. Additional information about clinical presentation of patients with COVID-19 is available.
CDC - Return to Work Criteria for HCP with Confirmed or Suspected COVID-19
Use the Test-based strategy as the preferred method for determining when HCP may return to work in healthcare settings:

sections.heading: Test-based strategy. Exclude from work until
sections.text:
Resolution of fever without the use of fever-reducing medications and
Improvement in respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath), and
Negative results of an FDA Emergency Use Authorized molecular assay for COVID-19 from at least two consecutive nasopharyngeal swab specimens collected ≥24 hours apart (total of two negative specimens). See Interim Guidelines for Collecting, Handling, and Testing Clinical Specimens for 2019 Novel Coronavirus (2019-nCoV).
If the Test-based strategy cannot be used, the Non-test-based strategy may be used for determining when HCP may return to work in healthcare settings:
Non-test-based strategy. Exclude from work until
At least 3 days (72 hours) have passed since recovery defined as resolution of fever without the use of fever-reducing medications and improvement in respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath); and,
At least 7 days have passed since symptoms first appeared
HCP with laboratory-confirmed COVID-19 who have not had any symptoms should be excluded from work until 10 days have passed since the date of their first positive COVID-19 diagnostic test assuming they have not subsequently developed symptoms since their positive test.
If HCP had COVID-19 ruled out and have an alternate diagnosis (e.g., tested positive for influenza), criteria for return to work should be based on that diagnosis.
Return to Work Practices and Work Restrictions

sections.heading: After returning to work, HCP should
sections.text:
Wear a facemask for source control at all times while in the healthcare facility until all symptoms are completely resolved or until 14 days after illness onset, whichever is longer. A facemask instead of a cloth face covering should be used by these HCP for source control during this time period while in the facility. After this time period, these HCP should revert to their facility policy regarding universal source control during the pandemic.
A face mask for source control does not replace the need to wear an N95 or higher-level respirator (or other recommended PPE) when indicated, including when caring for patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19.
Of note, N95 or other respirators with an exhaust valve might not provide source control.
Be restricted from contact with severely immunocompromised patients (e.g., transplant, hematology-oncology) until 14 days after illness onset.
Self-monitor for symptoms and seek re-evaluation from occupational health if respiratory symptoms recur or worsen.
When reviewing and implementing strategies addressed in the CDC guidance, the differences between cloth face coverings, facemasks and respirators are important to keep in mind:
Cloth face covering: Textile (cloth) cover that are intended to keep the person wearing one from spreading respiratory secretions when talking, sneezing, or coughing. They are not PPE and it is uncertain whether cloth face coverings protect the wearer. Guidance on design, use, and maintenance of cloth face coverings is available.
Facemask: Facemasks are PPE and are often referred to as surgical masks or procedure masks. Use facemasks according to product labeling and local, state, and federal requirements. FDA-cleared surgical masks are designed to protect against splashes and sprays and are prioritized for use when such exposures are anticipated, including surgical procedures. Facemasks that are not regulated by FDA, such as some procedure masks, which are typically used for isolation purposes, may not provide protection against splashes and sprays.
Monitor and Manage Ill and Exposed Health Care Personnel
Facilities and organizations providing health care should implement sick leave policies for HCP that are non-punitive, flexible and consistent with public health guidance.
As part of routine practice, HCP should be asked to regularly monitor themselves for fever and symptoms of COVID-19.
HCP should be reminded to stay home when they are ill.
If HCP develop fever (≥100.4oF or higher) or symptoms consistent with COVID-19* while at work, they should keep their cloth face covering or facemask on, inform their supervisor and leave the workplace.
Screen all HCP at the beginning of their shift for fever and symptoms consistent with COVID-19*
Actively take their temperature and document absence of symptoms consistent with COVID-19*. If they are ill, have them keep their cloth face covering or facemask on and leave the workplace.
*Fever is either measured temperature >100.0oF or subjective fever. Note that fever may be intermittent or may not be present in some individuals, such as those who are elderly, immunosuppressed or taking certain medications (e.g., NSAIDs). Clinical judgement should be used to guide testing of individuals in such situations. Respiratory symptoms consistent with COVID-19 are cough, shortness of breath and sore throat. Medical evaluation may be warranted for lower temperatures (<100.0oF) or other symptoms (e.g., muscle aches, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain headache, runny nose, fatigue) based on assessment by occupational health. Additional information about clinical presentation of patients with COVID-19 is available.
Return to Work Criteria for HCP with Confirmed or Suspected COVID-19
Use the Test-based strategy as the preferred method for determining when HCP may return to work in health care settings:
Test-based strategy. Exclude from work until
Resolution of fever without the use of fever-reducing medications, and
Improvement in respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath), and
Negative results of an FDA Emergency Use Authorized molecular assay for COVID-19.
HCP with laboratory-confirmed COVID-19 who have not had any symptoms should be excluded from work until 10 days have passed since the date of their first positive COVID-19 diagnostic test assuming they have not subsequently developed symptoms since their positive test.
Non-test-based strategy. Exclude from work until
At least 3 days (72 hours) have passed since recovery defined as resolution of fever without the use of fever-reducing medications and improvement in respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath), and
At least 7 days have passed since symptoms first appeared.
Return to Work Practices and Work Restrictions

sections.heading: After returning to work, HCP should
sections.text:
Wear a facemask for source control at all times while in the health care facility until all symptoms are completely resolved or until 14 days after illness onset, whichever is longer. A facemask instead of a cloth face covering should be used by these HCP for source control during this time period while in the facility. After this time period, these HCP should revert to their facility policy regarding universal source control during the pandemic.
A facemask for source control does not replace the need to wear an N95 or higher-level respirator (or other recommended PPE) when indicated, including when caring for patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19.
Of note, N95 or other respirators with an exhaust valve might not provide source control.
Be restricted from contact with severely immunocompromised patients (e.g., transplant, hematology-oncology) until 14 days after illness onset.
Self-monitor for symptoms and seek re-evaluation if respiratory symptoms recur or worsen.
Developing plans to allow asymptomatic HCP who have had an unprotected exposure to the virus that causes COVID-19 to continue to work.
These HCP should still report temperature and absence of symptoms each day before starting work. These HCP should wear a facemask (for source control) while at work for 14 days after the exposure event. A facemask instead of a cloth face covering should be used by these HCP for source control during this time period while in the facility. After this time period, these HCP should revert to their facility policy regarding universal source control during the pandemic.
A facemask for source control does not replace the need to wear an N95 or higher-level respirator (or other PPE) when indicated, including for the care of patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19.
If HCP develop even mild symptoms consistent with COVID-19, they must cease patient care activities and notify their supervisor or occupational health services prior to leaving work. These individuals should be prioritized for testing.
Developing criteria to determine which HCP with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 (who are well enough to work) could return to work in a health care setting before meeting all return to work criteria — if shortages continue despite other mitigation strategies.

sections.heading: Considerations include
sections.text:
The type of HCP shortages that need to be addressed.
Where HCP are in the course of their illness (e.g., viral shedding appears to be higher earlier in the course of illness).
The types of symptoms they are experiencing (e.g., persistent fever).
Their degree of interaction with patients and other HCP in the facility. For example, are they working in telemedicine services, providing direct patient care, or working in a satellite unit reprocessing medical equipment?
The type of patients they care for (e.g., immunocompromised patients).
As part of planning, health care facilities (in collaboration with risk management) should create messaging for patients and HCP about actions that will be taken to protect them from exposure to SARS-CoV-2 if HCP with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 are allowed to work.
If not already done, allow asymptomatic HCP who have had an unprotected exposure to the virus that causes COVID-19 to continue to work.
These HCP should still report temperature and absence of symptoms each day before starting work. These HCP should wear a facemask (for source control) while at work for 14 days after the exposure event. A facemask instead of a cloth face covering should be used by these HCP for source control during this time period while in the facility. After this time period, these HCP should revert to their facility policy regarding universal source control during the pandemic.
A facemask for source control does not replace the need to wear an N95 or higher-level respirator (or other PPE) when indicated, including for the care of patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19.
Of note, N95 or other respirators with an exhaust valve might not provide source control.
If HCP develop even mild symptoms consistent with COVID-19, they must cease patient care activities and notify their supervisor or occupational health services prior to leaving work. These individuals should be prioritized for testing.
If shortages continue despite other mitigation strategies, consider implementing criteria to allow HCP with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 who are well enough to work but have not met all return to work criteria to work. If HCP are allowed to work before meeting all criteria, they should be restricted from contact with severely immunocompromised patients (e.g., transplant, hematology-oncology) and facilities should consider prioritizing their duties in the following order:
If not already done, allow HCP with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 to perform job duties where they do not interact with others (e.g., patients or other HCP), such as in telemedicine services.
Allow HCP with confirmed COVID-19 to provide direct care only for patients with confirmed COVID-19, preferably in a cohort setting.
Allow HCP with confirmed COVID-19 to provide direct care for patients with suspected COVID-19.
As a last resort, allow HCP with confirmed COVID-19 to provide direct care for patients without suspected or confirmed COVID-19.
If HCP are permitted to return to work before meeting all return to work criteria, they should still adhere to all return to work practices and work restrictions recommendations described in that guidance. These include:
Wear a facemask for source control at all times while in the health care facility until all symptoms are completely resolved or until 14 days after illness onset, whichever is longer. A facemask instead of a cloth face covering should be used by these HCP for source control during this time period while in the facility. After this time period, these HCP should revert to their facility policy regarding universal source control during the pandemic.
A facemask for source control does not replace the need to wear an N95 or higher-level respirator (or other PPE) when indicated, including when caring for patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19.
Of note, N95 or other respirators with an exhaust valve might not provide source control.
They should be reminded that in addition to potentially exposing patients, they could also expose their co-workers.
Masks
Facemasks should be worn even when they are in non-patient care areas such as breakrooms.
If they must remove their facemask, for example, in order to eat or drink, they should separate themselves from others.
Subject: Covid-19
Section: Staff, Contractor and Consultant Screening, Illness and Testing

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to facilitate the reporting of and response to security incidents that have the potential to allow unauthorized access, disruption, damage, or theft of information resources. Incident response shall provide reasonable methods of limiting the impact on The Ferguson Clinic’s systems due to an incident and for facilitating the progressive and successful investigation of an incident.

sections.heading: SECURITY INCIDENT DEFINED
sections.text:
A security incident is defined as the attempted or successful unauthorized access, use, disclosure, modification, or destruction of information or interference with system operations in an information system.
Security incidents may be caused by a failure of a security mechanism or an attempted or threatened breach of these mechanisms.
Security incidents may be recognized by individuals observing the incident, learning of the incident later, finding the results of an incident, or by software monitoring and alerting.
Examples of information security incidents include, but are not limited to:
Unauthorized disclosures whether intentional or inadvertent of ePHI and PHI.
Unauthorized use, alteration, reproduction, or destruction of ePHI and PHI.
Unauthorized use of user ID’s, passwords, or access codes.
Failure to protect User IDs, passwords, or access codes (e.g., sharing codes, posting at workstation, etc.).
Unauthorized access to the computer workstation.
Indications of a computer virus.
Theft or tampering of computer equipment.

sections.heading: REPORTING
sections.text:
Breaches in information security will be reported in writing to the Privacy Officer. Reports may be made in writing directly to the Privacy Officer or through a secure and confidential incident reporting method established for reporting such incidents (e.g., email, phone call, and in-person).
If a The Ferguson Clinic employee is uncertain as to whether an incident is a risk to information security, he/she should report the incident.
Security risks may not be intentionally covered up or hidden by anyone aware of the risk.
All personnel will be given the name of the Privacy Officer and shown where the blank incident reports are kept.
All incidents will be reported to the Medical Director.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Security Incident Management

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Security Management Plan

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of the Security Management Plan is to develop, coordinate, manage and evaluate potential security risk and threats to staff, patients, visitors, and property.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of the The Ferguson Clinic to establish and maintain a secure environment to protect property, staff, patients, and visitors from harm.

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text: The Security Management Plan applies to all property, staff, patients, and visitors of the The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To address security issues concerning patients, visitors, personnel, and property.
To report and investigate all security incidents involving patients, visitors, personnel, or property.
To provide identification, as appropriate, for all patients, visitors, and staff.
To control access to sensitive areas.
RESPONSIBILITIES:The Safety Officer is responsible for the development, implementation, and monitoring of the Security Management Plan.

sections.heading: PROCESSES OF THE SECURITY PLAN
sections.text:
Identification of Patients, Visitors and Staff
Identification badges will be worn by staff.
Patients and visitors will gain access to the facility from a staff person.

sections.heading: Access to Sensitive Areas
sections.text:
Entry into the Procedure Room is restricted to authorized personnel only.
Valuables of patients undergoing surgery will be secured.
Security Issues:Staff is responsible for ensuring that all patient charts are safeguarded.
Access to computerized patient records is governed by security codes.
Medications and prescription pads are secured through lock or staff supervision.
Controlled drugs are received, stored, dispensed, and secured.
Staff members are encouraged to leave with other individuals after dark.

sections.heading: Incident Reporting and Investigation
sections.text:
All thefts, property losses and security incidents and concerns are documented and reported to the Office Manager, Safety Officer and Medical Director.
The Safety Officer is responsible for addressing security concerns and following up on any security incident.
Incidents involving criminal activity will be reported to the police.
The Office Manager ensures the incident is documented on an Incident Report form and reported to the QIP Committee.

sections.heading: Orientation and Education Program
sections.text:
The Safety Officer or designee is responsible for the training of staff during orientation and for providing continuing education on the following:
Processes for minimizing security risks for personnel in security-sensitive areas.
Emergency procedures followed during security incidents.
Reporting security incidents involving patients, visitors, personnel, and property.
Training can be met by classroom activities, one-on-one discussions or through the completion of a self-study packet on security issues.
All training is documented in employee personnel files.

sections.heading: Performance Monitoring
sections.text: The Office Manager, Safety Officer and QIP Committee monitor performance standard.

sections.heading: Performance Standards relate to the following
sections.text:
Staff knowledge and skill regarding security issues.
Level of staff participation in security management activities.
Security monitoring and inspection activities.
Emergency and incident reporting procedures that specify when and to whom reports are communicated.
Inspection, preventive maintenance, and testing of equipment.
Summaries of findings and recommendations, based on trends, performance measures, and performance improvement activities will be documented quarterly by the QIP Committee; and
Specific information will be communicated to staff when issues or opportunities to enhance security exist.

sections.heading: Annual Review
sections.text:
The Safety Officer and the Office Manager is responsible for the annual review of the Security Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
The annual review will be compiled at the end of the year based on information from a variety of sources including, but not limited to security incidents, staff orientation and training, QIP Committee minutes, performance monitoring activities, and other summaries of activities, including the findings of regulatory agencies.
The annual review will be presented to the Medical Director and Governing Body in a narrative report that covers the Security Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Security Management Plan

file: Selection and Use of Packaging Materials.idml
title: Selection and Use of Packaging Materials

sections.text:
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Selection and Use of Packaging Materials

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that packaging materials should ensure sterility of package contents until opened for use and should permit removal of contents without contamination.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text: Materials used for packaging of sterile supplies should permit the sterilization process to take place and should provide delivery of the contents without contamination.

sections.heading: Packaging Systems Should
sections.text:
Provide adequate seal integrity and be tamper-proof.
Provide an adequate barrier to particulate matter.
Withstand physical conditions of the sterilization process.
Provide an adequate barrier to fluids.
Permit adequate air removal.
Allow penetration and removal of sterilant.
Protect package contents from physical damage.
Resist tears and punctures.
Be free of toxic ingredients.
Be low lint; and
Be used in accordance with the manufacturers’ written instructions.
Packaging systems should be compatible with the steam sterilization process.
Packaging systems for steam sterilization should allow adequate air removal, steam penetration, and adequate drying.
Woven packaging should be processed to maintain the properties required for sterilization.
Woven packaging should be maintained at room temperature.
Laundering reusable fabrics provides the fibers with a relatively normal state of hydration before sterilization.
Packages should be sequentially double-wrapped when using woven and non-woven packaging
materials.
Loss of sterility of package contents is considered event-related, not time-related, and is dependent in part on the type of packaging used.

sections.heading: Event-related factors include the following
sections.text:
Frequency and method of handling.
Storage area conditions such as location, space, open/closed shelving, temperature, humidity, dust, insect, flooding, vermin, etc.
Packages wrapped in reusable fabrics or single-use disposable wrappers should be double-wrapped sequentially. Materials should be used according to manufacturers’ recommendations.
The sequential-wrapping method, covering the item once then wrapping it again, provides an improved barrier and allows for aseptic presentation.
Package integrity should be maintained.
Wrappers of all types should be free of holes.
Peel-packaging materials should be resistant to tears and punctures and should not delaminate when opened.
Contamination of sterile contents can occur with ports of entry including holes, breaks, or ruptures in packages.
Peel-packaging materials should provide a seal of proven integrity and not allow for resealing.
Seal integrity relies on bonding the plastic portion to the paper portion to prevent contamination.
Reference:  “Selection and Use of Packaging Materials,” Association of Operating Room Nurses, Standards and Recommended Practices.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Selection and Use of Packaging Materials

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Selection of Gowns and Drapes
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Selection of Gowns and Drapes

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to establish guidelines that address surgical attire and drapes used at the operative site.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Gowns and drapes should be made of materials that minimize the passage of micro-organisms from non-sterile to sterile areas.
Materials should be resistant to penetration by blood and other liquids.
Micro-organisms pass from non-sterile to sterile areas by the passage of liquids.  This is caused by wicking and pressure.
Bacterial passage causes contamination if wicking occurs from a sterile to non-sterile surface.
Reusable fabrics should maintain barrier quality through multiple laundering and sterilizations.
Reusable materials with heat-applied vulcanized patches can be effectively sterilized, patched fabric.
Laundering will eventually impair barrier quality of muslin.  Tests have shown that materials will lose their barrier quality after laundering and sterilizing.
Steam sterilizing and laundering swells cotton.
Drying and ironing will shrink cotton.
Drapes and gowns are to be compatible with the sterilization process since the primary function of the drapes and gowns is to define and preserve the sterile field during surgery by isolating the surgical site and prevent microbes in contaminated areas from migrating into aseptic areas.
Gowns and gloves should be resistant to tears, puncture, strain, and abrasion.
Quantitative data should be available to show that materials provide an effective barrier to microbes.
Stockinet cuffs of the gown should be covered by gloves during the procedure.
Reprocessing and/or reuse of single use items are not recommended.
Unused disposable gowns and drapes should not be re-sterilized unless the manufacturer provides written instructions for reprocessing.
Impermeability under the condition of use should be of primary concern when evaluating drapes and gowns.
Data should be available about the ability of each material to prevent bacterial transfer in various physiologic media in relation to variables of time, hydrostatic pressure, and physical deformation.
Gowns and drapes should be made of materials that are safe and comfortable for use in the
Operating Room environment.
All types of gowning and draping materials should be considered potentially flammable, only needing a combination of circumstances to result in a fire.
Materials should be as lint free as possible to reduce the dissemination of particles into wounds and the environment.
Lint may pose potential threats to patient health, such as foreign body reactions, embolism, and as a vehicle for transmission of micro-organisms.
Materials should maintain an aseptic barrier when used according to manufacturers’ directions.
Aseptic technique may be compromised by barrier materials that allow shifting of drapes and/or bacterial penetration.
These guidelines are written to present additional information to be used when implementing the preceding policy.
The ability to resist penetration of moist contamination is not always compatible with other factors considered desirable, such as comfort, economy, safety, and aesthetic appeal.
Materials should be non-abrasive and free of toxic ingredients, non-fast dyes, and noxious odors.
Materials should be non-glare and of a color that minimizes distortion from reflected light.
Gowns should fit and allow freedom of movement.
Gowns and drapes should maintain an environment appropriate to body temperature.
The capabilities of the practice settings, as well as the community requirements for collecting, transporting, and disposing of waste materials to meet ecological and pollution standards should be considered.
Materials should maintain integrity over the expected life of the garment or drapes, as claimed by the manufacturer.
Reference:  “Evaluating Aseptic Barrier Materials for Surgical Gowns and Drapes”, Association of Operating Room Nurses.  Standards and Recommended Practices
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Selection of Gowns and Drapes

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: To provide criteria and guidelines for determining the shelf life of facility processed sterile supplies.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
All items sterilized in the facility will have an indefinite shelf life and will no longer have an expiration date.  These items are considered sterile if the integrity of the package is not compromised or suspected of being compromised.  Sterility maintenance is event-related and not time related.  This event related policy requires the user to carefully inspect each package before use.
All products/items to be sterilized must be in a clean state, free of oils and other materials that interfere with penetration of the sterilizing agent.
All items to be sterilized must be packaged in materials designated to maintain the integrity of the sterile product.
All sterilizers shall be operated in accordance with manufacturer’s instructions.
The efficiency of the sterilizing process shall be monitored with reliable biological and chemical indicators

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Sterilized packaged goods will no longer have an expiration date, except for medications or other materials that deteriorate over time. The manufacturer’s expiration date will be used.
All items must be inspected before use. A package is considered sterile unless the wrapper of packaging material has been:
Torn
Punctured
Wet, spotted or has extensive wrinkles
Opened, unsealed, or has a broken seal
Mishandled or dropped
Damaged
All items for sterilization will be properly wrapped and processed to provide an effective barrier to microorganisms and to allow aseptic presentation upon opening.
The date of processing will be marked on each package to be used for recall purposes.  An
additional label may be attached which states: Sterile unless package is damaged or opened.
All sterilized items will be rotated by placing newly sterilized items behind the existing supply in
a position that allows the use of the older supply first,
Storage areas will be routinely inspected to be sure that the areas are kept clean, have the
proper ambient environment, traffic is limited to semi-restricted personnel, sterile supplies are
stored at least 8 to 10 inches from the ceiling and the floor, and at least 2 inches from the
outside walls and do not aid in the compromise of the packaging of the sterile items.
Dust covers may be used to further the integrity of the package.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Shelf-Life Guidelines
Section: Specimen Handling

sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: The quality of any laboratory test result is dependent on many variables, the first of which begins with the collection of the specimen. Care, skill, and knowledge when preparing the patient and specimen are essential to the provision of the highest quality standards for testing and services. The patient must first be properly prepared so that the best possible specimen can be collected. Next, the actual collection of the specimen must be completed. Then, the specimen should be properly processed, packaged, and transported to the laboratory in a timely manner and under environmental conditions that will not compromise the integrity of the specimen. After all these activities take place, a quality analysis can be performed.

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text:
To assure that accurate laboratory results are obtained, it is essential that specimens be collected, processed, and stored correctly.
Consider all specimens, controls, reagents, and calibrators that contain human blood, serum, urine, or body fluids as potentially infectious. Use good laboratory working practices when handling specimens. Follow all bio-safety practices established by OSHA.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that all specimens are collected, stored, and transported based on the recommendations of the contracted lab assuring the integrity of the specimen.
Following are general guidelines for blood and urine (urinalysis) specimen processing and handling at this facility.

sections.heading: SPECIMEN CONTAINERS
sections.text:
The person obtaining the specimen from the patient must ensure that the appropriate specimen primary container, e.g., blood sample tube, universal container, etc., is used for each sample.
All containers must be appropriately labelled.

sections.heading: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS
sections.text:
Standard precautions apply to the collection, handling and transporting of all specimens.
Always wash hands before and after obtaining and handling specimens
Cover cuts and lesions with waterproof dressing
Wear disposable aprons and appropriate gloves if there is likelihood of contact or contamination with blood or body fluids.
Take care not to contaminate the outside of the container with blood or body fluid
Do not use the specimen container for any other purpose
Always ensure the top is closed securely
Where needles, vacutainers and syringes have been used discard in appropriate sharps container at the point of use
When using needles always use safety devices
Additional precautions must be taken when a specimen is suspected to contain Hazard Group Three (3) or Four (4) organisms (see below).
Suspected Category Three (3) samples shall be segregated from other specimens.
Suspected Category Four (4) samples will NOT be collected. The patient will be immediately referred to the Emergency Room.

sections.heading: Main organisms include
sections.text:
Bacillus Anthracis (anthrax)
Brucella species (brucellosis)
Chlamydia psittaci (psittacosis)
Escherichia coli 0 157 (E coli 0157)
Shigella dysenteries (dysentery)
Salmonella typhi and parathypi (typhoid and Paratyphoid)
Mycobacterium Tuberculosis and another mycobacterium (Tuberculosis)
Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)
Hepatitis B &C
Plasmodium falciparum (falciparum malaria)
Rabies Virus
Prions causing all forms of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease CJD
SARS virus
All specimens from patients with suspected Group Three (3) infections must be designated high risk and labelled as Danger of Infection

sections.heading: Hazard Group Four (4)
sections.text:
Main organisms in group 4 are viruses that cause hemorrhagic fevers e.g., Lassa and Ebola virus.
No patient that is suspected of the viruses above will be treated at (Organization Name). Patients will be referred immediately to the Emergency Room.
The Nurse Manager will contact the Emergency Room of the potential of a highly infected patient being referred.

sections.heading: ACCEPTABLE SPECIMENS
sections.text: All outside lab orders shall include patient name, medical record number or second identifier (Patient ID or medical record number) as applicable, age, sex, date and time of collection, phlebotomists’ initials, and pertinent patient information. All tests requested to an outside lab with include a lab requisition form based on the Medical Director’s Standing Orders.

sections.heading: These are requirements for acceptable specimens
sections.text:
Samples are to be collected according to specific laboratory requirements.
Specimens must be properly labeled with the patient’s complete name and secondary identifier (Patient ID or medical record number), date and time of collection, and initials of phlebotomist.
Specialized tests must include the type of sample or the site of collection.

sections.heading: SPECIMEN REJECTION
sections.text:
When a specimen is rejected, the center shall call the patient and ask for a redraw of the specimen. Log the incident as a specimen rejection in the Lab Log.
Testing will be performed ONLY when the minimum identification criteria is met.

sections.heading: Specimens will be rejected when
sections.text:
Clots are present in anticoagulated tubes.
Specimens are grossly hemolyzed.
Tubes or specimens were not properly labeled or were mishandled (i.e., not stored or shipped at proper temperature, etc.).
The quantity of specimen is not sufficient to adequately perform testing.
The specimen was not collected in the proper container or tube.

sections.heading: GENERAL PROCESSING REQUIREMENTS FOR BLOOD SPECIMENS
SERUM SPECIMENS
sections.text:
Allow to clot for 30 minutes and spin for 10 minutes at 3000 rpm. If analysis of the specimen is to be delayed, carefully separate the serum from the clot, and transfer it to a specimen container that can be capped and labeled. Label it with complete patient information. Store at conditions recommended by the manufacturer.
Alternatively, collection tubes containing gel separators may be used; centrifuging these will provide a physical barrier between serum and red blood cells. Specimens collected in these tubes may be stored without transfer, if the gel creates a complete separation between serum and red cells.
END OF DAY PROCESSING REQUIREMENTS FOR BLOOD SPECIMENS
Patients check in for potential lab services ends at 4 pm each day.
Blood draws are to be picked up by courier by 5 pm each day unless alternate pick-up time requested by The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: URINE SPECIMENS FOR URINALYSIS
sections.text:
Patients requiring urinalysis, they will be sent to an authorized outside laboratory collection center.
Section: Specimen Handling

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to monitor the efficacy of the sterilizing process to insure the sterility of instruments and to maintain a documented monitoring control system to meet national guidelines.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Spore testing will be conducted weekly for routine loads or daily based on volume.
Biological indicators are placed in a test pack representative of the load.
When removed the vial (results test) is place in a biological spore testing machine with a biological indicator vial (control test) that has not been placed in the sterilizer.
After the appropriate time has elapsed (24 to 48 hours), read the results.  The indicator in the results test should be negative (-); the control test should be positive (+).
Record the results of the test on the spore test log and initial as confirmation of physical parameters being attained.
If the results of the spore tests from the vial placed in with the instruments are positive, the sterilizer is not to be used, and the tests are reported to the Medical Director.
The Surgical Tech or designee will perform a second test.  If the second test is positive, the sterilizer will be repaired, and not used until all tests are negative.
All instruments and packages processed with a positive test result are pulled from the shelves and re-sterilized.
The spore test log with a positive test will be compared to the surgical log; and
Patients identified will be called and asked to come into the office to check for infection.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Spore Testing per CDC Guidelines

Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Staff Training

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: FORMS TO BE USED:	Hazardous Communication Plan Training Form

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
The Safety Officer will explain the specific data sheets that apply to the new employee and inform the employee where copies of SDS are located.
Employees are to be trained by the Safety Officer at the time of their hire when they will be given the Safety Orientation.
Retraining is to be done as directed by the Safety Officer.  Retraining will be done when a new hazard is introduced into the work area, not a new chemical.
If a new solvent is brought into the workplace, and it has hazards like existing chemicals for which training has already been done, no new training is required.
If the solvent is a suspected carcinogen and there has never been a carcinogenic hazard in the workplace before, new training on the carcinogenic hazard must be done in the work areas where employees will be exposed.

sections.heading: TRAINING REQUIREMENTS
sections.text: Training will be conducted by the Safety Officer on an on-the-job basis.  Training will include an opportunity for employees to ask questions to ensure that they understand the information presented to them.

sections.heading: Training will include the following
sections.text:
1. The location of all fire extinguishers
2. The location of all exits and their signs
3. The location lf all emergency eye wash stations
4. The location of all SDS Book(s)
5. The location of all first aid kits
Date: _______________	Presented by: _________________________________________

☐ Annual Review     ☐ QA Follow-up

Provisions of the Hazard Communication Standard
Operations in employees work areas where hazardous chemicals are present
Location and availability of written plan, Name of Safety Officer

Labeling

Administrative:  Good housekeeping procedures, safe work practices, personal and 			medical monitoring

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic that no employee is expected to perform any non-routine hazardous task.

sections.heading: Informing Employees about Non-Routine Hazardous Tasks
sections.text: If at any time an employee is given non-routine tasks, he/she should contact the Safety Officer.  Each employee assigned to a non-routine task will receive a safety briefing of the hazards associated with non-routine work tasks.

sections.heading: Informing Contractors about Hazards
sections.text:
The Safety Officer will evaluate potential exposures and ensure that a contractor’s employees are provided with information regarding potential hazards prior to entering the workplace.
The Safety Officer will provide contractors and their employees with the following information:
Hazardous chemicals to which they may be exposed while on the job site
Measures the employees may take to lessen the possibility of exposure
Steps The Ferguson Clinic has taken to lessen the risks of exposure
SDS for all hazardous chemicals
Procedures to follow if they are exposed
Personal protective equipment required for the specific job (if any)
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Staff and Employee Communication

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
All medical services are performed under the direction of The Ferguson Clinic’s Medical Director and according to the Standing Orders.
Standing Orders will be completed and signed by the Medical Director prior to services being provided by the practice.
All signed Standing Orders will be placed in a binder and placed in a locked cabinet in either the Office Manager’s office or the RN’s Office.
Standing Orders will be reviewed annually.
Any new procedure that requires a standing order shall be approved by the Medical Director and Governing Body.
Section: Surgical Services
Subject:  Standing Orders

Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Sterile Processing per CDC Guidelines

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to follow the CDC guidelines in sterile processing.

sections.heading: GENERAL GUIDELINES
sections.text:
There must be proper ventilation, adequate lighting for task illumination, order, and neatness in work areas.
To reduce the occurrence of contaminated disinfectant the following control measures will be implemented:
Prepare the disinfectant correctly to achieve the manufacturer’s recommended use-dilution
Prevent common sources of extrinsic contamination of germicides (e.g., container contamination or surface contamination of the healthcare environment where the germicide is prepared and/or used.
All equipment used in Sterile Processing must be checked for electrical and mechanical safety prior to use:
Any defective equipment must be removed from service, repaired, and rechecked.
Safety regulations concerning the operation of all equipment must be strictly adhered to.
Preventive maintenance on Sterile Processing equipment is performed on a periodic basis, but not less than annually.
Documentation of inspection and preventive maintenance must contain date of inspection and service, type of service performed and signature.  These reports must be on file
Use mechanical, chemical, and biologic monitors to ensure the effectiveness of the sterilization process.
Monitor each load with mechanical (e.g., time, temperature, pressure) and chemical (internal and/or external) indicators. If the internal chemical indicator is visible, an external indicator is not needed.
Do not use processed items if the mechanical (e.g., time, temperature, and pressure) or chemical (internal and/or external) indicators suggest inadequate processing.
The use of biologic indicators to monitor the effectiveness of the sterilizers will be completed at least weekly with an FDA-cleared commercial preparation of spores (e.g., Geobacillus stearothermophilus for steam) intended for the type and cycle of parameters of the sterilizer.
All personnel using Sterile Processing equipment must be well trained in the handling, care and
use of equipment and supplies.
Manufacturers’ safety instructions must be on the equipment in view of the operator, and
equipment manuals must be on file and accessible to all operators of the equipment.

sections.heading: Personnel operating sterile processing equipment must be
sections.text:
Warned of all dangers and possible consequences
Instructed in how to prevent and avoid accidents
Knowledge of proper emergency measures to take should an accident occur
Accidents must be reported on an Incident Report.
Subject: Infection Control & Prevention
Section: Sterile Processing per CDC Guidelines

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to assure the sterility of supplies.

sections.heading: All sterile supplies should be checked before use for
sections.text:
Appropriate color change of outside tape.  Light colored stripes on tape turn dark when sterilized.
Expiration date.
Before a package is open, indicator paper stripe should be observed for appropriate color change.

sections.heading: Return to Soiled Utility
sections.text:
Any item whose sterility is questionable.
Wet, discolored or packages with broken seals.
