Teresa Ferguson_______________________			__6_/__1_/__2019_
Office Manager							Date
Authorized To Administer Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler
NAME								DATE
1.__John Ferguson, MD________________________________________6/1/2009_____
PURPOSE:
To ensure safe and effective treatment of patients undergoing Hyaluronic Acid treatment at The Ferguson Clinic the following treatment protocol has been developed.
PRODUCT:
Hyaluronic acid is generated by the streptococcus species of bacteria, chemically cross-linked with BDDE, and then stabilized. Hyaluronic Acids is indicated for the temporary correction of wrinkles and folds, the shaping of facial contours, and lip enhancement. The most frequently treated areas include nasolabial folds, oral commissures, cheeks, lips, and forehead.
ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS:
The treating professional will not treat patients with the following under any circumstance:
10.	Patients with hypersensitivity to any hyaluronic acid dermal filler preparation or to any of the components in the product formulation (e.g. patients with allergies to gram positive bacterial proteins).
11.	Patients with signs of infection/inflammation at the injection site.
12.	Pregnant or lactating women.
RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATIONS:
The treating professional will use clinical judgment when considering treatment of patients with the following:
7.	Patients with severe allergies manifested by a history of anaphylaxis.
8.	Patients with significant autoimmune disease.
9.	Patients on immunosuppressive therapy or therapy used to decrease the body’s immune response.
LIST OF SUPPLIES FOR TREATMENT:
	FDA approved Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler Injectable Gel
	Needle included in product, or similar 22-25g needle
	Optional blunt tip cannula* and pilot* needle (typically supplied but if not, a 22-25g needle is appropriate)
	Alcohol and/or chlorhexidine product used as an antiseptic
	Non-sterile gauze
	Ice for constriction and anesthesia
	Topical anesthesia cream
	Cotton tip applicators for contouring
PROTOCOL:
14.	The treating professional will complete an assessment and a medical history questionnaire will be administered before any injection(s) with all new patients.
15.	Patients with a history of allergies to gram positive bacterial proteins, patients with significant symptomatic autoimmune disease, pregnant, lactating, or patients actively trying to conceive will be denied treatment.
16.	If the patient has a significant history of anaphylaxis to medications the treating professional will consult with the Nurse Manager or Medical Director before determining whether the patient is appropriate for treatment.
17.	Upon passing a medical screening, the patient will be fully informed of risks, benefits, and potential adverse reactions. If the patient agrees to continue with treatment, an informed consent will be signed. At this time, the patient will also be provided the pre- and post-treatment instructions.
18.	Patient should be prepared using a clean technique. (Figure 1.1)
19.	At the patient’s request, a topical anesthetic cream may be applied to treatment areas.
20.	Patients should be injected while in a seated or semi-recumbent position.
21.	The treating professional will determine appropriate injection points and dose according to landmarks and location.
22.	The treating professional shall be authorized to perform the following Dermal Fillers Filler Injections:
a.	Nasolabial Folds (smile line)
i.	Dose:  0.1-1cc (bilaterally)
ii.	Number of Injections - 3-10 (depending on injection technique)
b.	Upper Lip/Chin (vertical lines)
i.	Dose:  0.1-1cc
ii.	Number of Injections – 3-10 (depending on injection technique)
c.	Vermillion Borders-upper and lower
i.	Dose:  0.1-2.0cc
ii.	Number of Injections:  3-10 (depending on injection technique)
d.	Oral Commissures/Marionette lines
i.	Dose:  0.1-2cc (bilaterally)
ii.	Number of Injections: 3-10 (depending on injection technique)
e.	Glabellar Crease(s)
i.	Dose:  0.1-1cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 3-10 (depending on injection technique)
f.	Lips
i.	Dose:  0.1-3cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 6-8 (depending on injection technique)
g.	Under eye (nasal-jugal groove)
i.	Dose:  0.1-3cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 6-8 (depending on injection technique)
h.	Temples
i.	Dose:  0.1-3cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 6-8 (depending on injection technique)
i.	Nose Contouring
i.	Dose:  0.1-1cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 6-8 (depending on injection technique)
j.	Cheek Contouring
i.	Dose: 0.1-6.0cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 1-10 (depending on injection technique)
k.	Neck/ Body Contouring
i.	Dose:  0.1-10.0cc
ii.	Number of Injections: 1-50 (depending on injection technique)
23.	If immediate blanching occurs and vascular occlusion is suspected, Hyaluronadase may be injected to absorb the dermal filler and restore tissue perfusion after a test dose is injected into the forearm. If the patient has an anaphylactic allergy to insects or bees, hyaluronidase will not be used. If either of these corrective treatments occur, the Medical Director and/or Nurse Manager will be notified immediately and will instruct further treatment if necessary.
24.	The treating professional shall record the outcome of the treatment in the patient chart. The data shall include: area(s) treated, dose, a diagram of where the injection(s) were located on the face, and any other applicable information.
25.	Patients who exhibit any immediate life-threatening signs of allergy or other serious complication such as difficulty breathing or anaphylactic shock secondary to Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler will require an emergency response initiated by calling 911 and the patient will be transported to the closest emergency facility via ambulance unless otherwise advised by EMS personnel.
POLICY:
All providers with current state licensure, shall be able to assess, consult and treat patients with Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler for severe facial wrinkles and folds, following the established guidelines.
SUPERVISION:
The providers will function under the general supervision of the Medical Director and/or Nurse Manager is immediately available for consultation by telecommunication and is physically available if medically necessary.
TRAINING/EDUCATION:
All providers will have specialized training/certification in the administration of Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler. A Registered Nurse(RN), Medical Doctor (MD), Advanced Registered Nurse Practitioner (ARNP), Physician Assistant (PA), or Doctor of Medicine in Dentistry/Doctor of Dental Surgery (DMD/DDS) experienced in this procedure may perform this training/certification. Satisfactory training will include comprehension/completion of the following:
	Mechanism of action of Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler
	Basic theory of treatment for cosmetic purposes
	Facial anatomy and muscle function
	Standard dosage for various areas
	Pharmacokinetics
	Storage and preparation of Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler
	Safety, efficacy, and complication issues
	Contraindications
	Assessment and identification of areas to be treated
	Safe application of injection techniques (at least 4 hours of supervised hands-on experience)
EVALUATION AND COMPETENCY:
Initial evaluation and final determination of satisfactory training and competence shall be by the Medical Director, Dr. John Ferguson.
AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL DOCUMENTATION:
All providers who have successfully completed training and have been determined to be competent to perform the administration of Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler will have current licensure and training on file in the Administration office.
REFERENCES/GLOSSARY:
*Pilot needle: A sharp pointed needle used to pierce the skin to introduce an available space for the blunt tip cannula.
*Blunt tip cannula: A blunt tip alternative to a sharp needle that attaches to the male portion of a luer lock syringe.  This alternative requires a trocar needle for piercing the skin in order to advance the cannula.
Clean technique (Figure 1.1)
DEVELOPMENT OF PLAN:
The Medical Director & Nurse Manager have developed this Standardized Procedure and Protocol for the Administration of Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler as a comprehensive working model.
This model will be reviewed annually at a Medical Executive Meeting and documented in the minutes of the meeting and will be kept in the Administration office.
This Standardized Procedure and Protocol have been approved by:

Medical Director							Date
Joey Seson, RN_________________________			__6_/__1_/__2019_
Nurse Manager	 						Date
Teresa Ferguson_______________________			__6_/__1_/__2019_
Office Manager							Date
Authorized To Administer Hyaluronic Acid Dermal Filler

1.__John Ferguson, MD________________________________________6/1/2009_____

sections.text:
Section:  Aesthetic Services

Section:  Aesthetic Services
Subject: Treatment Protocol for Laser Light Therapy
Section:  Aesthetic Services
Subject: Treatment Protocol for Laser Light Therapy
Section:  Aesthetic Services
Subject: Treatment Protocol for Laser Light Therapy
Section:
Subject:
PURPOSE:
To ensure safe and effective treatment of patients undergoing Laser Light Therapy treatment at The Ferguson Clinic the following treatment protocol has been developed.
PRODUCT:
Laser Treatments including wavelengths 500-1200 nm (ILP), 532 nm, 788 nm, 1064nm, 2094 nm, 10640 nm (10640nm MD, ARNP, RN, PA only)
CONTRAINDICATIONS:
The treating professional will not treat the following:
Patients with known infection to the proposed treatment site(s)
Individuals with any known recent sun exposure
Pregnant or lactating women.
LIST OF SUPPLIES FOR TREATMENT:
Laser safety goggles: to protect eyes
Conductivity gel
Tongue Blades for application of gel
Topical local anesthetic cream
Appropriate laser components
PROCEDURE:
The treating professional will complete an assessment and a medical history questionnaire will be administered before any treatments with all new patients.
Upon passing a medical screening, the patient will be fully informed of risks, benefits, and potential adverse reactions. If the patient agrees to continue with treatment, an informed consent will be signed. At this time, the patient will also be provided the pre- and post-treatment instructions.
A laser warning sign will be prominently displayed outside each entrance to the procedure room in which the laser is being used. These signs shall conform to the standard OSHA/ANSI danger signs that will include the type and maximum power of the class IV laser being used.
Appropriate protective eyewear is required for all persons in the Nominal Hazard Zone (NHZ) during laser use. The Laser Safety Officer has designated the entire room (Laser Treatment Controlled Area) as the NHZ so that protective eyewear is required for all persons in the room. These glasses will be made available at the entrance to the room.
Safety glasses must be always worn by all personnel in the NHZ while the laser is in operation. The safety glasses must be specific to the wavelength of the laser being used. Protective eyewear will be labeled according to the optical density and wavelengths filtered.
POLICY:
All providers with current state licensure, shall be able to assess, consult and treat patients with Laser Light Therapy for hyper-functional lines of the face and other approved medical conditions such as temporomandibular joint disorder, migraine headaches, and hyperhidrosis, following the established guidelines.
SUPERVISION:
The providers will function under the general supervision of the Medical Director and/or Nurse Manager who is immediately available for consultation by telecommunication and is physically available if medically necessary.
TRAINING/EDUCATION:
All providers will have specialized training/certification in the administration of Laser Light Therapy. Satisfactory training will include comprehension/completion of the following:
Laser Light Therapy principles
Specific training on each available laser and its appropriate use
Facial, skin, and vaginal anatomy and muscle function
Skin assessment and treatment plan
Records and quality assurance
Participation in all training programs and number of hours spent in maintaining proficiency should be well documented.
Laser physics, tissue interaction, laser safety, clinical application principles
Pre- and post-operative care of the laser patient
Minimum of 5 supervised treatments for each laser to evaluate competence
EVALUATION AND COMPETENCY:
Initial evaluation and final determination of satisfactory training and competence shall be by the Medical Director, Dr. John Ferguson.
AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL DOCUMENTATION:
All providers who have successfully completed training and have been determined to be competent to perform the administration of Laser Light Therapy will have current licensure and training on file in the Administration office.
DEVELOPMENT OF PLAN:
The Medical Director, Nurse Manager and Office Manager have developed this Standardized Procedure and Protocol for the Administration of Laser Light Therapy as a comprehensive working model.
This model will be reviewed annually at an annual management meeting and documented in the minutes of the meeting and will be kept in the Administration office.

Subject:

Poly-L-Lactic Acid injections are indicated for the temporary correction of wrinkles and folds, the shaping of facial contours, and the volumizing of chest, or other body parts.
ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS:
The treating professional will not treat patients with the following under any circumstance:
Patients with hypersensitivity to any hyaluronic acid dermal filler preparation or to any of the components in the product formulation (e.g. patients with allergies to gram positive bacterial proteins).
Patients with signs of infection/inflammation at the injection site.

RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Patients with severe allergies manifested by a history of anaphylaxis.
Patients with significant autoimmune disease.
Patients on immunosuppressive therapy or therapy used to decrease the body’s immune response.

FDA approved Poly-L-Lactic Acid substance
Needle included in product, or similar 22-25g needle
Optional blunt tip cannula* and pilot* needle (typically supplied but if not, a 22-25g needle is appropriate)
Alcohol and/or chlorhexidine product used as an antiseptic
Non-sterile gauze
Ice for constriction and anesthesia
Topical anesthesia cream
Cotton tip applicators for contouring
PROTOCOL:
The treating professional will complete an assessment and a medical history questionnaire will be administered before any injection(s) with all new patients.

If the patient has a significant history of anaphylaxis to medications the treating professional will consult with the Medical Director or Nurse Manager before determining whether the patient is appropriate for treatment.
Upon passing a medical screening, the patient will be fully informed of risks, benefits, and potential adverse reactions. If the patient agrees to continue with treatment, an informed consent will be signed. At this time, the patient will also be provided the pre- and post-treatment instructions.
Patient should be prepared using a clean technique. (Figure 1.1)
At the patient’s request, a topical anesthetic cream may be applied to treatment areas.
Patients should be injected while in a seated or semi-recumbent position.
The treating professional will determine appropriate injection points and dose according to landmarks and location.
If immediate blanching occurs and vascular occlusion is suspected, the treating professional may use nitro paste (if no contraindications) to vasodilate the area in question. If this corrective treatment occurs, the Medical Director and/or Nurse Manager will be notified immediately and will instruct further treatment if necessary.
The treating professional shall record the outcome of the treatment in the patient chart. The data shall include area(s) treated, dose, a diagram of where the injection(s) were located on the face, and any other applicable information.
Patients who exhibit any immediate life-threatening signs of allergy or other serious complication such as difficulty breathing or anaphylactic shock secondary to Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra) will require an emergency response initiated by calling 911 and the patient will be transported to Queen’s Medical Center or the closest emergency facility via ambulance unless otherwise advised by EMS personnel.
POLICY:
All providers with current state licensure, shall be able to assess, consult and treat patients with Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra) for severe facial wrinkles and folds, facial lipoatrophy, and facial contouring, following the established guidelines.
SUPERVISION:
The providers will function under the general supervision of the Medical Director and/or Nurse Manager who is immediately available for consultation by telecommunication and is physically available if medically necessary.
TRAINING/EDUCATION:
All providers will have specialized training/certification in the administration of Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra). A Registered Nurse (RN), Medical Doctor (MD), Advanced Registered Nurse Practitioner (ARNP), Physician Assistant (PA), or Doctor of Medicine in Dentistry/Doctor of Dental Surgery (DMD/DDS) experienced in this procedure may perform this training/certification. Satisfactory training will include comprehension/completion of the following:
Mechanism of action of Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra)
Basic theory of treatment for cosmetic purposes
Facial anatomy and muscle function
Standard dosage for various areas
Pharmacokinetics
Storage and preparation of Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra)
Safety, efficacy, and complication issues
Contraindications
Assessment and identification of areas to be treated
Safe application of injection techniques (at least 4 hours of supervised hands-on experience)
EVALUATION AND COMPETENCY:
Initial evaluation and final determination of satisfactory training and competence shall be by the Medical Director, Dr. John Ferguson.
AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL DOCUMENTATION:
All providers who have successfully completed training and have been determined to be competent to perform the administration of Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra) will have current licensure and training on file in the Administration office.
REFERENCES/GLOSSARY:
*Pilot needle: A sharp pointed needle used to pierce the skin to introduce an available space for the blunt tip cannula.
*Blunt tip cannula: A blunt tip alternative to a sharp needle that attaches to the male portion of a luer lock syringe.  This alternative requires a trocar needle for piercing the skin in order to advance the cannula.
Clean technique (Figure 1.1)
DEVELOPMENT OF PLAN:
The Medical Director, Nurse Manager and Office Manager have developed this Standardized Procedure and Protocol for the Administration of Poly-L-Lactic Acid (Sculptra) as a comprehensive working model.
This model will be reviewed annually Medical Executive meeting and documented in the minutes of the meeting and will be kept in the Administration office.
This Standardized Procedure and Protocol have been approved by:

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to not provide any treatment to any minor (under the age of 18) without the signed authorization of the minor’s parent or legal guardian.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVE
sections.text:
To define age guidelines for specific consents to medical treatment, consistent with
guidelines promulgated by the State of Hawaii.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Treatment will not be given to children under the age of 18 without the written consent of a parent or legal guardian.
To treat an emancipated minor, the following should be addressed, and The Ferguson Clinic consulted before a decision is made:
A copy of the emancipation order
Minor’s age
Means of support (total or partial)
Living situation (with parents or guardian or away from the parents or guardian).
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Treatment of a Minor

title: Understanding of Informed Consent, Patient/Parent or Legal Guardian

sections.text:
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Understanding of Informed Consent, Patient/Parent or Guardian

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Prior to any treatment, it is the policy of the The Ferguson Clinic to obtain a signed Informed Consent form from every patient for every procedure, which indicates the patient’s understanding of the procedure, risks, and potential complications.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To communicate effectively with the patient in a professional and caring manner
To assure that the patient diagnosis, treatment, and options are provided to the patient in a manner that they understand
To document information in the electronic medical record that accurately reflects the information provided to the patient and his/her consent to treatment
To reduce exposure to litigation

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text:
Before the patient signs an Informed Consent form, the Physician should discuss the following elements of the Informed Consent:
The patient’s diagnosis
Proposed procedure and options
Potential benefits and drawbacks
Possible complications, including the statistical risks for each complication, as applicable.
If a patient’s health makes it inadvisable to give such information to the patient, the information is to be provided to the patient’s parent or legal guardian

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.text: Give the patient time to discuss the procedure with his/her family.

sections.heading: Staff members also inform the patient of the following
sections.text:
The name of the physician or other practitioner who has primary responsibility for the patient’s care
The identity and professional status of individuals responsible for authorizing and performing procedures or treatments
The organization’s relationship to educational institutions involved in the patient’s care
Any business relationships between individuals treating the patient, or between the organization and any other health care service, or educational institutions involved in the patient’s care

sections.heading: Obtain a signed Informed Consent form from the patient
sections.text:
After review of materials and discussions with the medical staff, the patient should be able to directly state that he/she has been given the opportunity to have questions answered.
If the patient’s condition does not allow for such interaction, the information is given to the person designated by the patient or legally authorized representative.

sections.heading: DOCUMENTATION
sections.text:
The above discussion and its elements should be documented in the patient’s record.
The signed Informed Consent form must be filed in the patient’s record.
The health care professional who signs the Informed Consent form as a witness is attesting to the authenticity of the patient’s signature
Section: Rights of Patients
Subject: Understanding of Informed Consent, Patient/Parent or Guardian

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Utilities Management Plan
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Utilities Management Plan

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: The purpose of the Utilities Management Plan is to describe how The Ferguson Clinic promotes a safe, controlled, and comfortable environment of care; reduces the potential for organizational acquired illness; assesses and minimizes risks of utility failures and ensures operational reliability of utility systems.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to maintain a Utilities Management Program that protects
staff, patients, visitors, and the patient care environment in the event of any utility systems failure.

sections.heading: SCOPE
sections.text: The Utilities Management Plan applies to the facility of The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: OBJECTIVES
sections.text:
To promote a safe, controlled, comfortable environment of care.
To reduce the potential for organizational-acquired illnesses.
To assess and minimize risks of utility failures.
To ensure operational reliability of utility systems.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
Medical Staff and clinical personnel are responsible for knowing how and when to perform emergency clinical interventions when utility systems fail.
The Office Manager is responsible for maintaining the Utilities Management Plans, including:
Establishing criteria for identifying, evaluating, and taking inventory of critical operating components of systems to be included in the Utilities Management Plan. These criteria address the impact of utility systems on:
Life support system Infection control systems.
Environmental support systems.
Equipment support systems.
Communication systems.
Inspecting, testing, and maintaining critical operating components.
Developing and maintaining current utility system operational plans to help ensure reliability, minimize risks, and reduce failures.
Mapping the layout of utility systems and labeling controls for a partial or complete emergency shutdown.
Maintaining a listing of service representatives to be called in the event of an emergency.
Developing emergency procedures for utility system disruptions or failures that address:
Specific procedures in the event of a utility systems malfunction.
Identification of an alternate source of essential utilities.
Shutoff of malfunctioning systems and notification of staff in affected areas.
Obtaining repair services.

sections.heading: PROCESSES OF THE UTILITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
Preventative Maintenance Agreements
sections.text:
The maintenance of selected equipment that is critical to The Ferguson Clinic’s function, and/or
the safety of patients, staff, and visitors are provided for by the Office Manager or through
agreements with companies that specialize in such services.
These agreements may provide for both preventive maintenance inspections and repair service.
To facilitate the process, the Office Manager must obtain and maintain current copies of all maintenance contracts entered that relate to The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: Documentation of the Utilities Management Plan will include
sections.text:
Preventative maintenance contracts.
Preventative maintenance records, monitoring logs, and inspection.

sections.heading: Inspection Testing and Maintenance
sections.text:
Critical utility components will be tested before initial use and annually thereafter.
Emergency lights with battery packs will be tested monthly for 30 seconds; and annually for 1 ½ hours (Emergency exit signs, emergency back-up lights in halls, OR, recovery, etc.).
Compressed gas will be monitored daily.
Eye wash stations will be tested weekly.
Visual inspection of fire extinguishers will be performed monthly.Emergency generators will be tested weekly and monthly according to NFPA 110 guidelines.

sections.heading: Incident Reporting
sections.text:
Documentation of all incidents relating to utility failure will be recorded on an Incident Report.
Incident Reports will be sent to the Office Manager.
The Office Manager is responsible for investigating utility systems problems, failures, or user errors, and reporting incidents and corrective actions. All incidents will be reviewed by the QIP Committee.  Orientation and continuing education for users and maintainers of the utility systems:
T	The Office Manager or designee will train staff during orientation on the following:
Monitor system capabilities, limitations, and special applications.
Emergency procedures in the event of system failure.
Information and skills necessary to perform assigned maintenance responsibilities.
Location and instructions for use of emergency shut-off controls.
Whom to contact in an emergency.  Supervisors are responsible for training employees during orientation, and on how and when to perform clinical interventions in the event of a utility failure.  An annual review of the utility functions can be met by classroom activities, one-on-one discussions or through the completion of a self-study packet that includes information on utilities, shut-off valves and what to do in the event of a utility failure.
All training is documented in employee personnel files.

sections.heading: Performance Monitoring
sections.text:
Monitoring and tracking of system failures will be performed by the Office Manager and reported to the QIP Committee.
The Office Manager and QIP Committee will monitor performance standards.
Summaries of findings and recommendations, based on trends, performance measures, and performance improvement activities will be documented quarterly by the QIP Committee.
Specific information will be communicated to staff when issues or opportunities to reduce the risk
of safety hazards exit.

sections.heading: Performance Standards relate to the following
sections.text:
Staff knowledge and skills.
Level of staff participation.
Monitoring and inspection activities.
Emergency and incident reporting.
Inspection, preventive maintenance, and testing of equipment.
Annual Review
The Office Manager, in collaboration with the QIP Committee, is responsible for the annual review
of the Utility Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
The annual review will be compiled at the end of the year based on information from a variety of sources including, but not limited to incident reports on occupational illness, worker safety, patient and visitor injuries, product safety recall notices, hazard surveillance surveys, pro-active risk assessments, staff orientation and training, QIP Committee minutes, performance monitoring activities, and other summaries of activities, including the findings of regulatory agencies.
The annual review will be presented to the Governing body in a narrative report that covers the
Utility Management Plan’s objectives, scope, performance, and effectiveness.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Utilities Management Plan
Section: Venipuncture

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: All staff that perform venipuncture must be trained, be within their scope of practice, demonstrate competency, competency documented in the personnel file and be privileged to perform venipuncture by the Medical Director.

sections.heading: DEFINITION
sections.text: The puncture of a vein as part of a medical procedure, typically to withdraw a blood sample or for an intravenous injection.

sections.heading: Difference between phlebotomy and venipuncture
sections.text: Venipuncture is just that, puncturing the vein. This can be done for a couple of reasons: intravenous (IV) therapies or drawing blood samples (Phlebotomy). ... Phlebotomy is usually done with a new venipuncture each time and is typically performed at slightly different sites on the patient to reduce discomfort.

sections.heading: Venipuncture Methods
sections.text:
Syringe Method.
Vacuum Tube Method.
Butterfly Method.

sections.heading: Venipuncture Site Selection
sections.text:
The median cubital and cephalic veins are most commonly used for venipuncture.
Alternative sites are the basilic vein on the dorsum of the arm or dorsal hand veins.
Due to its close proximity to the brachial artery and median nerve, the basilic vein, which is located on the pinkie side of the arm, should be used only if there is not another more prominent arm vein. Veins in the foot and ankle should be utilized only as a last resort.
Veins on the underside of the wrist should be avoided.
Certain other sites should also be avoided.

sections.heading: Sites to avoid
sections.text:
Extensive scarring from burns or surgery
The upper extremity on the side that a mastectomy was performed.
Hematoma – A venipuncture should not be performed on a hematoma,
regardless of how small it may be. If there is not an alternate vein to draw,
the venipuncture should be performed distal to (below) the hematoma.
Cannula, Fistula or Vascular Graft – Blood should only be drawn from an
arm with a cannula, fistula or vascular graft with the provider’s prior approval.
Edematous extremities – tissue fluid accumulation can alter test results.
Sites with noticeable skin conditions, such as eczema or infection.
Palpate and trace the path of the vein with the index finger. Arteries pulsate are
more elastic and have a thicker wall than a vein. Thrombosed veins lack resilience, feel cord-like and roll easily. If you are using a tourniquet for preliminary vein selection, it should be released after one minute, left off for two minutes and then reapplied before performing the venipuncture.

sections.heading: Procedure and Performance of a Venipuncture
sections.text:
Select the proper size needle and attach it to the syringe or Vacutainer hub.
When using a syringe, make sure that you pull the plunger in and out to assure free motion.
Position the draw site for best visualization and/or palpation. Apply the tourniquet 3-4 inches above the selected puncture site. Do not place tightly or leave on for more than 1 minute. Instruct the patient to make a fist and hold it; do not have them pump their hand. Select the venipuncture site by palpating with the gloved index finger.
Prepare the patient’s arm using a alcohol wipe. Allow to air dry. Do not dry the site with gauze and do not blow on the area to dry as this will contaminate the site. After cleansing the area, if it is accidentally touched before performing the venipuncture, it should be cleansed again.
Grasp the patient’s arm firmly using your thumb to draw the skin taut and anchor the vein; do not use the index finger to pull the skin upward as this increases the risk of sticking yourself. Swiftly insert the needle through the skin, bevel side up, at a 15 – 30-degree angle with the skin, into the lumen of the vein.
If the patient complains of “shooting, electric-like pain, or tingling or numbness proximal or distal to the puncture site,” the needle should be removed immediately. It is possible that a nerve has been punctured and possibly damaged. The venipuncture should be repeated in a different site. The Medical Director or Office Manager should be notified, and the incident report should be documented.
If an arterial puncture is suspected, as indicated by a bright red, quick, pulsing flow, with or without rapid development of a hematoma, the needle should be removed immediately. Forceful, direct pressure should be applied to the site for a minimum of five minutes or until the bleeding has stopped. The nursing staff should be notified, and they in turn must notify the physician. The Medical Director of Office Manager should also be notified, and the incident report should be documented.
If the blood does not begin to flow, reposition the needle by gently moving the needle either backwards for forwards in the arm. If the blood is flowing slowly, gently adjust the angle to see if the needle is sitting up against the wall of the vein. Loosen the tourniquet, as it may be obstructing blood flow. If you are vacutaining, try another tube – there may be no vacuum in the tube.
After you have attempted to reposition the needle and are still not successful, remove the tourniquet, remove the needle and begin the process with a new site. In the case of a difficult venipuncture, an individual may make a maximum of two attempts before having someone else try. A third stick is allowable if a partial sample has been obtained and you as the drawer feel reasonably confident that you can obtain the specimen on the next try.
When the collection is complete, remove the tourniquet and place gauze over the venipuncture site. While the needle is still in the vein, activate the safety button with the tip of the index finger; the needle will automatically retract from the vein and the safety device will cover the needle.
Apply adequate pressure to the puncture site to stop the bleeding and avoid formation of a hematoma. If you used a needle and syringe, ask your patient or a parent to apply pressure to the site so that you can fill your tubes. Do not have the patient bend his/her arm; this may cause the arm to start bleeding when the arm is straightened out.
If blood was drawn with a syringe, attach the blood transfer device to the syringe and fill tubes according to the ‘Order of Draw for a Venipuncture’
Dispose of the contaminated materials and needle in the appropriate waste containers.
Mix and label all appropriate tubes at the patient’s bedside. Return to your patient and assess the site of the puncture. Apply taped-guaze or CoFlex to the site.

sections.heading: Additional Considerations When Performing a Venipuncture
The following considerations should be taken into account
sections.text:
Preventing a Hematoma: puncture only the uppermost wall of the vein.
Remove the tourniquet before removing the needle. Make sure the needle fully penetrates the upper-most wall of the vein; partial penetration may allow blood to leak into the tissue surrounding the vein. Adequate pressure should be applied to stop the bleeding once the phlebotomy is complete. A hematoma can cause a post-phlebotomy compression injury to a nerve.
Preventing Hemolysis: Mix tubes gently, by inversion, 5-10 times – do not shake them. Avoid drawing blood from a hematoma. If using a needle and syringe, avoid drawing the plunger back too forcefully. Make sure the venipuncture site is dry. Avoid probing for the vein. If using a blood transfer device to fill vacutainer tubes, allow the vacuum to pull the blood into the tubes; do not use the plunger on the syringe to force the blood into the tubes more quickly.
Preventing Hemoconcentration: An increased concentration of larger molecules and formed elements in the blood may be due to several factors including prolonged tourniquet application (greater than 1 minute), massaging, flicking, squeezing or probing the site, long-term IV therapy, and sclerosed or occluded veins.
Preventing injury to a nerve, tendon, or muscle: Use careful palpitation and appropriate angle of entry. Excessive probing (uncalculated side-to-side movement) with the needle should be avoided.
Preventing dizziness or fainting and potential follow-up injuries due to a fall: Be sure patient is seated in an appropriate draw chair and or lying in a bed. Have appropriate back up staff as available.
Preventing of infection: Follow proper infection control policies.
Preventing injury from improper immobilization – Immobilize the patient with care.

sections.heading: Order of Draw for Venipuncture
sections.text:
Blood collection tubes must be drawn in a specific order to avoid cross-contamination of additives between tubes. Follow the order of draw listed here for both syringes (utilizing the blood transfer device) and vacutainers:
Blood Cultures
Na Citrate Coagulation tubes – light blue top tube
Non-additive tube – red top tube
SST red or gold top – this tube contains a gel separator and clot activators.
Sodium Heparin – green top tube
Lithium Heparin – green top tube
EDTA – lavender top tube
ACDA or ACDB – light yellow top tube
Oxalate/fluoride – light gray top tube

sections.heading: Collapsed Vein
sections.text:
The most common symptoms of collapsed veins are cold hands and feet due to circulation loss, sharp pain at the injection site, and discoloration of the skin. There may also be itching at the injection site as the vein starts to heal.
Section: Venipuncture

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Violent and Aggressive Patients & Visitors

sections.heading: Physical & Verbal Abuse
sections.text:
Unreasonable and/or offensive remarks or behavior/rude gestures/innuendoes.
Sexual and racial harassment.
Threatening behavior (with or without a weapon).
Actual physical assault (whether it results in actual injury) includes being pushed or shoved as well as being hit, punched, or attacked with a weapon, or being intentionally struck with bodily fluids or excrement.
Discrimination of any kind.
Damage to personnel or personnel’s property.

sections.heading: Violence and Aggression
sections.text:
Physical contact with another person which may or may not result in pain or injury. The contact uninvited and is an attempt to cause harm, injury or to intimidate.
Non-physical aggression includes the use of language which causes offence or threatens the safety of personnel.

sections.heading: Types of Difficult/Angry Patients and/or Visitors
sections.text:
Withdrawn, secretive, vague – limits information as a form of control.
Critical – everything is wrong, bad.
Intimidating – highly sarcastic, cutting.
Depressed – dwell on all the misfortunes, makes others feel guilty.

sections.heading: Anger
sections.text: Often due to loss of control, feelings of powerlessness.

sections.heading: Recognizing When Someone is Angry
sections.text:
Raised voices/shouting.
Flushed face.
Wild gesticulations.
Angry words.
Rigid body.
Withdrawal.
Sarcasm.
Dismissive comments.
Gritted teeth, clenched jaw.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
The management team will seek to ensure that it takes all measures to prevent:
Physical or verbal abuse of its personnel, including racial and sexual harassment.
Physical assault on employees by patients and other members of staff.
Ensure all managers are responsible for adequate supervision of employees to ensure that they do not put themselves at risk of physical and verbal abuse by patients or visitors.
Ensure that the contents of this document are brought to the attention of all personnel.
Undertake suitable and enough violence and aggression risk assessments, to quantify any risks and to identify proactive measures to eliminate and/or control the risks.
Ensure all personnel undertake workplace violence training.
Ensure all new personnel are oriented to the this and all other safety policies/training, etc., within 30 days of hire.
Ensure all violence and aggression incidents are reported by the Incident Report Policy.

sections.heading: Safe Work Environment
sections.text:
Carry out risk assessments to assess and review the duties of personnel, identifying any “at risk” situations and taking appropriate steps to reduce or remove the risk to personnel, particularly if they are working alone.
Assess and review the layout of the premises to reduce the risk to personnel where physically possible.
Assess and review the provision of personal safety equipment, such as alarms.
Provide support to victims or refer to a suitable qualified healthcare professional.
Make personnel aware of risks and ensure personnel involvement in training.
Record any incidents on an Incident Report and take any remedial action to ensure similar incidents are prevented in the future.

sections.heading: Personnel Responsibilities
sections.text: At all times, personnel should take care to ensure a safe work environment and must consider their own safety and that of their colleagues.

sections.heading: Personnel must
sections.text:
Familiarize themselves with this policy and conform to the requirements, guidelines and instructions contained within it.
Ensure they are familiar with the location of equipment or devices provided to use in at risk situations (e.g., panic buttons, alarms, etc.) and know how to use them.
Be responsible for their own security and the security of others who may be affected by their acts and omissions.
Cooperate with managers on security matters and observe all safety rules always.
Undertake and participate in relevant training made available by the Ferguson Clinic.
Promptly report all incidents of physical or verbal abuse (threatened or actual), violence and aggression incidents, hazards or near misses and damage, in accordance with The Ferguson’s Clinic’s Adverse Event Policy.
Record all details of incidents in compliance with Incident Reporting Policies and Procedures.
Suggest precautionary measures involving changes in the layout of the work environment that can reduce risk, such as removal of possible weapons or projectiles (e.g., telephones or letter openers) from patient access.
Advise the Office Manager of any perceived risks involved in work activities.
Never attempt to physically tackle someone during a violent or aggressive incident.

sections.heading: Dealing with Violence in the Waiting/Reception Area
sections.text:
Dial 911.
Use the emergency panic button (if applicable).
Work as a team and follow response as defined by The Ferguson Clinic.
Do not put yourself or your colleagues in danger.
Evacuate other patients and visitors from the waiting/reception area.

sections.heading: If an Incident Occurs
sections.text:
Ask the perpetrator to stop his/her behavior, while remaining calm. Under no circumstances should you respond in a heightened, angry manner.
If personnel receive an abusive telephone call, they should warn the caller that they will terminate the call if they do not modify their behavior. If the caller continues to be abusive, personnel have the authority to terminate the call.
Should the perpetrator be breaking the law, physically assault anyone or cause damage to the premises, call the police right away.
Do not attempt to remove the person from the premises unless it is under extreme circumstances. Call the police.
If it is necessary to resort to calling the police and having a person removed, all personnel involved in the incident should make a written statement, giving as many details as possible, including exact words used by the perpetrator. This statement should be undertaken immediately while the incident is fresh in mind.
Where an injury has been sustained (irrespective of how serious), a Workplace Violence Incident Report shall be completed. The injured party will be sent to the ER or encouraged to seek medical care.
The Organization will press charges against anyone who physically or verbally assaults another, damages, or steals property.
Where the perpetrator is a member of the staff or a volunteer, disciplinary proceedings will be completed up to including termination and possible charges submitted.

sections.heading: Actions Following an Incident
sections.text:
A meeting to undertake a significant event analysis will be convened as soon as possible which will include the QI Committee and personnel involved. That meeting will be discussed with the Governing Body at the next meeting.
Following the discussion, further action (if any) will be decided (e.g., written warning, removal from the Ferguson Clinic practice, etc.).
Where there are significant mitigating circumstances (e.g., severe mental problems), may be considered when deciding on any further action.
Any personnel involved in a violent incident will be offered counseling/medical treatment.
Do’s and Don’ts
Do
Do not
Recognize your own feelings
Meet anger with anger
Project calmness; move and speak slowly, quietly, and confidently.
Raise your voice, point, or stare or communicate in a way that might generate hostility (such as apathy, coldness, condescension
Put yourself in their shoes
Be sensitive or take their acts or words personally
Be prepared to apologize
Threaten any intervention unless you are prepared to act on it
Assert yourself appropriately
Make them feel trapped or cornered
Be patient – allow people to get things off their chest – when they are calmer and quieter, express your ideas/point of view
Argue or even feel that you must win the argument
Be empathetic listener. Encourage the person to talk and listen patiently.
Reject all a client’s demands from the start.
Focus your attention on the other person to let them know you are interested in what they have to say.
Pose challenging stances such as standing directly opposite someone, hands on hips, or crossing your arms.
Maintain a relaxed, yet attentive, posture and position yourself at a right angle, rather than directly in front of the other person.
Avoid any physical contact, finger pointing, or long periods of fixed eye contact.
Acknowledge the person’s feelings. Indicate that you can see he (or she) is upset.
Make sudden movements that be threatening. Notice the tone, volume, and rate of your speech.
Ask for small, specific favors such as asking the person to move to a quieter area.
Challenge, threaten, or dare the individual. Never belittle the person or make him (or her) feel foolish.
Establish ground rules if unreasonable behavior persists. Calmly describe the consequences of any violent behavior.
Criticize or act impatiently toward the agitated individual.
Use delay tactics that will give the person time to calm down. For example, offer a drink of water in a disposable cup.
Attempt to bargain with a threatening individual.
Be reassuring and point out choices. Break big problems into smaller, more manageable problems.
Try to make the situation seem less serious that it is.
Accept criticism in a positive way. When a complaint might be true, use statements like, “You’re probably right,” or “It was my fault.” If the criticism seems unwarranted, ask clarifying questions.
Make false statements or promises you cannot keep or try to impact a lot of technical or complicated information when emotions are high.
Ask for their recommendations. Repeat back to them what you feel they are requesting of you.
Take sides or agree with distortions.
Arrange yourself so that a visitor cannot block your access to an exit.
Invade the individual’s personal space. Make sure there is a space of 3-6 fee between you and the hostile/irate person.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Violent and Aggressive Patients & Visitors

title: Visiting Physicians and Allied Health Professionals

sections.heading: AUDIENCE
sections.text: This policy applies to all Physicians and Allied Health Care Professional that participate in The Ferguson Clinic educational classes and onsite training for cosmetic procedures and other modalities.

sections.heading: DEFINITIONS
sections.text:
The following are definitions, explanations, qualifications, or stipulations regarding certain terms used in this policy.
Physician Student(s)
As used throughout this Policy includes medical doctors and allied health care professionals.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Additionally, the medical staff realizes that a physician student can prevent The Ferguson Clinic from meeting its commitments to provide high quality patient care in a safe environment. The Ferguson Clinic visiting physician students are expected to conduct their activities in this healthcare environment in a professional manner adhering to all the Policies, Procedures and Protocols set forth by The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: These include
sections.text:
Complete attendance throughout the training sessions, unless otherwise excused for medical reasons or emergency.
Professional Conduct always.
Follow all Policies, Procedures and Protocols as directed by The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
It will be the responsibility of the Medical Director of The Ferguson Clinic to evaluate the behavior of each visiting student physician. If deemed unacceptable, the visiting physician student will be asked to leave The Ferguson Clinic immediately.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Visiting Physicians and Allied Health Care Professionals

sections.heading: AUDIENCE
sections.text:
This Policy applies to all physicians, health care professionals and other authorized visitors that
participate in The Ferguson Clinic’s onsite training and observance of procedures for
educational or social purposes.

sections.heading: DEFINITIONS
sections.text:
The following are definitions, explanations, qualifications, or stipulations regarding certain
terms used in this policy.
Visitors
As used throughout this Policy includes medical doctors and health care professionals and
other visitors to the patient of The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text:
services rendered to their patients whether it be for training purposes or as a support for the
patient at the request of the patient.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Additionally, The Ferguson Clinic realizes that a visitor can prevent The Ferguson Clinic from
meeting its commitments to provide high quality patient care in a safe environment. The
Ferguson Clinic visitors are expected to conduct their activities in this healthcare
environment in a professional manner adhering to all the Policies, Procedures and Protocols
set forth by The Ferguson Clinic.

sections.heading: These include
sections.text:
Visitors at the patient’s request are not approved for entry into the procedure room.
Visiting health care professionals for training will complete attendance throughout the training sessions, unless otherwise always excused for medical reasons or emergencies and display professional conduct.

sections.heading: ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
sections.text:
It will be the responsibility of the Office Manager in cooperation with the Medical Director of
Section: Surgical Services
Subject: Visitors in the Procedure/Operating Room

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to handle all workplace corrosives in such a manner as to ensure the safety of all patients, staff, and visitors.

sections.heading: PROCEDURES
sections.text: Corrosives can burn the eyes and skin and need to be handled carefully.  Inhaling corrosive gases or mists can cause serious damage, sometimes permanent or fatal.

sections.heading: Corrosives include
sections.text:
Acids (hydrochloric, hydrofluoric, nitric, sulfuric, etc.)
Bases (alkalis, caustics usually end with hydroxide, oxide, or amine).
Oxidizers (fluorine, chlorine, etc.)
Solvents (methylene chloride)
When handling corrosives, appropriate personal protective equipment will be worn, based on the product label:
Gloves
Goggles/ safety glasses
Face shields
Protective clothing
Respiratory mask
Subject: Hazard Communication
Section: Workplace Corrosives

Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Workplace Violence
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Workplace Violence
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Workplace Violence
PURPOSE: The Ferguson Clinic maintains a zero-tolerance standard of violence in the workplace. The purpose of this policy is to provide Ferguson Clinic personnel guidance that will assist them in maintaining an environment at and within The Ferguson Clinic’s property and events that are free of violence and the threat of violence.
POLICY: Violent behavior of any kind or threats of violence, either implied or direct, are prohibited at The Ferguson Clinic, in properties and at The Ferguson Clinic sponsored events. Such conduct by a The Ferguson Clinic employee will not be tolerated. An employee who exhibits violent behavior may be subject to criminal prosecution and shall be subject to disciplinary action up to and including dismissal. Violent threats or actions by a non-employee may result in criminal prosecution. The Ferguson Clinic will investigate all complaints filed and will also investigate any possible violations of this policy of which we are made aware. Retaliation against a person who makes a complaint regarding violent behavior or threats of violence made to him/her is also prohibited.  DEFINITIONS:  Workplace Violence: Behavior in which an employee, former employee or visitor to a workplace inflicts or threatens to inflict damage to property, serious harm, injury, or death to others at the workplace.
Threat: The implication or expression of intent to inflict physical harm or actions that a reasonable person would interpret as a threat to physical safety or property.
Intimidation: Making others afraid or fearful through threatening behavior.
Zero-tolerance: A standard that establishes that any behavior, implied or actual, that violates the policy will not be tolerated.
Court Order: An order by a Court that specifies and/or restricts the behavior of an individual. Court Orders may be issued in matters involving domestic violence, stalking or harassment, among other types of protective orders, including Temporary Restraining Orders.
PROHIBITED BEHAVIOR:  Violence in the workplace may include, but is not limited to the following list of prohibited behaviors directed at or by a co-worker, supervisor, or member of the public:
Direct threats or physical intimidation.
Implications or suggestions of violence.
Stalking.
Possession of weapons of any kind on The Ferguson Clinic property, including parking lots, other exterior premises or while engaged in activities for The Ferguson Clinic in other locations, or at The Ferguson Clinic’s sponsored events, unless such possession or use is a requirement of the job.
Assault of any form.
Physical restraint, confinement.
Dangerous or threatening horseplay.
Loud, disruptive, or angry behavior or language that is clearly not part of the typical work environment.
Blatant or intentional disregard for the safety or well-being of others.
Commission of a violent felony or misdemeanor on The Ferguson Clinic property; and
Any other act that a reasonable person would perceive as constituting a threat of violence.
Domestic Violence, while often originating in the home, can significantly impact workplace safety and the productivity of victims as well as co-workers. For the purposes of this document, “domestic violence” is defined as abuse committed against an adult or fully emancipated minor. Abuse is the intentional reckless attempt to cause bodily injury, sexual assault, threatening behavior, harassment, or stalking, or making annoying phone calls to a person who is in any of the following relationships:
Spouse or former spouse.
Domestic partner or former domestic partner.
Cohabitant or former cohabitant and or another household member.
A person with whom the victim is having, or has had, a dating or engagement relationship; and
A person with whom the victim has a child.

sections.heading: REPORTING ACTS OR THREATS OF VIOLENCE:  Personnel who
sections.text:
Is the victim of violence? or.
Believes they have been threatened with violence, or
Witnesses an act or threat of violence towards anyone else shall take the following steps:
If an emergency exists and the situation is one of immediate danger, the employee shall contact the local police officials by dialing 9-1-1 and may take whatever emergency steps are available and appropriate to protect himself/herself from immediate harm, such as leaving the area.
If the situation is not one of immediate danger, the employee shall report the incident to the Office Manager and/or designee as soon as possible and complete The Ferguson Clinic Workplace Violence Incident Report Form.
PROCEDURES- FUTURE VIOLENCE: Personnel who have reason to believe they, or others, may be victimized by a violent act sometime in the future, at the workplace or as a direct result of their employment with The Ferguson Clinic, shall inform the Office Manager and/or designee by immediately completing an Incident Report Form so appropriate action may be taken. The Office Manager and/or designee will notify the local law enforcement officials.
Personnel who have signed and filed a restraining order, temporary or permanent, against an individual due to a potential act of violence, who would violate the order by coming near them at work, shall immediately supply a copy of the signed order to the Office Manager.
INCIDENT INVESTIGATION:  Acts of violence or threats will be investigated immediately to protect personnel from danger, unnecessary anxiety concerning their welfare, and the loss of productivity. The Office Manager will initiate an investigation into potential violation of work rules/policies. Simultaneously, the Office Manager will refer the matter to local police for their review of potential violation of civil and/or criminal law.

sections.heading: Procedures for investigating incidents of workplace violence include
sections.text:
Visiting the scene of an incident as soon as possible.
Interviewing injured and threatened personnel and witnesses.
Examining the workplace for security risk factors associated with the incident, including any reports of inappropriate behavior by the perpetrator.
Determining the cause of the incident; and
Taking mitigating action to prevent the incident from recurring.
Recording the findings and mitigating actions taken.
In appropriate circumstances, The Ferguson Clinic will inform the reporting individual of the results of the investigation. To the extent possible, The Ferguson Clinic will maintain the confidentiality of the reporting employees and the investigation but may need to disclose results in appropriate circumstances, e.g., to protect individual safety. The Ferguson Clinic will not tolerate retaliation against any employee who reports workplace violence.
MITIGATING MEASURES:  Incidents which threaten the security of all personnel shall be mitigated as soon as possible following their discovery.

sections.heading: Mitigating actions include
sections.text:
Notification of law enforcement authorities when a potential criminal act has occurred.
Provision of emergency medical care in the event of any violent act upon personnel.
Post-event trauma counseling for those employees desiring such assistance.
Assurance that incidents are handled by the Workplace Violence Prevention policy; and
Requesting that The Ferguson Clinic’s attorney files for a restraining order as appropriate.
TRAINING AND INSTRUCTION: The Ferguson Clinic’s Safety Officer shall be responsible for ensuring that all personnel are provided training and instruction on general workplace security practices.

sections.heading: Training and instruction shall be provided as follows
sections.text:
To all current personnel when the policy is first implemented.
To all new employees or employees who have been given new job assignments for which specific workplace security training for that job assignment has not previously been provided; and
To affected personnel whenever management is made aware of a new or previously unrecognized hazard.
Workplace security training and instruction includes, but is not limited to, the following:
Preventive measures to reduce the threat of workplace violence, including procedures for reporting workplace security hazards.
Methods to diffuse hostile or threatening situations.
Escape routes; and
Explanation of this Workplace Violence Prevention Policy.
In addition, specific instructions shall be provided to all employees regarding workplace security hazards unique to their job assignment.
Subject: Environment of Care
Section: Workplace Violence

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: Only authorized users may access The Ferguson Clinic’s systems. Accordingly, The Ferguson Clinic has implemented physical safeguards for all workstations that access ePHI to restrict access to only authorized users.

sections.heading: PHYSICAL SAFEGUARDS
sections.text:
Employees will monitor their workstations and take appropriate measures to protect workstations from potential threats to the computer equipment and/or to the integrity and confidentiality of data contained on the equipment.
Workstations that have access to ePHI must be in secure areas with minimal risk of unauthorized access.
Computer monitors must be positioned in a manner that prevents unauthorized persons from viewing screen contents.
All computers will automatically convert to a blank screen or screen saver after 5 minutes of inactivity.
Workstations and terminals shall be logged off and/or locked up when personnel operating such stations are on an extended break or have left for the day. Employees will log off when they will be away from their computer workstations for more than 15 minutes.
All personnel will maintain their confidential passwords in compliance with the policy and procedure entitled “Passwords”.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Workstation Security

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: In compliance with HIPAA, when applicable, The Ferguson Clinic has established guidelines for workstation use to promote reasonable security and safeguards in the handling of ePHI and PHI. These guidelines specify proper equipment operation procedures, functions to be performed, and the physical attributes of the surroundings of the workstation, focusing on reasonable controls for workstations that can access ePHI and PHI.

sections.heading: WORKSTATION DEFINED
sections.text: A workstation is an electronic computing device, for example, a laptop or desktop computer, or any other device that performs functions and electronic media stored in its immediate environment.

sections.heading: GUIDELINES FOR PROPER WORKSTATION USE
sections.text:
All computer workstations that are plugged into an electrical power outlet shall use a surge protector.
Only computer terminals, laptops and other devices authorized by the Privacy Officer may be connected to The Ferguson Clinic’s information systems/network.
Only software or application programs authorized by the Privacy Officer may be installed or loaded onto The Ferguson Clinic’s information system equipment, including without limitation, individual computer terminals.
Settings, which include hardware and software modification, shall not be reconfigured by any user using the control panel without the Privacy Officer’s express permission. Users shall not disable virus protection software under any circumstances.
Workstations shall not be shared by individuals that have different levels of confidential information clearance.
Email that contains ePHI must be encrypted before transmission and all such email must comply with the policy and procedure entitled “Email, Instant Messaging and Internet Use”.
Subject: Clinical Records and Health Information
Section: Workstation Use

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to obtain a CLIA Waiver prior to performing Urine Pregnancy Quick Tests and Glucose Quick Tests in the medical facility. The Medical Director will be the designated Laboratory Director. The CLIA Waiver will be renewed biennially.

sections.heading: HISTORY
sections.text:
Congress passed the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA) in 1988 establishing quality standards for all laboratory testing to ensure the accuracy, reliability, and timeliness of patient test results regardless of where the test was performed. The final CLIA regulations were published in the Federal Register on February 28, 1992. The requirements are based on the complexity of the test and not the type of laboratory where the testing is performed. On January 24, 2003, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) published final CLIA Quality Systems laboratory regulations that became effective April 24, 2003.
Under CLIA, a laboratory is defined as a facility that performs testing on materials derived from the human body for providing information for the diagnosis, prevention, or treatment of any disease or impairment of, or assessment of the health of, human beings.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The Office Manager and/or designee will download the PDF CLIA Application.
The application will be completed, and the Medical Director will sign it.
The application will be mailed to the applicable office.
Upon receipt of the invoice, the Office Manager will pay the fee.
Upon receipt of the CLIA Waiver, it will be placed in a frame and placed conspicuously in the facility.
The CLIA Waiver must be renewed every biennially.
Section: CLIA Waiver

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to perform Blood Glucose Quick Tests on all patients that are Diabetic prior to any procedure being performed using single-patient glucometers.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
The day of the procedure the patient will be asked to bring their home glucometer, or a single-use glucometer will be provided. The patient will be asked to submit to a blood glucose quick test prior to being medicated and performing the procedure. The patient will complete the Blood Glucose Test Consent Form. The Glucose Test Consent Form will be placed in the patient’s medical record.
A test strip will be removed from the container.
The clinical staff will use the finger stick method to obtain a small drop of blood to be placed on the test strip.
The clinical staff will place the blood-soaked test strip and place it in the Glucometer.
The clinical staff will obtain the value from the Glucometer, and it will be documented in the patient’s medical record.
The Physician will review the value/result and determine if the procedure will be performed.
The results and determination from the Physician will be documented in the patient’s medical record.
If the glucose test results are within normal limits, the procedure process will proceed uninterrupted.
If the glucose test results are outside of normal limits, the Medical Director will assess the patient, and it will be determined if it is safe to proceed with the procedure.
All documentation will be placed in the patient’s medical record.
Section: Glucose Testing

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure the accuracy of all glucose tests performed at their facilities.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Upon opening a new box of glucose quick tests, a control will be performed to ensure that the tests performed will maintain the high level of accuracy for the patient/client.
The box will be opened, and one test strip will be removed.
A control – purchased at a medical supply company will be removed from its box.
The control will be performed based on instructions provided by the manufacturer.
The results will be documented on the Glucose Test Quality Control Log.
If it is determined that the glucose test fails the control, the box will be returned to manufacturer for a refund and/or replacement and will not be used for future glucose tests.
Section: Glucose Test Quality Control

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: To provide guidelines to assure patients will have surgical implants properly documented and tracked, as required by the FDA Safe Medical Device Act.

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: All non-absorbable surgical implants are tracked utilizing a system which allows for timely identification of patients and implants when the need arises.

sections.heading: Items used at The Ferguson Clinic that are tracked include
sections.text:
Breast Implants
Cheek Implants
Chin Implants
Any other Implant permanently inserted in a patient

sections.heading: Implant Log
sections.text:
An implant log shall be maintained to include the following documentation:
Patient Name
Date of Service
Procedure
Type of implant
Manufacturer’s name, product number, serial number, lot number, location, and size.

sections.heading: DEVICE TRACKING
sections.text:
In addition to the above information the devices mandated by the FDA for the Safe Medical Devices Act tracking
also require the following information:
Device Registration Form either scanned and uploaded to EMR or paper placed in the patient’s medical record.
Supplied by the manufacturer with each implant
The RN Circulator completes the information as completely as possible, noting on the Perioperative Record that the Registration Form was sent to the manufacturer.
See: Pharmaceutical Services: Recall of Medical Devices, Drugs, Supplies, etc. per FDA guidelines.

sections.heading: IN-HOUSE STERILIZATION OF WRAPPED IMPLANTABLE ITEMS
sections.text:
Permanent, non-sterile implantable implants will be discarded or returned to manufacture.
References: Department of Health and Human Services, Food and Drug Administration.  Medical Device Tracking
Section: Surgical Services
Subject:  Implants, Documentation and Tracking

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: All lab test results are reviewed promptly by the ordering Physician or Allied Health Professional. Abnormal or overdue results/tests are flagged for attention.

sections.heading: PURPOSE
sections.text: To ensure timely and appropriate review of all laboratory results/tests ordered by the Physician and/or Allied Health Professional.

sections.heading: Lab
sections.text:
The Licensed Health Care Professional will order laboratory tests per the Medical Director’s Standing Orders, by completing the appropriate form (or EHR template) including all required fields to ensure accurate processing of the request.
Licensed Health Care Professionals will document lab tests ordered in the chart note and in the Lab Log.
All discussions with patients shall be documented in the medical record.
The ordering Physician or Allied Health Professional shall review incoming labs/test results daily and cross references the received results with those previously ordered, if applicable.
Licensed Health Care Professionals will log each lab test in the Lab Log. This will be used as a reminder system if electronic to ensure that these tests and subsequent results are not overlooked.
If lab test results have not been received within the expected timeframe, the Licensed Health Care Professional will contact the lab for said results.
Physicians or Allied Health Professional must initial and date the results of all lab tests results directly on the report.
IF USING AN EHR: Physicians or Allied Health Professional must verify that received tests results have been viewed/acted upon through the EHR system.
Any action taken or initiated by a Licensed Health Care Professional in response to the Physician’s or Allied Health Professional’s orders shall be documented (with date and, if
relevant, time) in the medical chart.

sections.heading: Lab Test Reporting to Patients
sections.text:
Licensed Health Care Professionals shall notify patients of all lab test results.
Results reporting can take place through the following processes:
Phone call
Secure email
Certified Letter (if applicable)
Patient online portal/personal health record
Section: Lab Test Results

title: Test Kits, Laboratory Devices and Supporting Supplies

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure all test kits, laboratory devices and supporting supplies are FDA approved for use under the type of CLIA certificate obtained.

sections.heading: RESPONSIBILITY
sections.text:
The Nurse Manager or Office Manager will be responsible to ensure compliance.
Section: Test Kits, Laboratory Devices and Supporting Supplies

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to refer patient is that meet the criteria of the standing order developed by the Medical Director to an outside lab for blood tests to assist in the Physician’s assessment prior to any procedure being performed.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
When it has been determined that a patient meets the criteria for any procedure, the Physician(s) or designee will give the patient an order for lab work required to be completed prior to any procedure being performed.
The Physician(s) or designee will complete the lab order and give it to the patient to take to a lab of their choice. If the patient does not have a lab of choice, the Physician(s) or designee will suggest a few that they may consider.
The labs ordered will be documented on the Lab Test Log to ensure that each ordered lab for each patient is received prior to any procedures being performed. The lab log will act as a checks and balances to ensure compliance.

sections.heading: FOLLOW UP
sections.text:
The Registered Nurse and/or designee will follow up with the patient the next day to ensure that the patient has either made an appointment with the lab or has already gone. The Registered Nurse and/or designee will document the follow up call in the patient’s medical record.
The office will receive the lab report with the results of the lab work ordered.  The Physician(s) will ensure that the test results are within the normal margins to be able to perform the procedure. That information will be communicated to the patient by telephone, text or email depending on the desired method of communication by the patient.
If the lab report has not been received within 3-4 days, the Registered Nurse and/or designee will contact the patient to see where they had the lab tests completed.  The Registered Nurse and/or designee will then call the appropriate lab for the lab report.
If the lab values do not meet the criteria for the procedure, the procedure will be cancelled.
All communication and follow up will be documented in the patient’s medical record.
Section: Outside Laboratory Testing

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to have determine what tissue specimen shall be submitted to pathology if clinically appropriate.

sections.heading: List of Tissue Specimens to be submitted to pathology
sections.text:
Any tissue clinically suspicious for malignancy
Breast Tissues
Skin Lesions
Subcutaneous fat, at the discretion of the surgeon
Excised skin, at the surgeon’s discretion

sections.heading: List of Exempt Tissues Specimens not to be submitted to pathology
sections.text: Excised skin with no suspicious lesions at the discretion of the surgeon

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Specimens are placed in a sterile container containing Formalin, Normal Saline, or as specified by the Pathologist.
Specimen is prepared for transport following the contracted labs requirements.
Specimen is delivered to the contracted lab via currier to Diagnostic Laboratory Services, Inc., or another accredited pathologic laboratory.
Section: Tissue Exemption

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to perform Urine Pregnancy Tests per manufacturer’s guidelines and per the standing orders established by the Medical Director at The Ferguson Clinic regarding patients undergoing any Procedure(s) on patients that are reproductive.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
This test is to be done on all women who are within childbearing ages.  The only exception to this rule is if the woman, by history, has had a hysterectomy. If a patient is refusing to comply with this rule, a “Pregnancy Waiver” must be signed. The refusal must be witnessed and signed by an employee.
Gather the equipment needed for the testing.
Explain the test to the patient.
Have the patient sign and date the Pregnancy Test Consent Form
Have the patient wash and dry their hands.
Have the patient urinate in clean, dry container. (This container must be marked with the patient identification.)
Urine samples exhibiting visible precipitates should be allowed to settle to obtain a clear sample for testing.
Bring the urine to room temperature before testing.
Don gloves
Place the test device on a level surface.
Hold the dropper vertically and transfer three (3) full drops of urine to the sample “well”.
Begin the time for three (3) minutes or as the manufacturer instructs. Avoid bubbles in the well.
Wait for the red line(s) to appear. Read the result at three (3) minutes. Be sure that the background clears before trying to read the results.
Do not read the results after three (3) minutes. It is very important that this test is accurately timed.
Dispose of the specimen and the test strip properly.
Remove gloves
Wash and dry hands completely.
This protocol will change depending on the brand of test strips obtained. The basic steps will remain the same.
Section: Urine Pregnancy Tests

sections.heading: POLICY
sections.text: It is the policy of The Ferguson Clinic to ensure the accuracy of all urine pregnancy tests performed at their facilities.

sections.heading: PROCEDURE
sections.text:
Upon opening a new box of urine pregnancy quick tests, a control will be performed to ensure that the tests performed will maintain the high level of accuracy for the patient/client.
The box will be opened, and one pregnancy test will be removed.
A control – purchased at a medical supply company will be removed from its box.
The control will be performed based on instructions provided by the manufacturer.
The results will be documented on the Urine Pregnancy Quality Control Log.
If it is determined that the urine pregnancy test fails the control, the box will be returned to manufacturer for a refund and/or replacement and will not be used for future pregnancy tests.
Section: Urine Pregnancy Tests Quality Control
